previous_plan.title: Flash VOD Migration Mobile App - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native replacement for legacy Flash VOD platform with Cognito auth, PostgreSQL entitlements, and CloudFront-delivered DRM video segments on AWS ECS Fargate.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-tier mobile architecture: React Native client communicating over TLS to Fastify API on ECS Fargate, PostgreSQL for subscriber and entitlement data, CloudFront for video delivery with signed URLs. DRM proxy referenced but not detailed.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito for subscriber auth, Widevine and FairPlay DRM mentioned, GDPR and CCPA noted. Device limit enforcement and license proxy design absent.
previous_plan.data_model: subscribers, content_licenses, device_registrations, purchase_records, watch_history - basic schema without pseudonymization or regional routing specification.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve ISS-001 (DRM proxy) and ISS-002 (device registration atomicity). For ISS-001: the license proxy sits within the Fastify drm-proxy module, validating stream tokens then forwarding Widevine challenges to AWS Elemental Key Provider and generating FairPlay CKC responses via KMS-backed content keys, caching results in Redis for 8 hours per device:title. For ISS-002: PostgreSQL advisory locks on subscriber_id during device count check+insert atomically enforce the 5-device ceiling without distributed lock complexity. ISS-003 GDPR/financial conflict resolved via pseudonymization pattern on purchase_records at deletion time.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit DRM license proxy design to system_components and workflows: Fastify drm-proxy module validates stream token in Redis, calls AWS Elemental Key Provider for Widevine challenge response and KMS for FairPlay CKC generation, caches license in Redis for 8 hours per device:title
fix_report.expected_outcome: Content licensor audit will find documented DRM proxy with KMS integration, license TTL, and entitlement gating satisfying Widevine and FairPlay certification requirements

fix_report.action_taken: Added PostgreSQL advisory lock pattern to device registration endpoint: pg_try_advisory_lock on subscriber_id, SELECT COUNT FOR UPDATE from device_registrations, reject if >=5 before INSERT, release lock
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent registration attempts cannot exceed 5 devices; atomicity guaranteed without application-level distributed lock overhead

target_output.title: Flash VOD Legacy Modernization - React Native Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A GDPR/CCPA-compliant React Native VOD mobile app built on Node.js/Fastify with ECS Fargate replacing a Flash-era cable operator platform. Handles subscriber entitlement verification, Widevine L1/L3 and FairPlay DRM license proxying, in-app purchases synced across iOS and Android, and CloudFront-signed HLS/DASH segment delivery. Dual-region deployment (us-east-1, eu-west-1) enforces GDPR data residency with pseudonymization resolving the purchase record financial-retention conflict.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native 0.74 app (Expo bare) connects over TLS 1.3 to a Fastify API cluster on ECS Fargate behind a regional ALB. Cognito handles subscriber authentication issuing JWTs; device-bound stream tokens are generated per-play and cached in Redis with 60-second TTL. A DRM proxy module within the Fastify service validates stream token entitlement then forwards Widevine license challenges to AWS Elemental Key Provider and generates FairPlay CKC responses via KMS-backed content keys. HLS/DASH segments are stored in S3 and delivered via CloudFront signed URLs expiring in 60 seconds. Two independent stacks in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with CloudFront geo-routing enforce GDPR data residency by directing EU subscriber API calls and video delivery to the eu-west-1 origin.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Expo bare workflow, react-native-video 6 with Widevine/FairPlay native modules, Zustand, React Query, FastImage. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4, Bull (Redis-backed), Passport.js for Cognito JWT validation. Data: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS Multi-AZ), Redis 7 (ElastiCache). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudFront with signed URLs, S3 for HLS/DASH segments, AWS Elemental MediaConvert, KMS for FairPlay CKC. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Expo EAS Build, ECR.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Content Catalog: paginated browse of channel bundles, VOD titles, and live channels with entitlement-aware CTA (stream/purchase/rent). DRM Playback Engine: pre-rolls Widevine L1/L3 or FairPlay license 3 seconds before user taps play, handles license renewal mid-stream. Purchase and Rental Flow: in-app purchase via Apple StoreKit 2 and Google Play Billing with backend receipt validation and entitlement sync. Device Manager: subscriber self-service to view and remove registered devices (max 5). Watch History Sync: position_seconds written to API on pause/exit and restored on next play on any registered device. Content Expiry Notifications: push via SNS/APNs/FCM when licensed titles expire within 7 days.
target_output.system_components: React Native client (iOS/Android) with native Widevine and FairPlay DRM modules. ECS Fargate API cluster running Fastify with modules: catalog, entitlements, stream-tokens, drm-proxy, purchases, notifications. DRM Proxy module: receives player license challenge, verifies active stream token in Redis, calls AWS Elemental Key Provider for Widevine signed license response, calls KMS-backed FairPlay CKC generator for iOS. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ per region. ElastiCache Redis 7 per region for stream tokens and Bull job queues. CloudFront distribution with two origin groups. S3 buckets holding HLS/DASH packaged segments. Bull background workers: content_expiry_job (nightly) and entitlement_sync_job (post-purchase).
target_output.workflows: Device Registration with Atomicity: Mobile app POSTs to /auth/device-register with Cognito JWT. Fastify acquires PostgreSQL advisory lock on subscriber_id via pg_try_advisory_lock, executes SELECT COUNT(*) FROM device_registrations WHERE subscriber_id = ? FOR UPDATE, returns HTTP 409 if count >= 5, otherwise INSERTs device record and returns device_token JWT, releases lock. DRM License Acquisition: Player calls POST /stream-tokens with device_token and title_id; API validates entitlement in PostgreSQL, writes stream_token (60s TTL) to Redis keyed by subscriber_id:device_id:title_id. Player calls POST /drm/widevine-license or /drm/fairplay-license passing stream_token and license challenge bytes. DRM proxy validates token in Redis, forwards Widevine challenge to AWS Elemental Key Provider, caches signed license in Redis for 8 hours per device:title, returns license bytes to player. In-App Purchase Sync: User completes StoreKit 2 checkout; mobile app POSTs receipt to POST /purchases with idempotency key; backend validates with Apple/Google APIs, creates purchase_record and entitlement row in content_licenses; Bull job syncs entitlement to all subscriber devices via SNS push.
target_output.data_model: subscribers (id UUID PK, cognito_sub, email_hash VARCHAR(64), region ENUM us/eu, gdpr_consent_at TIMESTAMP, ccpa_opt_out BOOLEAN, deleted_at TIMESTAMP). content_licenses (id UUID PK, title_id UUID FK, license_type ENUM svod/tvod/live, drm_key_id VARCHAR(128), expiry_date TIMESTAMP, active BOOLEAN). device_registrations (id UUID PK, subscriber_id UUID FK, device_fingerprint VARCHAR(128), platform ENUM ios/android, registered_at TIMESTAMP, last_seen_at TIMESTAMP). purchase_records (id UUID PK, subscriber_id UUID FK nullable, deleted_subscriber_uuid UUID nullable, title_id UUID, purchase_type ENUM buy/rent, store ENUM apple/google/direct, amount_cents INTEGER, purchased_at TIMESTAMP). stream_sessions (id UUID PK, subscriber_id UUID FK, device_id UUID FK, title_id UUID, started_at TIMESTAMP, ended_at TIMESTAMP, bytes_delivered BIGINT, avg_bitrate_kbps INTEGER). watch_history (id UUID PK, subscriber_id UUID FK, title_id UUID, position_seconds INTEGER, updated_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: POST /auth/device-register (Cognito JWT, returns device_token; enforces 5-device limit with pg advisory lock). GET /catalog/titles?bundle_id=&page= (paginated content catalog with entitlement status). GET /entitlements/:subscriber_id (list of active content_licenses). POST /stream-tokens (device_token + title_id, returns 60s Redis stream_token). POST /drm/widevine-license (stream_token + challenge bytes, returns signed Widevine license). POST /drm/fairplay-license (stream_token + challenge bytes, returns FairPlay CKC). POST /purchases (receipt + idempotency_key, creates entitlement). DELETE /auth/devices/:device_id. PATCH /watch-history/:title_id (upsert position_seconds). Rate limit: 100 req/min per device_token, 10 req/min for stream-token and drm endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Authentication: Cognito SRP + JWT with 15-min access token expiry; device-bound device_token prevents cross-device token replay. DRM: Widevine L1 (hardware-backed key storage) on certified Android devices, L3 fallback with content licensor approval; FairPlay via KMS-backed CKC service on iOS. Stream tokens include subscriber_id:device_id:title_id hash stored in Redis with 60s eviction. Device Registration Atomicity: pg_try_advisory_lock on subscriber_id enforces 5-device ceiling atomically. GDPR Article 17: subscriber PII nulled on deletion; purchase_records pseudonymized via deleted_subscriber_uuid for financial audit trail. CCPA: opt-out flag prevents watch history from feeding analytics. CloudFront WAF with rate-limiting and Widevine challenge replay detection.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Dual-region ECS Fargate: us-east-1 (primary) and eu-west-1 (EU data residency). Each region: Fargate cluster with API service (2-8 tasks, CPU 60% autoscale) and Bull worker service (1-3 tasks). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ per region; ElastiCache Redis cluster per region. CloudFront with geo-routing origin groups: X-Subscriber-Region header routes EU traffic to eu-west-1 ALB. GitHub Actions CI: lint + jest unit tests, Docker build + ECR push, ECS rolling deploy (50% min healthy). Mobile CI: Expo EAS Build producing signed iOS .ipa (TestFlight) and Android .aab (internal track) on each main merge. Environments: dev, staging (anonymized data), production (staged rollout: 5/25/100% over 3 days).
target_output.observability: Datadog RUM in React Native for crash reporting and ANR tracking. Custom playback telemetry events (playback_started, buffer_ratio, bitrate_switch, playback_error) emitted from react-native-video callbacks to Datadog Metrics. Server-side: Datadog APM on Fastify handlers; custom metrics: stream_token_issuance_rate, drm_license_latency_p99, device_registration_rate, purchase_validation_latency. Alerts: drm_license_latency_p99 > 800ms (PagerDuty critical), buffer_ratio > 3% (Slack warning), ECS task error rate > 2% (PagerDuty critical).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $3,200/month at 50K active subscribers: dual-region ECS Fargate ($800), dual-region RDS Multi-AZ ($1,100), ElastiCache Redis ($400), CloudFront egress for 500TB/month video ($700), Elemental Key Provider license requests ($200). Fargate autoscales on CPU 60% threshold; CloudFront handles CDN burst without infrastructure changes. S3 Intelligent-Tiering for older VOD segments.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Cognito auth with device registration (pg advisory lock), content catalog browse, VOD playback with Widevine L1/L3 and FairPlay, in-app purchase with Apple/Google receipt validation, CloudFront signed URL delivery, watch history sync, dual-region deployment, GDPR/CCPA controls. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Live sports multi-view, offline download with DRM-bound local license, content expiry push notifications, advanced channel bundle management. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Family profile management with per-profile watch history, third-party channel add-on marketplace.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for React Native and Fastify. Jest for unit and integration tests (80% coverage gate on entitlement, drm-proxy, and purchase modules). Detox E2E tests covering device registration, DRM playback start, and purchase flow on iOS and Android simulators. PR requires 1 senior approval; DRM module changes require security team sign-off. Stream token and DRM proxy logic isolated in separate Fastify modules for future microservice extraction.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AWS Elemental Key Provider regional availability; Mitigation: 8-hour license cache in Redis per device:title reduces real-time KMS dependency. Risk: Apple/Google receipt validation API downtime blocking purchases; Mitigation: Bull retry queue with idempotency keys. Tradeoff: monolithic Fastify vs. separate DRM proxy microservice - chosen monolith for MVP with clear module boundaries. Risk: Widevine L1 attestation failing on mid-range Android devices; Mitigation: L3 fallback path pre-approved by content licensor for non-premium titles. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL advisory locks vs. Redis SETNX for device registration - chosen PostgreSQL as authoritative device count source.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Widevine L3 fallback approved by content licensor for non-premium tier titles. CloudFront signed URLs expire at 60 seconds matching stream_token TTL. FairPlay CKC generated via KMS-backed service avoiding GPL obligations. GDPR deletion uses pseudonymization pattern preserving purchase_records for 7-year financial audit while satisfying PII erasure.

metadata.notes: Flash-era VOD modernization with DRM proxy architecture and device registration atomicity resolved in round 2.

project_goal.value: Research prototype exploring adaptive bitrate algorithm improvements for low-latency live sports streaming, targeting sub-2-second glass-to-glass latency on congested mobile networks
project_goal.rationale: Competitive sports streaming research objective to reduce viewer-perceived latency below 2s on LTE/5G networks

target_users.value: Streaming research team (6-8 engineers) running comparative ABR algorithm experiments internally
target_users.rationale: Internal research tooling; no external users

project_class.rationale: Exploratory prototype to validate ABR algorithm hypotheses, not a production streaming service

capabilities.rationale: QUIC/HTTP3 server for segment delivery, ClickHouse for telemetry, Grafana for realtime visualization, no browser UI needed

complexity_level.rationale: Custom HTTP/3 QUIC stack, concurrent synthetic viewer emulation, and ClickHouse time-series analytics require advanced engineering

risk_level.rationale: Internal research system, no PII, no external exposure, no production traffic

data_sensitivity.rationale: All data is synthetic telemetry from test harness; no user data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs in GKE cluster accessible only to research team VPN

access_model.value: Custom JWT for research team API access; Grafana authenticated via Google Workspace SSO
access_model.rationale: Lightweight auth sufficient for internal research tool

feature_scope.value: Synthetic viewer harness emulating network profiles, ABR algorithm plugin interface, segment server with QUIC, ClickHouse telemetry ingestion, Grafana dashboards for latency/rebuffer comparison
feature_scope.rationale: Full research platform to compare ABR algorithm variants side-by-side

mvp_scope.value: Single-stream QUIC server with pluggable ABR module, synthetic harness with 3 network profiles (LTE-good, LTE-congested, 5G), ClickHouse ingest for latency_samples and rebuffer_events, Grafana dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed to run first comparative experiment between BOLA and throughput-based algorithms

security_baseline.value: JWT auth on control API; GKE cluster internal network policies; no sensitive data stored
security_baseline.rationale: Low-risk internal research tool; basic auth controls sufficient

frontend_stack.value: None - headless research platform; Grafana serves as visualization frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom UI needed; Grafana provides sufficient dashboard capabilities for research team

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with custom HTTP/3 QUIC stack using quic-go library
backend_stack.rationale: Go concurrency model ideal for emulating thousands of synthetic viewer goroutines; QUIC required for sub-2s latency research

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for time-series telemetry (latency_samples, rebuffer_events, abr_decisions); Redis 7 for ABR algorithm state per virtual viewer
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage enables sub-second queries over millions of per-segment telemetry rows

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot cluster on GCP, internal VPC only
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot for zero node management; research team already on GCP

privacy_retention_policy.value: Synthetic telemetry retained for 90 days per experiment; no PII involved
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No data privacy obligations; retention for reproducibility of experiment results

future_scope.value: Multi-CDN edge emulation, CMAF chunk-level latency probing, live player SDK integration for real-device testing
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 research directions after initial algorithm validation

constraints.value: Must emulate at least 10,000 concurrent synthetic viewers per GKE node; experiment runs must be reproducible with fixed random seeds; no proprietary video content (use Big Buck Bunny test stream)
constraints.rationale: Scale requirement for statistically significant ABR comparisons; reproducibility is a research integrity requirement

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for infrastructure metrics; custom ClickHouse dashboards for ABR telemetry; experiment run metadata stored in ClickHouse experiment_runs table
observability_baseline.rationale: Research team needs both infrastructure health and fine-grained per-algorithm telemetry in one dashboard view

execution_preference.value: Experiment-driven iterations; each ABR variant deployed as a Go plugin; experiments triggered via REST API with config JSON specifying network profiles and algorithm variant
execution_preference.rationale: Researchers need to launch and compare experiments programmatically without code changes

project.goal: Validate sub-2s glass-to-glass latency ABR improvements for live sports streaming on congested mobile networks
project.target_users: Streaming research team (6-8 engineers)
project.timeline: Prototype ready for first experiment in 8 weeks

backend.framework: Custom HTTP/3 QUIC server using quic-go
backend.abr_interface: Go plugin interface for swappable ABR algorithm modules

security.auth: Custom JWT for REST control API
security.network: GKE network policies, internal VPC only

data.db: ClickHouse for telemetry time-series

constraints.reproducibility: Fixed random seeds for network profile simulation
constraints.content: Big Buck Bunny test stream, no proprietary content

open_questions.real_device_testing: Live player SDK integration for real devices deferred to Phase 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype scope is well-defined for the first comparative experiment between BOLA and throughput-based ABR. The reproducibility requirement with fixed random seeds is critical for publishing research results and must be enforced at the harness level, not just documented.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go goroutines mapping to synthetic viewers is architecturally sound for 10K concurrency. The ABR plugin interface must be defined as a Go interface rather than plugin.so to avoid CGO complexity; each algorithm variant compiled as a separate binary or selected via factory pattern. ClickHouse MergeTree with ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq) will enable efficient per-experiment replay queries. The QUIC server should track per-connection RTT estimates and expose them to the ABR algorithm via a NetworkStateProvider interface.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal-only deployment with GKE network policies is appropriate for this risk level. JWT signing key stored in GCP Secret Manager even for a research tool establishes good practices.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10,000 concurrent viewer goroutines per GKE Autopilot node requires careful memory budgeting: each viewer goroutine needs ABR state (~2KB), QUIC connection state (~4KB), and Redis handle. Total: ~60MB per 10K viewers - well within GKE Autopilot node memory. Fixed random seed reproducibility must be validated by a regression test that reruns the same experiment config and asserts identical per-segment ABR decisions.

backend.service_design: Go services: segment-server (QUIC HTTP/3, serves HLS/DASH segments), harness-controller (REST API for experiment config and launch), abr-engine (stateless algorithm evaluation per viewer tick), telemetry-sink (batch ClickHouse inserts). ABR interface: type AbrAlgorithm interface with methods NextBitrate(state NetworkState, buffer BufferState) int and Reset().
backend.api_patterns: REST control API: POST /experiments (config JSON: algorithm, network_profiles, viewer_count, seed), GET /experiments/:id/status, GET /experiments/:id/summary (aggregated metrics from ClickHouse), DELETE /experiments/:id (stop and cleanup). All endpoints JWT-authenticated.
backend.failure_handling: Viewer goroutine panics recovered with defer/recover and logged to ClickHouse rebuffer_events with error type. ClickHouse batch inserts use retry with exponential backoff. If ClickHouse is unavailable, telemetry buffered in Redis sorted set with 5-minute TTL to avoid data loss for short outages.
backend.scaling_notes: Each GKE Autopilot pod runs up to 10K viewer goroutines via a configurable VIEWER_COUNT env var. Experiment controller scales pods for larger experiments (e.g., 100K viewers = 10 pods).

security.auth_design: JWT HS256 signed with key from GCP Secret Manager. Tokens issued by harness-controller /auth/token endpoint with team email claim. 24-hour expiry for research session convenience.
security.secrets_management: JWT signing key in GCP Secret Manager; accessed by harness-controller on startup via Workload Identity. No long-lived credentials on disk.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Experiment launch and stop events logged to ClickHouse experiment_runs table with researcher email, config hash, and timestamp for research audit trail.

data.entities: stream_segments (segment_seq, bitrate_kbps, duration_ms, size_bytes, cdn_edge_node), bitrate_ladder_configs (id, experiment_id, rungs JSON array of kbps values), network_probes (experiment_id, viewer_id, probe_seq, estimated_bandwidth_kbps, rtt_ms, loss_pct, timestamp), abr_decisions (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq, algorithm, selected_bitrate_kbps, buffer_level_ms, decision_latency_us, timestamp), rebuffer_events (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq, stall_duration_ms, cause ENUM underflow/startup, timestamp), latency_samples (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq, glass_to_glass_ms, segment_fetch_ms, decode_ms, timestamp), cdn_edge_nodes (id, region, simulated_latency_ms)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree tables, ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq) for per-experiment query locality. Redis 7 sorted sets for per-viewer live ABR state during experiment runs.
data.analytics_events: Grafana dashboards query ClickHouse directly: p50/p95/p99 latency per algorithm per network profile, rebuffer rate per minute, ABR decision distribution, avg bitrate over time. Comparison view shows control vs. experimental algorithm side-by-side.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster (us-central1): segment-server Deployment, harness-controller Deployment, telemetry-sink Deployment, ClickHouse StatefulSet (single node for research scale), Redis Deployment. All internal ClusterIP services; Grafana exposed via internal LoadBalancer to research VPN.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./..., golangci-lint, Docker build + push to GCR, kubectl apply via Workload Identity. Experiment regression test: launch reference experiment with seed=42, assert abr_decisions match golden file.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Go /metrics endpoints (goroutine count, QUIC connection count, ClickHouse insert latency). Grafana dashboards: infrastructure health + ABR research telemetry from ClickHouse via Grafana ClickHouse plugin.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a research prototype, not a production system. Key decisions: Go goroutines for synthetic viewer concurrency (10K per pod), ABR algorithm abstracted as a Go interface not a plugin.so to avoid CGO, ClickHouse MergeTree ordered by experiment_id+viewer_id+segment_seq for efficient comparative queries, QUIC via quic-go to match the research target (sub-2s latency requires HTTP/3 chunk delivery). No frontend agent runs since project_class is research_prototype with no browser UI.
target_output.title: Low-Latency ABR Research Platform - GKE Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 research prototype running on GKE Autopilot that emulates up to 100,000 concurrent synthetic viewers across configurable network profiles (LTE-congested, LTE-good, 5G) to compare adaptive bitrate algorithm variants in a controlled setting. A custom HTTP/3 QUIC server delivers HLS segments at configurable latency targets while pluggable ABR algorithm modules make per-segment bitrate decisions. All telemetry (latency_samples, rebuffer_events, abr_decisions, network_probes) is ingested into ClickHouse and visualized via Grafana dashboards enabling side-by-side algorithm comparison.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two core services run in GKE: the segment-server (Go QUIC HTTP/3 using quic-go, serving pre-packaged Big Buck Bunny HLS segments at simulated CDN edge latencies) and the harness-controller (Go REST API managing experiment lifecycle). The harness-controller spawns N viewer goroutines per experiment pod, each running a simulated viewer loop: probe network state, call ABR interface NextBitrate(), request next segment via QUIC, record glass-to-glass latency. Telemetry events are batched by the telemetry-sink service and written to ClickHouse MergeTree tables. Redis 7 holds per-viewer live ABR algorithm state during active experiment runs. Grafana connects directly to ClickHouse via the Grafana ClickHouse plugin for real-time and post-hoc comparative analysis.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, quic-go 0.42 for HTTP/3 QUIC, chi router for REST API, prometheus/client_golang for metrics. Telemetry: ClickHouse 23.8 (MergeTree), ClickHouse Go driver for batch inserts. Cache: Redis 7 for live ABR state. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot (us-central1), GCR for images, GCP Secret Manager via Workload Identity. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana 10 with ClickHouse plugin. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Experiment Launcher: POST /experiments with algorithm name, viewer_count, network_profile_ids, random_seed, bitrate_ladder_config. Synthetic Viewer Harness: per-experiment goroutine pool emulating configurable network impairments (packet loss %, bandwidth cap, RTT jitter) per network_profile. ABR Engine: pluggable Go interface (NextBitrate, Reset) with implementations: ThroughputBased, BOLA, BufferBased, and custom experimental variants. QUIC Segment Server: serves 2-second HLS chunks with configurable edge latency simulation per cdn_edge_node profile. Telemetry Dashboard: Grafana showing p50/p95/p99 glass-to-glass latency, rebuffer rate per minute, ABR decision bitrate distribution, bandwidth utilization - all filterable by experiment_id and algorithm.
target_output.system_components: segment-server (Go, quic-go HTTP/3): serves HLS 2s chunks from in-memory segment store; per-connection RTT exposed via NetworkStateProvider to ABR engine. harness-controller (Go, chi REST): experiment CRUD, viewer goroutine pool manager, JWT validation. abr-engine (Go package): AbrAlgorithm interface with factory selecting implementation by name; state encapsulated per viewer. telemetry-sink (Go): consumes events from in-process channel, batches into ClickHouse INSERT every 500ms or 10K rows. ClickHouse StatefulSet: MergeTree tables for abr_decisions, latency_samples, rebuffer_events, network_probes, experiment_runs. Redis 7: per-viewer buffer state hash (viewer:{id}:abr_state). Prometheus + Grafana: infrastructure and ABR research dashboards.
target_output.workflows: Experiment Execution: Researcher POSTs /experiments with config JSON (algorithm='BOLA', viewer_count=10000, network_profile='lte_congested', seed=42, ladder=[300,750,1500,3000,6000]). harness-controller creates experiment_run record in ClickHouse, initializes Redis ABR state per viewer, starts goroutine pool. Each viewer goroutine: set network impairment per profile, request GET /segment/{seq} over QUIC, measure fetch latency, call ABR.NextBitrate(buffer, bandwidth), record abr_decision + latency_sample to telemetry channel. Telemetry-sink batches and inserts every 500ms. Experiment completes when all segments in test stream delivered; summary metrics written to experiment_runs. Algorithm Comparison: Researcher launches two experiments with identical config except algorithm='ThroughputBased' vs algorithm='BOLA'. Grafana comparison dashboard renders p99 glass-to-glass latency and rebuffer_rate_per_minute side-by-side for the two experiment_ids, with statistical significance overlay computed via ClickHouse query.
target_output.data_model: stream_segments (segment_seq UInt32, bitrate_kbps UInt32, duration_ms UInt32, size_bytes UInt64, cdn_edge_node String) ENGINE=Memory. bitrate_ladder_configs (id UUID, experiment_id UUID, rungs Array(UInt32)) ENGINE=MergeTree. network_probes (experiment_id UUID, viewer_id UInt32, probe_seq UInt32, estimated_bandwidth_kbps UInt32, rtt_ms UInt32, loss_pct Float32, timestamp DateTime64(3)) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, probe_seq). abr_decisions (experiment_id UUID, viewer_id UInt32, segment_seq UInt32, algorithm String, selected_bitrate_kbps UInt32, buffer_level_ms UInt32, decision_latency_us UInt32, timestamp DateTime64(3)) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq). rebuffer_events (experiment_id UUID, viewer_id UInt32, segment_seq UInt32, stall_duration_ms UInt32, cause Enum('underflow','startup'), timestamp DateTime64(3)) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq). latency_samples (experiment_id UUID, viewer_id UInt32, segment_seq UInt32, glass_to_glass_ms UInt32, segment_fetch_ms UInt32, timestamp DateTime64(3)) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (experiment_id, viewer_id, segment_seq). experiment_runs (id UUID, algorithm String, viewer_count UInt32, network_profile String, seed UInt64, status Enum('running','complete','failed'), started_at DateTime, completed_at Nullable(DateTime), researcher_email String) ENGINE=MergeTree.
target_output.api_design: POST /experiments (JWT required, body: {algorithm, viewer_count, network_profile, seed, bitrate_ladder}, returns experiment_id). GET /experiments/:id/status (running/complete/failed, elapsed_seconds, viewers_active). GET /experiments/:id/summary (p50/p95/p99 latency_ms, rebuffer_rate_pct, avg_bitrate_kbps, total_rebuffers - all aggregated from ClickHouse). DELETE /experiments/:id (stop active viewers, mark failed). POST /auth/token (team email + shared secret, returns 24h JWT). GET /metrics (Prometheus scrape endpoint).
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT HS256 tokens signed with key from GCP Secret Manager via Workload Identity; 24-hour expiry for research convenience. GKE network policy restricts all pod-to-pod traffic to allow-listed service ports; no public LoadBalancer. Grafana exposed only via internal LoadBalancer reachable from research VPN. No PII stored; all experiment data is synthetic telemetry. Experiment launch events logged to ClickHouse experiment_runs with researcher_email and config_hash for research audit trail. ClickHouse and Redis not exposed outside GKE cluster network.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot (us-central1): segment-server Deployment (2-4 replicas based on active experiments), harness-controller Deployment (1 replica), telemetry-sink Deployment (1 replica), ClickHouse StatefulSet (1 node, 500GB PVC), Redis Deployment (1 replica). All internal ClusterIP except Grafana (internal LoadBalancer). GitHub Actions CI: go test ./..., golangci-lint, Docker build + push to GCR, kubectl apply with Workload Identity. Experiment regression test in CI: launch with seed=42, compare abr_decisions output against golden JSONL file. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo. Environment: single research cluster (no staging/prod distinction for a prototype).
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Go /metrics endpoints on all services: goroutine count, QUIC active connections, ClickHouse insert batch latency, Redis command latency. Grafana infrastructure dashboard: pod CPU/memory, ClickHouse insert rate, Redis hit rate. ABR research dashboard (ClickHouse queries): p50/p95/p99 glass_to_glass_ms per experiment, rebuffer_events per minute per algorithm, abr_decisions bitrate histogram, network_probe bandwidth distribution by profile. Dashboard parameter variables: experiment_id (multi-select for comparison), algorithm, network_profile.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot: approximately $150/month for always-on services plus on-demand pod scaling for large experiments. ClickHouse PVC 500GB: $50/month. Total ~$200/month at research scale. For 100K viewer experiments: harness-controller scales to 10 pods (10K viewers each) automatically via HPA on goroutine count metric. ClickHouse single node handles 10K inserts/second sufficient for 100K concurrent viewers at 1 telemetry event per 2-second segment.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): QUIC segment server with Big Buck Bunny HLS test stream, harness-controller with goroutine pool (up to 10K viewers), ABR interface with BOLA and ThroughputBased implementations, ClickHouse telemetry ingestion, Redis ABR state, Grafana comparison dashboards, JWT auth, GKE Autopilot deployment, CI with regression golden file test. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Multi-CDN edge node emulation with configurable latency profiles per cdn_edge_node, CMAF chunk-level latency probing (100ms chunks), additional ABR variants (MPC, Pensieve-inspired), experiment result export to GCS for paper reproduction. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Live player SDK integration for real Android/iOS device testing against the QUIC segment server, replacing synthetic goroutines for real-device experiment runs.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go standard library preferred over third-party packages except quic-go and ClickHouse driver. All ABR algorithm implementations must implement the AbrAlgorithm interface and pass the shared algorithm unit test suite with configurable seed. ClickHouse queries in separate query files, not inline SQL strings. Experiment reproducibility validated by CI regression test. Prometheus metric naming follows streaming_abr_ prefix convention. PRs require 1 peer review; research engineers can self-merge single-file changes on experiment day.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse single node becoming a write bottleneck at 100K viewers with 2-second segments (25K inserts/second peak). Mitigation: telemetry-sink batches 10K rows per INSERT to reduce query overhead; scale to ClickHouse cluster in Phase 2 if needed. Risk: quic-go connection state overhead at 10K concurrent QUIC connections per pod. Mitigation: benchmark goroutine + connection memory at 1K, 5K, 10K before Phase 1 launch; adjust VIEWER_COUNT cap based on measured overhead. Tradeoff: Go interface for ABR algorithms vs. plugin.so - chosen interface factory to avoid CGO build complexity and plugin symbol naming pitfalls.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ABR algorithm implemented as Go interface factory pattern (not plugin.so). Big Buck Bunny 2-second HLS segments at 300/750/1500/3000/6000 kbps ladder confirmed for test stream. Fixed random seeds enforced via harness-controller seeded PRNG per viewer goroutine. Grafana ClickHouse plugin chosen over custom query service for research dashboard flexibility.

metadata.notes: ABR research prototype with Go QUIC server, 10K synthetic viewers, ClickHouse telemetry, and Grafana comparison dashboards. Moderate quality: observability and scaling sections slightly thinner than ideal.

profile.domain: Open-Source ML Library Documentation (Video Scene Detection)

project_goal.value: Static documentation and showcase site for an open-source ML-powered video scene detection library, featuring interactive demos, API reference, and benchmark visualizations
project_goal.rationale: Library maintainers need a public-facing site to drive adoption, showcase benchmark results, and host interactive demos

target_users.value: ML engineers, video processing developers, and researchers evaluating the scene detection library for integration into their pipelines
target_users.rationale: Primary audience is technical developers browsing docs before integrating the library

project_class.rationale: Purely static output with no server-side runtime; docs and benchmarks are pre-built at deploy time

capabilities.rationale: Static MDX content delivery and Vercel Analytics for page view tracking

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with MDX content, pre-built benchmark charts, and a WebAssembly demo; no backend logic

risk_level.rationale: Public docs site with no user accounts, no PII, no payment flows

data_sensitivity.rationale: No user data collected beyond anonymous page view analytics

external_exposure.rationale: Publicly accessible documentation site for an open-source library

access_model.value: No authentication; fully public read-only site
access_model.rationale: Open-source library documentation is public by design

feature_scope.value: MDX documentation pages, API reference auto-generated from docstrings, benchmark comparison charts from YAML data files, interactive WebAssembly demo for scene detection on uploaded clips, changelog with version entries, search via Pagefind
feature_scope.rationale: Full documentation site with interactive capabilities to differentiate from plain README

mvp_scope.value: MDX docs pages, API reference, benchmark charts from YAML, changelog; WebAssembly demo deferred to post-MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Core docs and benchmarks unblock early adopters; WASM demo is additive

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel; CSP headers configured to allow WebAssembly eval; no cookies beyond Vercel Analytics
security_baseline.rationale: Standard public static site security; CSP must permit wasm-unsafe-eval for WASM demo

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with MDX and TypeScript; React islands for interactive components; Pagefind for static search
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro's island architecture ships zero JS for static pages while enabling React for benchmark charts and WASM demo

backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime needed; all content pre-built at deploy time

data_platform.value: None - benchmark data sourced from YAML files in repository; no database
data_platform.rationale: Benchmark results committed to repo as YAML and rendered to static charts at build time

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with automatic CDN
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel provides zero-config Astro deployment with global edge CDN

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected; Vercel Analytics uses anonymous edge-computed metrics without cookies
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Privacy-friendly analytics sufficient for open-source site; no GDPR consent banner needed

future_scope.value: Versioned docs for multiple library releases, community gallery of scene detection use cases, integration guides for FFmpeg and OpenCV pipelines
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap to support growing library ecosystem

constraints.value: Build must complete in under 2 minutes on Vercel free tier; no JavaScript runtime at request time; WebAssembly binary must be served from same origin to satisfy COOP/COEP headers
constraints.rationale: Vercel free tier build limits and WASM SharedArrayBuffer isolation requirements

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views and Web Vitals; no custom telemetry
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard Vercel Analytics sufficient for tracking doc page popularity

execution_preference.value: PR-driven content updates; GitHub Actions rebuilds and deploys on merge to main; preview deployments for every PR
execution_preference.rationale: Open-source contribution workflow with preview URLs for content reviewers

project.goal: Public documentation and showcase site for open-source ML video scene detection library
project.target_users: ML engineers and video processing developers
project.timeline: Launch with library v1.0 release in 6 weeks

frontend.components: React islands for BenchmarkChart and WasmDemo components
frontend.search: Pagefind static search index built at Astro build time

data.benchmark_data: YAML files in /data/benchmarks/ directory rendered to charts via Recharts React component

constraints.no_server_runtime: No serverless functions or API routes
constraints.wasm_coop: COOP/COEP headers required for WASM SharedArrayBuffer

open_questions.versioned_docs: Starlight versioning plugin feasibility to be evaluated post-MVP

reasoner_reviews.product: The documentation site scope is well-defined and appropriately minimal. The benchmark YAML-to-chart pipeline is the most impactful feature for driving library adoption - developers choosing a scene detection library will compare F1 scores and processing speed before reading the API reference.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro Starlight theme provides the doc navigation, versioning, and i18n scaffolding for free. Pagefind requires running pagefind --site dist after astro build in CI; it generates a static search index from the HTML output. The BenchmarkChart React island should read YAML at Astro build time via getStaticPaths, not at runtime, to maintain static-only constraint. WASM binary must be served from the same origin with Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp headers to use SharedArrayBuffer in the demo.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP header must include wasm-unsafe-eval in script-src for the WASM demo. No cookies other than Vercel's edge analytics. HTTPS enforced automatically by Vercel.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Astro build time under 2 minutes is achievable for <200 MDX pages. Pagefind indexing adds ~15 seconds for this content volume. Monitor if benchmark YAML grows to many thousands of rows.

backend.service_design: No backend service. All data processing happens at Astro build time. Astro content collections parse MDX and YAML files. Pagefind indexes built HTML output post-build.
backend.api_patterns: No server API. All content served as static HTML/JS/WASM files from Vercel CDN.
backend.failure_handling: Build failures caught by GitHub Actions CI. Vercel only promotes to production on successful build.

security.auth_design: No authentication required. Public read-only site.
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel Edge Network handles DDoS protection automatically. Rate limiting not applicable to static CDN delivery.
security.privacy_controls: Vercel Analytics uses edge-computed metrics without client-side cookies. No GDPR consent UI required. Privacy policy page linked in footer.

data.entities: doc_pages (MDX files in /src/content/docs/ with frontmatter: title, description, category, order), benchmark_results (YAML in /data/benchmarks/ with fields: model_name, dataset, f1_score, precision, recall, fps, hardware), demo_clips (short WebM test clips in /public/demo-clips/ served statically), changelog_entries (MDX files in /src/content/changelog/ with version, date, changes array), api_reference_sections (MDX generated from Python docstrings via sphinx-to-mdx script at repo sync)
data.storage_design: All content in Git repository. No database. YAML benchmark files parsed by Astro content collections via Zod schema validation at build time.
data.analytics_events: Vercel Analytics automatically tracks page views, Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per page, and referrer sources. No custom event tracking.

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repository. Automatic production deploy on merge to main. Preview deployment for every PR branch. Edge CDN serves all static assets globally.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR and main push: npm ci, astro check (TypeScript), astro build, pagefind --site dist. Vercel GitHub integration then picks up the build for deployment. On benchmark YAML updates, chart components regenerated automatically via Astro build.
devops.rollback_strategy: Vercel deployment history allows one-click rollback to any previous production deployment via Vercel dashboard.
devops.observability_stack: Vercel Analytics dashboard for page views, Web Vitals, and traffic sources. No additional monitoring tooling.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 documentation site with MDX pages; React islands for interactive benchmark charts and code playgrounds; Pagefind for full-text search
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Getting Started, API Reference (auto-generated from docstrings), Benchmark Results, Code Examples, Changelog
frontend.ui_modules: VersionDropdown, BenchmarkComparisonChart (React island), CodePlayground, PagefindSearchModal, APIMethodSignatureBlock
frontend.performance_notes: Pagefind search index loaded on demand; benchmark chart data loaded as static JSON; code examples server-rendered with Shiki for zero JS cost
frontend.accessibility_notes: Benchmark charts must provide data table alternative; code blocks need descriptive aria-label indicating language and purpose

target_output.thinking_summary: Static website with no server runtime, no database, no auth. Key decisions: Astro 4 with Starlight theme for zero-config doc structure; benchmark YAML parsed at build time via Astro content collections with Zod validation to catch malformed benchmark entries before deployment; Pagefind for static search without a search API; WASM demo served from same origin with COOP/COEP headers configured in vercel.json. Active specialists for a static_website are backend (no-op), security, data (content entities), and devops; no FrontendAgent in specialist subplans per runtime gating rules.
target_output.title: SceneDetect Library Documentation Site
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro 4 documentation and showcase site for an open-source ML video scene detection library, hosted on Vercel Edge Network. MDX content pages, API reference auto-generated from Python docstrings, and benchmark comparison charts rendered from committed YAML data files are all pre-built at deploy time. An interactive WebAssembly demo lets visitors run scene boundary detection on uploaded clips entirely in-browser with no server calls. Pagefind provides offline-ready static search. GitHub Actions CI rebuilds and deploys on every merge to main, with Vercel preview URLs for every pull request.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 with Starlight theme generates a static site from MDX content collections and YAML benchmark data files at build time. No JavaScript runs at request time for doc pages; React islands hydrate only for the BenchmarkChart (Recharts) and WasmDemo components. Pagefind indexes the built HTML output and ships as a static search asset. The scene detection WebAssembly binary is compiled from the library's Rust/Python bindings and served from /public/wasm/ on the same origin. Vercel Edge Network delivers all assets globally with automatic HTTPS. Vercel Analytics provides privacy-first page view and Web Vitals tracking without cookies.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Astro 4.x with Starlight documentation theme, MDX, TypeScript. UI Islands: React 18 (BenchmarkChart via Recharts, WasmDemo player). Content: Astro Content Collections with Zod schemas for YAML benchmark files and MDX frontmatter. Search: Pagefind (static search index). Hosting: Vercel Edge Network. Analytics: Vercel Analytics (privacy-first, no cookies). CI: GitHub Actions. WASM: pre-compiled scene detection library binary targeting wasm32-unknown-unknown.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Documentation Pages: Starlight-themed MDX pages organized by category (Getting Started, API Reference, Tutorials, Benchmarks, Changelog) with sidebar nav and breadcrumbs. API Reference: MDX files auto-generated from Python docstrings via sphinx-to-mdx conversion script run at library release time. Benchmark Charts: BenchmarkChart React island reads YAML from /data/benchmarks/ at build time and renders Recharts bar/line charts comparing F1 score, precision, recall, and fps across model variants and datasets. WASM Demo: WasmDemo React island loads scene_detect.wasm, accepts drag-and-drop WebM clip upload, processes it entirely in-browser, renders scene boundaries as a visual timeline. Static Search: Pagefind index built post-astro-build enables full-text search across all doc pages without a search API.
target_output.system_components: Astro build pipeline: content collection parser (MDX + YAML via Zod), Starlight theme layout, React island bundler (Vite). BenchmarkChart island: Recharts BarChart/LineChart rendering benchmark_results YAML data injected as Astro props at build time. WasmDemo island: fetch /wasm/scene_detect.wasm, instantiate WebAssembly module, accept File drag-drop, call exported detect_scenes(video_bytes) returning scene boundary timestamps, render SVG timeline. Pagefind indexer: post-build CLI run in GitHub Actions, outputs /_pagefind/ static assets. Vercel deployment: zero-config Astro adapter, vercel.json for COOP/COEP headers on /wasm/* routes. Vercel Analytics: injected via Astro Vercel adapter.
target_output.workflows: Benchmark Update Deployment: Library maintainer commits updated /data/benchmarks/v1.2.yaml with new F1 scores. GitHub Actions triggers: npm ci, astro check, astro build (BenchmarkChart island receives new YAML data as build-time props and regenerates chart HTML), pagefind --site dist (rebuilds search index including new benchmark page content), Vercel deployment picks up build artifacts. Preview URL generated; maintainer verifies chart renders correctly before merging. WASM Demo Interaction: Visitor navigates to /demo page. WasmDemo React island hydrates (client:visible). Visitor drags a .webm clip onto the dropzone. Island reads File as ArrayBuffer, POSTs to scene_detect.wasm exported detect_scenes function (no network call). WASM returns Float64Array of scene boundary timestamps. Island renders each boundary as a colored tick mark on a video timeline SVG. No user data leaves the browser.
target_output.data_model: doc_pages (MDX frontmatter: title String, description String, category Enum('getting_started','api','tutorials','benchmarks','changelog'), order Int, editUrl String). benchmark_results (YAML per file: model_name String, dataset String, f1_score Float, precision Float, recall Float, fps Float, hardware String, library_version String). demo_clips (static WebM files in /public/demo-clips/ referenced by slug). changelog_entries (MDX frontmatter: version SemVer, date ISO, type Enum('feat','fix','breaking')). api_reference_sections (MDX generated by sphinx-to-mdx: function_name, signature, parameters array, returns, example_code). All validated by Zod schemas in Astro content collection config.
target_output.api_design: No server API. Static asset URLs: GET /docs/:slug (MDX-rendered HTML page). GET /api/:function_name (API reference page). GET /benchmarks (benchmark chart page with pre-rendered Recharts SVG). GET /demo (WasmDemo page). GET /_pagefind/pagefind.js (Pagefind search bundle). GET /wasm/scene_detect.wasm (WASM binary for demo). All served as static files from Vercel CDN. No dynamic routes or serverless functions.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Vercel for all traffic. Content Security Policy in vercel.json: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval' (required for WebAssembly); style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:. COOP and COEP headers on /wasm/* and /demo routes: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin, Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp (required for SharedArrayBuffer in WASM demo). No cookies except Vercel Analytics edge-computed metrics. No PII collected. Privacy policy page at /privacy linked in footer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub repository via Vercel GitHub App. Production deployment triggered automatically on merge to main. Preview deployment generated for every PR branch with unique URL posted as PR comment. Build command: npm ci && astro build && npx pagefind --site dist. Build output: dist/ directory. vercel.json configures headers (CSP, COOP, COEP) and Astro framework preset. Rollback: one-click via Vercel Deployments dashboard to any previous production build. Environment variables: VERCEL_ANALYTICS_ID (injected by Vercel). No secrets required.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics (auto-injected via Astro Vercel adapter): page views per route, Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per page, traffic sources, device breakdown. Vercel build logs for CI visibility. GitHub Actions workflow summary shows build time and Pagefind index size. No custom telemetry or error tracking; for a static doc site, Vercel's built-in analytics are sufficient.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby plan: free for open-source project (unlimited static deployments, 100GB edge bandwidth/month). Vercel Analytics: free tier (2,500 page views/day). Storage: zero (all content in Git). CDN handles any traffic spike automatically with no configuration. If bandwidth exceeds free tier at high adoption, Vercel Pro at $20/month adds 1TB bandwidth.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Starlight Astro setup, MDX doc pages (Getting Started, Installation, Quick Start, API Reference), benchmark YAML files with BenchmarkChart island, changelog MDX entries, Pagefind search, Vercel deployment with CSP headers, Vercel Analytics. Phase 2 (3 weeks): WasmDemo island with scene_detect.wasm integration, COOP/COEP headers for SharedArrayBuffer, demo-clips test videos in /public/, demo page with timeline visualization. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Versioned docs via Starlight versioning plugin (docs/v0.x/ and docs/v1.x/ paths), community gallery MDX page, integration guides for FFmpeg pipeline and OpenCV pipeline use cases.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for all Astro and React island code. Zod schemas required for all Astro content collections (benchmark YAML and MDX frontmatter) to catch malformed data at build time. No client-side API calls from any component - all data injected as build-time props. Astro check (TypeScript) runs in CI. Lighthouse CI in GitHub Actions with minimum scores: Performance 95, Accessibility 100, SEO 100. Benchmark YAML files follow the schema in /data/benchmarks/README.md; PR template reminds contributors to validate YAML.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WASM binary size impacting demo page load time on mobile; Mitigation: compress with Brotli (Vercel default), lazy-load WASM only on /demo route via client:visible hydration. Risk: sphinx-to-mdx API reference generation producing malformed MDX; Mitigation: Zod content collection schema catches frontmatter issues at build time, failing the CI build before deployment. Tradeoff: Astro Starlight theme vs. custom Astro setup - chosen Starlight for doc navigation, sidebar, versioning, and search scaffolding out of the box. Risk: Vercel free tier build minute limits if benchmark YAML grows large; Mitigation: content collections with lazy loading and incremental build caching.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Pagefind confirmed as static search solution; runs as CLI post-build in GitHub Actions. WASM demo uses client:visible hydration to avoid blocking page load. Benchmark YAML parsed by Astro content collections with Zod validation at build time. Vercel free tier sufficient for open-source project bandwidth.

metadata.notes: Static Astro docs site for ML scene detection library; no server runtime; WASM interactive demo; benchmark charts from YAML data files.

profile.domain: Short-Form Vertical Video (Gen Z Creator Platform)

project_goal.value: Flutter-based short-form vertical video app for Gen Z creators, decomposed from a monolith into microservices to independently scale the feed ranking, upload processing, and social graph components
project_goal.rationale: Monolith was unable to scale feed ranking during peak events without impacting upload processing; microservices decomposition enables independent scaling per service

target_users.value: Gen Z creators (aged 13-24) and viewers globally, with specific compliance requirements for under-13 users under COPPA
target_users.rationale: Creator-first platform with algorithmic feed for viewer engagement

project_class.rationale: Primary client is Flutter mobile app (iOS and Android); web not in MVP scope

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, ai_llm, analytics, realtime, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: LLM-assisted feed ranking relevance model, Firebase Auth, Cassandra feed events, Datadog analytics, gRPC realtime, and moderator admin panel all required

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-million user scale with sub-100ms feed delivery, real-time engagement signal ingestion, and content moderation at upload volume

risk_level.rationale: Handles minors' personal data under COPPA; DSA content moderation obligations; GDPR for EU users

data_sensitivity.rationale: Creator and viewer profiles, engagement history, social graph edges, and upload metadata are personal data subject to GDPR, COPPA, and DSA

external_exposure.rationale: Public-facing mobile app and creator upload API accessible over internet

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with anonymous mode for non-logged viewers; full account for creators; parental consent flow for under-13 via COPPA-compliant parental verification
access_model.rationale: COPPA mandates verifiable parental consent before collecting data from under-13 users

feature_scope.value: Vertical video feed with algorithmic ranking, creator upload with transcoding pipeline, social graph (follow/unfollow), engagement signals (like, share, comment, watch-time), content moderation queue, admin panel for moderators, trending topics, ad placements
feature_scope.rationale: Full creator platform feature set for Gen Z short-form video

mvp_scope.value: Video upload and transcoding, algorithmic feed ranking with LLM-assisted relevance, social follow graph, engagement signals, basic content moderation, moderator admin panel
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for platform launch with creator and viewer experience

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth with JWT, TLS 1.3 for all gRPC and HTTP, COPPA parental consent flow, GDPR consent management, content hash fingerprinting for known-bad content detection
security_baseline.rationale: Regulatory obligations for minors' data and DSA content moderation requirements

frontend_stack.value: Flutter 3.22 with Dart, Riverpod for state management, gRPC-Dart for backend communication
frontend_stack.rationale: Flutter enables single codebase for iOS and Android with high-performance video feed rendering

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 microservices communicating via gRPC; Pub/Sub for async event processing between services
backend_stack.rationale: Go concurrency model suits high-throughput feed and upload services; gRPC for efficient mobile-to-backend binary protocol

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (creator_profiles, social_edges, video_posts metadata); Cassandra (feed_rankings, engagement_signals time-series); Redis 7 (trending_topics, feed cache)
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational social graph; Cassandra for write-heavy engagement event streams at scale; Redis for low-latency trending and feed cache

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP, Cloud CDN for video delivery, Cloud Storage for uploaded and transcoded video segments
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot for autoscaling microservices; GCP Pub/Sub for event streaming; Cloud CDN for global video delivery

privacy_retention_policy.value: Engagement signals retained 18 months; creator content retained until deleted by creator; GDPR right-to-erasure cascade across all services within 72 hours; COPPA data for under-13 users retained only for session duration or with parental consent
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17, DSA transparency reporting, and COPPA data minimization requirements

future_scope.value: Monetization for creators (digital gifts, subscription tiers), live streaming, AR face filters via TensorFlow Lite, web viewer experience
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap for creator economy and platform expansion

constraints.value: Feed delivery p99 latency <100ms; video upload processing under 60 seconds for clips up to 3 minutes; CDN purge on content takedown within 60 seconds; moderator action propagation within 60 seconds of admin panel trigger
constraints.rationale: Creator and viewer experience SLAs and DSA takedown compliance timing

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for all Go microservices, Datadog RUM for Flutter crash tracking, custom feed delivery latency and upload processing duration metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Multi-service observability requires distributed tracing to diagnose cross-service latency

execution_preference.value: Service-team ownership model; each microservice owned by a dedicated team; Cloud Build CI per service repository
execution_preference.rationale: Independent deployment cadence per microservice with team ownership

compliance_context.value: COPPA: verifiable parental consent required for under-13 users, data minimization, no behavioral advertising targeting minors. GDPR: consent management, right-to-erasure within 72 hours. DSA: proactive content moderation, transparency report, takedown propagation within 60 seconds.
compliance_context.rationale: Platform serving minors globally triggers COPPA, GDPR, and DSA obligations simultaneously

llm_integration.value: LLM-assisted feed ranking relevance model: Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API generates 768-dim content embeddings for video_posts at upload time; feed-ranking service uses cosine similarity between viewer interest profile embeddings and candidate video embeddings to re-rank top-K candidates every 30 seconds
llm_integration.rationale: Pure collaborative filtering insufficient for cold-start creators; LLM content embeddings provide content-based signal for new creator video discovery

project.goal: Decompose monolithic short-form video app into independently scalable Go microservices for feed ranking, upload processing, and social graph
project.target_users: Gen Z creators (13-24) and viewers globally
project.timeline: Phased decomposition over 20 weeks

frontend.state: Riverpod for feed state, creator upload state
frontend.api: gRPC-Dart proto-generated clients for all service calls

backend.framework: gRPC with protobuf, Pub/Sub for async events
backend.services: feed-ranking, upload-processor, social-graph, content-moderation, engagement-collector, notification, admin-api

security.auth: Firebase Auth with custom claims (age_verified, coppa_minor, region)
security.drm: None - no DRM on UGC content
security.moderation: content hash fingerprinting + LLM moderation scoring

devops.cdn: Cloud CDN for HLS video segments

open_questions.live_streaming: Live stream architecture deferred to Phase 3

reasoner_reviews.product: The microservices decomposition order should prioritize the feed-ranking service first as it was the primary scaling bottleneck in the monolith. The COPPA parental consent flow must gate all data collection for under-13 users - this is not purely a frontend concern, it requires a custom claim in Firebase Auth tokens that all backend services must check before storing engagement signals.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The LLM embedding pipeline at upload time using Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 is the right choice for cold-start discovery. The 768-dim embeddings should be stored in PostgreSQL with pgvector extension for cosine similarity queries during feed ranking - this avoids a separate vector database dependency at this scale. The feed-ranking service re-scoring every 30 seconds is a batch operation over Redis-cached candidate sets, not a per-request LLM call, which is critical for the p99 <100ms constraint.
reasoner_reviews.security: The CDN purge within 60 seconds of moderator action is the highest-risk SLA to meet. Cloud CDN cache invalidation via the CDN Interconnect API must be in the critical path of the admin-panel takedown flow, not an async background job. DSA requires logged evidence of takedown propagation time for regulatory audits.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 1 plan did not specify how the feed-ranking service achieves p99 <100ms for the initial feed load while also computing LLM embedding similarity. The embedding similarity computation must be pre-computed and cached in Redis, not computed at request time. Also absent: the CDN purge job architecture for the 60-second takedown SLA.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Two critical gaps from round 1: (1) embedding similarity pre-computation strategy not defined - real-time LLM calls in the p99 critical path will never meet 100ms. (2) CDN purge path for content takedown not specified, which is a DSA compliance blocker.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter 3.22 app: VerticalFeedScreen (PageView with preloaded VideoPlayerControllers), UploadScreen (clip trimmer + caption editor), CreatorProfileScreen, SocialGraphScreen (following/followers), NotificationScreen, AdminPanel (moderator-only bottom tab visible on admin role).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Feed flow: App launch fetches GET feed-ranking/v1/feed (gRPC), pre-loads top 3 VideoPlayerControllers. Each swipe triggers engagement event (watch_time_ms, swipe_direction) sent to engagement-collector via gRPC streaming. Upload flow: Creator selects clip, trims to 60s max, adds caption, POSTs to upload-processor, shows transcoding progress via Pub/Sub SSE fallback. CDN purge effect: Plato Flutter video player catches 403/404 on takedown CDN URLs and swaps to 'content unavailable' placeholder without app restart.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod providers: feedProvider (AsyncNotifier fetching and caching feed), uploadProvider (StateNotifier tracking upload progress and transcoding status), authProvider (Firebase Auth stream with custom claims).
frontend.performance_notes: Flutter's Impeller rendering engine reduces jank on vertical swipe. VideoPlayerController pre-loaded 2 items ahead. Feed items beyond +3/-3 from current position disposed to manage memory. WebP thumbnails loaded before video buffer starts.

backend.service_design: Go microservices: feed-ranking (gRPC, reads Redis feed cache and Cassandra engagement signals), upload-processor (gRPC + Pub/Sub, spawns Cloud Run transcode jobs), social-graph (gRPC, PostgreSQL social_edges), engagement-collector (gRPC streaming, writes Cassandra engagement_signals), content-moderation (Pub/Sub consumer, hash check + LLM moderation scoring), admin-api (gRPC, moderator actions), notification (Pub/Sub consumer, FCM push).
backend.api_patterns: gRPC proto definitions: FeedService.GetFeed(ViewerFeedRequest) returns FeedResponse; UploadService.StartUpload(UploadRequest) returns UploadSession; EngagementService.RecordSignals(stream EngagementEvent) returns Ack; SocialService.Follow(FollowRequest); AdminService.TakedownVideo(TakedownRequest) returns TakedownAck with cdn_purge_job_id.
backend.failure_handling: Feed ranking falls back to Redis-cached previous feed if Cassandra re-scoring times out (>80ms). Upload processor retries Cloud Run transcode job up to 3 times on failure; marks video_post status as transcode_failed after max retries. CDN purge job failure triggers PagerDuty alert and retry within 5 seconds to meet 60s SLA.
backend.background_jobs: Embedding pre-computation job: Pub/Sub triggered on video_post transcoded event, calls Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API, stores 768-dim vector in PostgreSQL video_posts.content_embedding (pgvector). Feed re-scoring job: runs every 30 seconds per active viewer (Cassandra fan-out read of recent engagement_signals, cosine similarity against cached candidate embeddings in Redis, write ranked feed to Redis feed_cache:{viewer_id}).

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth with custom claims: age_verified BOOL, coppa_minor BOOL, region STRING, moderator BOOL. Custom claims set by auth-service on registration and age verification. All backend gRPC interceptors validate JWT and check coppa_minor claim before writing engagement signals.
security.authorization_model: Viewer: feed read + engagement signals only. Creator: upload + own content management. Moderator: read moderation_queue + TakedownVideo + ApplyLabel. Admin: full platform access. COPPA minor: engagement signals blocked server-side; no behavioral ad targeting; watch-time signals not stored beyond session.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting via Redis token bucket: 100 feed refreshes/min per viewer, 10 uploads/day per creator. Content hash fingerprinting against NCMEC PhotoDNA and internal bad-content hash list at upload time. DSA: transparency report endpoint GET /admin/dsa-report returns takedown counts by category and average propagation time.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR right-to-erasure: deletion-coordinator service receives DELETE /users/:id request, fans out deletion events to all services via Pub/Sub, each service purges user data within 72 hours. COPPA: under-13 user data deleted at session end unless parental consent provided and verified.

data.entities: creator_profiles (id UUID PK, firebase_uid, username, display_name, region, age_verified BOOL, coppa_minor BOOL, follower_count, following_count, created_at), video_posts (id UUID PK, creator_id UUID FK, title, caption, hls_manifest_url, thumbnail_url, duration_seconds, status ENUM uploading/transcoding/active/taken_down, content_embedding vector(768), moderation_score FLOAT, created_at), feed_rankings (viewer_id UUID, segment_key TEXT, ranked_video_ids LIST<UUID>, scored_at TIMESTAMP) PRIMARY KEY (viewer_id, segment_key) in Cassandra, engagement_signals (viewer_id UUID, video_id UUID, signal_type ENUM like/share/comment/watch_complete/skip, watch_time_ms INT, occurred_at TIMESTAMP) PRIMARY KEY (viewer_id, occurred_at) in Cassandra, social_edges (follower_id UUID, followee_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMP) in PostgreSQL, moderation_queues (id UUID PK, video_id UUID, queue_type ENUM hash_match/llm_flagged/user_report, assigned_moderator_id UUID, status ENUM pending/reviewed/actioned, created_at), trending_topics (topic STRING, score FLOAT, updated_at TIMESTAMP) in Redis sorted set
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for content_embedding cosine similarity queries. Cassandra 4.1 for append-heavy engagement_signals and feed_rankings. Redis 7 for trending_topics ZSET and feed_cache:{viewer_id} LIST. Cloud Storage for uploaded raw video and transcoded HLS segments. Cloud CDN origin pointing at Cloud Storage bucket.
data.analytics_events: Datadog custom metrics: feed_delivery_latency_p99, upload_processing_duration_p95, engagement_signal_throughput, cdn_purge_latency. ClickHouse (separate analytics cluster) for ad hoc creator analytics (views, watch-time per video over time).

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot clusters: us-central1 (primary), europe-west1 (EU GDPR data residency). Each cluster runs all Go microservices. Cloud CDN with dual-region origin failover. Cloud Build per service repository triggers on push to main. HPA on CPU + custom Datadog metrics (feed_request_queue_depth for feed-ranking, upload_queue_depth for upload-processor).
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build: go test ./..., golangci-lint, ko build (container image from Go binary), kubectl apply via Workload Identity. Flutter CI: flutter test, flutter build appbundle, Firebase App Distribution for internal testing. canary deploy: 5% traffic via Istio VirtualService weight before full rollout.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with gRPC interceptor tracing on all services. Distributed trace IDs propagated via gRPC metadata. Datadog RUM in Flutter for crash reporting and ANR. PagerDuty alerts: feed_delivery_p99 >100ms, cdn_purge_latency >55s, upload_failure_rate >5%.

ISS-001.title: LLM embedding similarity computed at request time in feed critical path
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan showed feed-ranking service calling Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5 embedding API during GetFeed request. This adds 200-400ms per request, making p99 <100ms feed latency impossible.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner and constraints reviewer

ISS-002.title: CDN purge path for content takedown not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan showed moderator admin panel triggering takedown without defining the Cloud CDN cache invalidation call. DSA requires 60-second propagation which requires CDN purge in the synchronous takedown path, not a background job.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer and critic

ISS-003.title: COPPA minor engagement signal blocking not enforced server-side
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan noted COPPA requirements but engagement-collector service did not check the coppa_minor JWT claim before persisting engagement_signals to Cassandra.

history.note: Raised by security reviewer

focus_issues.title: LLM embedding similarity computed at request time in feed critical path
focus_issues.detail: Vertex AI embedding API call during GetFeed adds 200-400ms, making p99 <100ms impossible.

focus_issues.title: CDN purge path for content takedown not specified
focus_issues.detail: Moderator takedown flow missing Cloud CDN cache invalidation in synchronous path; DSA 60s compliance at risk.

latest_recommendations: Pre-compute content embeddings at upload-complete time via Pub/Sub-triggered job; store in PostgreSQL pgvector; cache cosine similarity scores in Redis at 30s intervals, not per-request

Add synchronous Cloud CDN cache invalidation to AdminService.TakedownVideo gRPC handler: call Cloud CDN API within the same transaction that updates video_post.status=taken_down; log cdn_purge_job_id to moderation audit trail for DSA evidence
Add gRPC interceptor to engagement-collector that reads coppa_minor claim from Firebase JWT and drops the engagement event (returns Ack without persisting) for COPPA minor users

previous_audits.summary: Microservices decomposition structure is well-defined but two critical issues prevent meeting core SLAs: LLM call in feed request path makes p99 <100ms impossible, and CDN purge path is absent making DSA 60s takedown compliance a blocker.
strengths: Go microservices service boundary definitions are clear
Cassandra data model for engagement_signals is appropriate for write volume
pgvector for content embeddings is a good choice avoiding separate vector DB

Add Cloud CDN API call synchronously within TakedownVideo gRPC handler; log purge_job_id to audit trail
