target_output.title: Media Archive Multimodal Search Engine
target_output.executive_summary: A GKE Autopilot-hosted multimodal video search system for a large broadcast archive, using self-hosted CLIP and Whisper models on Vertex AI to generate searchable embeddings across visual keyframes, ASR transcripts, and catalog metadata for millions of archival clips. Round 3 resolves critical Weaviate versioning and NetworkPolicy issues while maintaining semantic search latency under 500ms p95.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four Python FastAPI microservices deployed on GKE Autopilot in us-central1: ingest-worker orchestrates frame extraction, Whisper ASR, and Vertex AI embedding generation; search-api handles natural-language queries via ANN retrieval from versioned Weaviate collections fused with PostgreSQL BM25 catalog scores; catalog-api manages structured metadata and taxonomy; admin-api controls model version promotion and taxonomy editing. All services communicate over internal GKE service mesh with mTLS. Google IAP terminates external authentication at the GKE ingress with OIDC token validation at each service boundary. Weaviate 1.24 maintains separate named collections per embedding model version enabling zero-downtime model migrations.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2. Vector Store: Weaviate 1.24 with HNSW index, version-namespaced collections. Metadata DB: PostgreSQL 16 with pg_trgm for BM25 text scoring. Embedding: Vertex AI custom model endpoints running self-hosted CLIP ViT-L/14 for visual, Whisper large-v3 for ASR, sentence-transformers for text encoding. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot us-central1, GCS for video and keyframe storage, Cloud Build CI/CD, Cloud Logging, Google Cloud Monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Natural-Language Search: archivist enters query 'protest footage from a European capital in winter' and receives ranked clip results with timecode-level previews and similarity scores. Timecode Preview: clicking a result scrubs the clip player to the matched keyframe offset. Relevance Feedback: archivists mark results relevant or irrelevant; signals batch into monthly fine-tuning jobs. Taxonomy Management: admin UI to add controlled vocabulary terms for genre, decade, and geographic region. Model Version Promotion: admin triggers parallel indexing of new model version, reviews shadow recall evaluation, and atomically switches routing ConfigMap.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot Cluster: ingest-worker pods on GPU spot node pool for Vertex AI embedding calls, search-api pods on general node pool with HPA on RPS, catalog-api and admin-api pods. Weaviate cluster on high-memory stateful pods (3 replicas, 64GB RAM each) with one collection per embedding_model_version. PostgreSQL 16 managed instance for catalog metadata. GCS buckets for raw video, keyframes, and model artifacts. Vertex AI custom model endpoints for CLIP and Whisper inference. Cloud Scheduler for batch jobs including GDPR TTL deletion and relevance feedback export.
target_output.workflows: Ingestion Workflow: ingest-worker polls for archival_clip records with ingest_status=pending, fetches video from GCS, extracts keyframes at 2fps via FFmpeg, runs Whisper ASR on audio, calls Vertex AI CLIP endpoint to generate 512-d visual embeddings per keyframe, encodes transcript segments via sentence-transformer, writes vectors to the active Weaviate collection tagged with model_version_id, updates ingest_status to indexed. Natural-Language Search Workflow: archivist submits query to search-api, service embeds the query string via Vertex AI text encoder, executes ANN search against the current active Weaviate collection returning top-100 candidates, fetches matching catalog records from PostgreSQL, applies BM25 re-ranking on title and taxonomy metadata, returns ranked results with timecode offsets and GCS-signed thumbnail URLs as SSE stream.
target_output.data_model: archival_clips (id UUID PK, catalog_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, title, broadcast_date, duration_seconds, storage_gcs_path, ingest_status ENUM pending/indexing/indexed/failed, created_at). multimodal_embeddings (id UUID PK, clip_id FK, model_version_id FK, vector_id_weaviate VARCHAR, embedding_type ENUM visual/asr_text/metadata, frame_offset_ms INT, created_at). embedding_model_versions (id UUID PK, model_name VARCHAR, version_tag VARCHAR UNIQUE, weaviate_collection_name VARCHAR UNIQUE, promoted_at TIMESTAMP, deprecated_at TIMESTAMP). transcript_segments (id UUID PK, clip_id FK, start_ms INT, end_ms INT, text TEXT, confidence FLOAT). visual_keyframes (id UUID PK, clip_id FK, frame_offset_ms INT, gcs_path VARCHAR, scene_label VARCHAR). search_queries (id UUID PK, user_sub VARCHAR, query_text TEXT, result_clip_ids UUID[], latency_ms INT, created_at TIMESTAMP). relevance_feedback_logs (id UUID PK, query_id FK, clip_id FK, signal ENUM relevant/irrelevant, created_at). catalog_taxonomies (id UUID PK, term VARCHAR, parent_id FK, vocabulary_set ENUM genre/decade/geography).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with OIDC bearer token required on all endpoints. POST /search (body: {query: string, filters: {decade?, genre?, geography?}}) returns SSE stream of ranked results with clip_id, timecode_ms, score, thumbnail_url. GET /clips/:id returns clip metadata and embedding status. GET /clips/:id/preview?t=ms returns GCS signed URL for keyframe at timecode. POST /feedback (body: {query_id, clip_id, signal}) records relevance signal. GET /admin/model-versions lists versions with recall scores. POST /admin/model-versions/:id/promote triggers namespace switch after recall validation. POST /admin/taxonomy creates controlled vocabulary term. Licensing role required for /clips/:id/export.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Google IAP terminates at GKE ingress for all inbound traffic; downstream services validate OIDC tokens on every request and enforce RBAC (archivist, researcher, licensing, admin). Kubernetes NetworkPolicy applied to Weaviate namespace: default deny-all egress with explicit ingress allow rules from ingest-worker pods (port 8080) and search-api pods (port 8080); no external egress permitted from Weaviate pods. GCS buckets use CMEK with Cloud KMS. TLS 1.3 enforced on all GKE service mesh communication. Search query audit log written to Cloud Logging with user_sub, query_text, result_clip_ids, and latency. Cloud Scheduler triggers daily deletion job removing search_queries records and Cloud Logging entries older than 180 days to satisfy GDPR retention limits. GDPR data subject requests handled via catalog_code lookup and pseudonymization of clip metadata containing identifiable individuals.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1 with three logical node pools: general (search-api 3-10 replicas HPA on RPS, catalog-api, admin-api), GPU spot (ingest-worker batch pods scaled by Cloud Scheduler job queue depth), high-memory stateful (Weaviate 3 replicas with 64GB RAM PVCs). Weaviate versioned collection routing: ingest-worker writes to the embedding_model_versions.weaviate_collection_name for the current active version; search-api reads routing from a ConfigMap updated atomically on model promotion; old collection retained 30 days post-promotion before deletion. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy for Weaviate namespace enforces deny-all egress with explicit allow rules for ingest-worker and search-api pods only. Cloud Build CI/CD: unit tests, container builds, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy. Model promotion requires manual approval in Cloud Build with shadow recall evaluation step comparing new vs old collection recall@10 on held-out query set. Cloud Scheduler jobs: daily GDPR TTL deletion (search_queries > 180 days), nightly relevance feedback batch export for fine-tuning, weekly embedding coverage report.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboards. Search p50/p95 latency tracked per endpoint. Cloud Trace instruments the multimodal embedding pipeline with spans for video frame extraction, CLIP encoding, and Vertex Matching Engine query latency. Cloud Logging captures structured search request logs with query_text, num_results, and embedding_latency_ms fields. Alert policies fire when search p95 exceeds 2s or when the Vertex Matching Engine index build job fails. BigQuery log sink archives search queries for weekly usage analysis.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vertex AI custom endpoint minimum 2 warm replicas at approximately $800 per month; scales to 8 replicas during bulk ingestion batches. GCS nearline storage for video archive reduces per-GB cost versus standard class. Weaviate high-memory pods approximately $600 per month for 3 replicas with 64GB RAM each to hold HNSW index in memory for 50M vectors. Total steady-state estimate approximately $2,200 per month; bulk re-indexing batches add approximately $400 per model version migration event.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Ingestion pipeline for existing archive backlog covering visual and ASR embeddings, Weaviate HNSW index with first model version collection, search-api with natural-language query and timecode results, archivist React UI with search and preview, Google IAP auth and OIDC role enforcement. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Relevance feedback collection UI, monthly fine-tuning pipeline with Cloud Batch jobs, embedding_model_versions promotion workflow with shadow recall evaluation, versioned Weaviate collection routing via ConfigMap. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Licensing role clip export feature, taxonomy admin UI, Cloud Scheduler GDPR TTL deletion job, catalog_taxonomies controlled vocabulary for faceted filtering on decade, genre, and geography.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints enforced via mypy in Cloud Build CI. FastAPI endpoints require Pydantic v2 request/response models with explicit field validation. Weaviate schema changes versioned in migration scripts committed to infra repo alongside application code. Embedding model artifacts stored in GCS with version tags matching embedding_model_versions.version_tag. PR requires integration test against staging Weaviate instance before merge. No external HTTP calls from within GKE cluster permitted except to Vertex AI endpoints and GCS APIs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Weaviate HNSW recall degrades if ef_construction parameter is too low during index build at 50M+ vectors. Mitigation: benchmark recall@10 vs latency on staging before promoting each model version. Risk: Vertex AI custom endpoint cold-start adds 60-90 seconds to first query after idle period. Mitigation: minimum 2 pre-provisioned replicas during business hours via scheduled scaling. Tradeoff: per-version Weaviate collections double storage during 30-day migration windows. Accepted because rollback capability is critical for model quality regression scenarios. Risk: Whisper ASR quality on archival audio with broadcast noise requires domain fine-tuning; initial retrieval recall may be lower for speech-heavy content.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed self-hosted CLIP ViT-L/14 over external OpenAI CLIP API due to content confidentiality constraint. Weaviate chosen over Pinecone because named collections support versioned migration without vendor index limitations. Vertex AI chosen over self-managed GPU VMs to reduce MLOps overhead while keeping compute within GCP. PostgreSQL BM25 fusion via pg_trgm selected over Weaviate keyword search for richer metadata scoring on catalog fields.

metadata.notes: Round 3 flawed plan: ISS-001 and ISS-002 now correctly reflected in plan sections, but observability section is intentionally under-specified to leave a residual quality issue.

project_goal.value: Self-hosted live streaming studio management platform for esports tournament organizers with scene switching, overlay management, and multi-destination RTMP broadcasting via a web-based production dashboard
project_goal.rationale: Core business need replacing manual OBS-per-operator workflows with a collaborative browser-based production suite

target_users.value: Tournament production directors, broadcast engineers, and overlay operators at esports events
target_users.rationale: Primary personas identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: Requires a browser-based production dashboard backed by a stateful streaming engine and real-time WebSocket control API

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, realtime, integrations, admin_panel, devops
capabilities.rationale: Web canvas, WebSocket control plane, Keycloak RBAC, RTMP multi-destination, admin ops panel, GitLab CI/CD

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-frame scene switching with real-time WebSocket state sync, per-destination RTMP worker isolation, and Vault-based key management

risk_level.rationale: Internal-facing, no PII; risk centers on live broadcast reliability and stream key security during tournament events

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores RTMP stream credentials and overlay templates but no personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Dashboard accessible only to credentialed production staff on event VPN or LAN

access_model.value: RBAC with Keycloak roles: director (full broadcast control), operator (scene and overlay editing), viewer (read-only health monitor)
access_model.rationale: Role separation prevents operators from accidentally issuing go-live or destination commands

feature_scope.value: Scene composer, overlay template editor, multi-destination RTMP broadcaster, pre-show checklist gate, stream health monitor, admin configuration panel for shows and destinations
feature_scope.rationale: Full production suite features confirmed in PRD v2

mvp_scope.value: Scene switching with up to 3 RTMP destinations, basic overlay support, pre-show checklist, stream health panel, Keycloak RBAC auth, Vault stream key management
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP sufficient for first tournament pilot; pre-show checklist promoted to MVP after audit

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all dashboard traffic, RTMP stream keys stored in Vault (never in browser), Keycloak MFA for director role, audit log for scene transitions and go-live events
security_baseline.rationale: Protect live stream credentials and prevent unauthorized scene changes during broadcast

frontend_stack.rationale: App Router enables server components for admin panel while client components handle real-time canvas WebSocket updates

backend_stack.rationale: High-throughput WebSocket handling and low-latency scene-switch command processing with @fastify/websocket plugin

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7
data_platform.rationale: Postgres for show and scene config persistence with Patroni HA; Redis for ephemeral stream state and pub/sub between API pods

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner dedicated servers
hosting_target.rationale: Low-latency network path to RTMP ingest, infrastructure sovereignty, and cost efficiency versus cloud compute

privacy_retention_policy.value: Stream session logs retained 30 days; show and scene configs retained indefinitely; no personal data collected or stored
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 30-day window covers operational debugging; no regulatory retention obligations apply

execution_preference.rationale: Team workflow established in prior projects on Hetzner Kubernetes

compliance_context.rationale: Internal broadcast operations compliance requirements documented in ops runbook

project.goal: Web-based esports production studio replacing manual OBS workflows for tournament broadcasts
project.target_users: Production directors, broadcast engineers, overlay operators
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full feature set in 20 weeks

frontend.state: Zustand for canvas scene state, SWR for admin data, native WebSocket client for real-time events

backend.framework: Fastify with @fastify/websocket
backend.api: WebSocket /ws/production for real-time scene commands; REST /api/v1 for CRUD configuration

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with RBAC; director role requires TOTP MFA
security.transport: TLS 1.3 via NGINX Ingress; RTMP stream keys in Vault never in browser
security.audit: Scene transition and go-live events logged to production_audit_log with operator identity

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with Patroni HA for show and scene config persistence
data.cache: Redis 7 Cluster for stream state, pub/sub between API pods, and session store

devops.ci: GitLab CI with ESLint, Vitest, Docker build, Helm deploy
devops.hosting: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner AX102 nodes

constraints.latency: Scene switch to composite change under 100ms
constraints.self_hosted: No cloud vendor dependencies in broadcast hot path

confirmed_decisions.streaming_engine: custom Node.js child-process RTMP workers per destination
confirmed_decisions.overlay_renderer: Puppeteer headless Chrome with Canvas fallback for static overlays

reasoner_reviews.product: The pre-show checklist is a high-leverage safety feature that prevents on-air incidents caused by misconfigured RTMP destinations or missing source feeds. Promoting it to MVP scope is the correct call and significantly reduces tournament-day risk for production teams.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The per-destination Node.js child process architecture is the right isolation boundary — it keeps RTMP worker failures from affecting the compositing pipeline and allows per-destination reconnection backoff without blocking the main event loop. The Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar pattern is the cleanest way to handle key injection without cloud KMS.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stream key management is the critical security surface in this architecture. The previous plan's design of returning keys to the browser is a fundamental credential exposure flaw. Vault Agent sidecar injection into the streaming engine process environment is the correct pattern — keys exist only in the container memory of the streaming engine pod, never in browser storage or WebSocket frames.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The under-100ms scene-switch latency constraint is achievable on LAN with the Node.js child process model. The bottleneck is the FFmpeg compositing cycle; at 30fps the compositor has 33ms per frame, leaving ample headroom for the WebSocket command round-trip plus one compositing cycle.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router: client components for the real-time scene canvas, overlay layer panel, and broadcast control bar; server components for the admin panel configuration pages; a /monitor route renders a read-only stream health dashboard for viewer-role users
frontend.pages_and_flows: Scene Composer (drag-and-drop source feed and overlay layout), Overlay Editor (HTML/CSS template creation with variable binding schema), Broadcast Control (go-live gate, per-destination status cards, bitrate sparklines), Pre-Show Checklist (pass/fail item list with enable-broadcast gate), Admin Panel (show lifecycle, user role assignment, RTMP destination config showing platform name and status only)
frontend.state_management: Zustand store holds current scene state, active overlay variables, and per-destination health snapshots; WebSocket events from Fastify dispatch scene-switch confirmations and health metric updates into the Zustand store; SWR handles admin panel configuration fetching with 10-second revalidation
frontend.frontend_risks: Puppeteer overlay render latency may cause visible lag during complex CSS animations in long broadcasts; Canvas-based renderer provided as fallback for static overlay templates to reduce headless browser resource usage

backend.service_design: Fastify server with three internal modules: SceneControlService (WebSocket handler and scene state machine dispatching to Redis pub/sub), StreamingEngine (per-destination Node.js child process supervisor managing RTMP mux workers), and OverlayService (Puppeteer lifecycle manager and frame capture relay to compositor)
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket /ws/production accepts typed command objects: scene_switch, go_live, end_broadcast, source_update — authorization guard validates Keycloak role before dispatch; REST /api/v1 routes handle CRUD for shows, scenes, overlays, and destinations; /api/v1/shows/:id/destinations never returns raw stream keys
backend.failure_handling: Per-destination RTMP child processes communicate health over IPC; the supervisor detects missed heartbeats and spawns a replacement worker with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s); a destination failure emits a WebSocket health event to the director UI without interrupting sibling destination workers or the composite stream
backend.scaling_notes: Single Fastify instance handles up to 20 concurrent operator WebSocket connections comfortably at current tournament scale; Redis pub/sub enables horizontal Fastify pod scaling for multi-show concurrent events without shared in-process state

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm per organization with OIDC tokens validated as Fastify middleware on every HTTP request and WebSocket upgrade; director role requires TOTP MFA enforced by Keycloak policy; token expiry set to 1 hour with silent refresh in the Next.js client
security.authorization_model: Role-based command guards in SceneControlService: directors can issue scene_switch, go_live, end_broadcast; operators can update overlays and sources; viewers receive broadcast-only health events; role validated server-side on every WebSocket command
security.secrets_management: HashiCorp Vault KV v2 stores one secret per RTMP destination keyed by destination_id; Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar injects keys as environment variables into streaming engine pod containers at startup; the browser destination config UI shows platform_name and vault_key_ref label only — raw keys never transmitted over network
security.audit_and_logging_controls: production_audit_log table records every scene_switch, go_live, end_broadcast, and key rotation event with operator_id, occurred_at, and payload JSONB for post-broadcast forensic review; logs shipped to Loki with show_id label for correlated querying

data.entities: production_shows, stream_scenes, overlay_templates, rtmp_destinations, broadcast_profiles, stream_health_metrics, production_audit_log
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Patroni primary with synchronous replica for zero-RPO show configuration; stream_health_metrics table partitioned by day with 30-day retention via pg_partman; Redis 7 Cluster for ephemeral scene state and WebSocket pub/sub fanout
data.schema_notes: stream_scenes.layout_json and overlay_templates.variable_schema_json stored as JSONB for flexible compositor queries; rtmp_destinations stores vault_key_ref (Vault path) not the raw key; stream_health_metrics indexed on destination_id and recorded_at for time-range queries
data.retention_and_deletion: stream_health_metrics partitioned by day, auto-dropped after 30 days; production_audit_log retained 1 year for post-incident review; RTMP Vault leases expire post-event and are rotated before the next tournament

devops.deployment_topology: 3-node Hetzner AX102 Kubernetes cluster: nodes 1 and 2 host Next.js, Fastify, Vault, Redis, and Patroni pods; node 3 tainted for streaming engine and Puppeteer workloads to prevent CPU contention with control plane services
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: ESLint and Prettier lint stage, Vitest unit tests with 75% coverage gate, Docker build and push to self-hosted Harbor registry, Helm chart upgrade with --atomic flag triggering auto-rollback on failed pod readiness probes within 5 minutes
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Fastify /metrics (request rate, WebSocket connection count, scene-switch latency histogram) and streaming engine custom metrics (per-destination bitrate_kbps, dropped_frame_rate, reconnect_count); Grafana dashboards for active broadcast and show history; Alertmanager pages on-call if any destination bitrate drops more than 20% sustained for 30 seconds
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release revision on failed health probes; Postgres schema migrations use expand-contract pattern enabling rollback without schema reversion; Vault key references remain stable across app version rollbacks

ISS-001.title: RTMP destination failure crashes composite pipeline
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 streaming engine used a single shared FFmpeg mux context for all destinations; a write error on one RTMP socket threw an unhandled exception that terminated the shared process, dropping all active destinations simultaneously.

history.note: Single shared mux context; no per-destination process isolation

ISS-002.title: RTMP stream keys returned to browser via API
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described a REST endpoint returning raw RTMP stream keys to the browser client to populate the destination configuration UI, exposing live broadcast credentials in browser memory and developer tools.

history.note: Keys returned by /api/v1/destinations to populate browser config form

ISS-003.detail: Round 1 phased implementation placed the pre-show checklist in Phase 2; audit identified it as a safety-critical broadcast gate that must ship in MVP to prevent on-air incidents with misconfigured destinations.

history.note: Scope decision deferred checklist to post-MVP

focus_issues.title: RTMP stream keys returned to browser via API
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan described a REST endpoint returning raw RTMP stream keys to the browser client to populate the destination configuration UI.

previous_plan.title: Esports Studio Broadcaster v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A self-hosted web-based production suite on Hetzner Kubernetes for esports tournament broadcasting, with a Next.js dashboard controlling an RTMP streaming engine via WebSocket, supporting multi-destination streaming to Twitch, YouTube, and private RTMP ingests.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js frontend communicates with a Fastify backend via WebSocket for real-time scene switching commands. The backend drives an FFmpeg-based RTMP mux process compositing talent feeds and overlays before pushing to configured destinations. PostgreSQL stores show configs; Redis caches active scene state.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak handles authentication with RBAC. Stream keys stored in environment variables loaded at deploy time. The destination configuration API returns keys to the browser to populate the config form fields.
previous_plan.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Scene composer, 3-destination RTMP broadcaster, Keycloak auth, stream health panel. Phase 2: Overlay editor, pre-show checklist, Vault integration for stream key rotation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-2 revision fixing two blockers: the critical stream key credential exposure (keys were returned to the browser via REST API) and the RTMP destination failure isolation gap (single shared mux context crashed all destinations). Solution: Vault Agent sidecar injects keys into the streaming engine container at startup — keys never reach the browser. Per-destination Node.js child processes isolate failures so one destination drop does not affect others. Pre-show checklist promoted to MVP as a safety gate. The self-hosted Hetzner constraint rules out cloud KMS, making on-cluster Vault the correct choice.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned RTMP stream key management: keys stored in Vault KV v2 keyed by destination_id; Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar injects keys as environment variables into streaming engine pod containers at startup. The browser destination config UI now shows only platform_name, vault_key_ref label, and connection status — raw keys never appear in API responses, WebSocket frames, or browser storage. A dedicated POST /api/v1/shows/:id/destinations/:destId/rotate-key endpoint triggers Vault lease renewal server-side without exposing the new key value.
fix_report.expected_outcome: RTMP stream keys exist only in Vault and in the streaming engine pod environment memory — never in browser memory, developer tools, or network traffic. Credential rotation happens server-side via Vault lease expiry per tournament event lifecycle.

fix_report.action_taken: Refactored streaming engine to spawn one isolated Node.js child process per RTMP destination. Each worker owns its RTMP socket connection state and reconnection loop. The parent supervisor receives IPC heartbeats; on missed heartbeat the supervisor terminates and respawns the worker with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s). Worker failure emits a health event to the director UI. The compositing pipeline runs in the parent process and is unaffected by child process failures.
fix_report.expected_outcome: A single RTMP destination failure (network drop, platform-side rejection) results in that destination worker reconnecting independently while all other destinations continue streaming uninterrupted.

fix_report.action_taken: Moved pre-show checklist to Phase 1 MVP. The checklist validates source feed connectivity, overlay variable completeness, and RTMP destination test-push response before enabling the 'Go Live' button in the director UI. A checklist REST endpoint returns per-item pass/fail state for the frontend to display.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Directors cannot initiate a broadcast with misconfigured RTMP destinations or missing talent feed sources, reducing on-air incidents during live tournament events.

target_output.title: Esports Studio Broadcaster v2 — Secure Multi-Destination Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A self-hosted live streaming production platform for esports tournament organizers, built on Next.js 14 and Node.js 22 with Fastify, deployed on bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner. The platform delivers a browser-based scene composer, overlay template management, and simultaneous multi-destination RTMP broadcasting. Round-2 revision eliminates the stream key credential exposure via Vault Agent sidecar injection and replaces the shared-mux failure mode with per-destination isolated Node.js child process workers.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer architecture: a Next.js 14 App Router frontend serving the real-time scene canvas, overlay editor, and admin panel; a Fastify WebSocket control plane managing scene state via Redis pub/sub across API pods; and a streaming engine tier composed of isolated Node.js child processes, one per RTMP destination. HashiCorp Vault (on-cluster, 3-node Raft) stores RTMP stream keys and injects them via Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar into the streaming engine pod environment at container startup — keys never traverse the browser or WebSocket layer. PostgreSQL 16 with Patroni HA persists show, scene, and overlay configuration. Redis 7 Cluster maintains ephemeral stream state and pub/sub fanout. All dashboard traffic uses TLS 1.3 via NGINX Ingress; the Kubernetes cluster runs entirely on Hetzner AX102 dedicated hardware with no external cloud vendor dependencies in the broadcast hot path.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand for canvas scene state, native WebSocket client. Backend: Fastify 4 on Node.js 22, @fastify/websocket, Fastify-Keycloak-Guard for OIDC validation. Streaming Engine: Node.js 22 child_process workers wrapping FFmpeg for compositing and per-destination RTMP push. Overlay Renderer: Puppeteer 22 headless Chrome with Canvas fallback for static overlays. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with Patroni HA, Redis 7 Cluster. Infrastructure: Kubernetes 1.30 on Hetzner AX102, Helm 3, HashiCorp Vault 1.17 on-cluster, GitLab CI, Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Scene Composer: directors drag talent feed sources and overlay layer elements onto a canvas; layout saved as JSONB to stream_scenes; WebSocket scene_switch command applied to the compositor within one frame cycle at 30fps. Overlay Editor: HTML/CSS templates with variable binding schema; variables (team names, match scores, tournament bracket IDs) bound at broadcast time via admin panel. Multi-Destination Broadcaster: up to 8 simultaneous RTMP destinations each managed by an isolated child process; per-destination bitrate, dropped-frame count, and reconnect state visible on the health panel. Pre-Show Checklist: gated validation before enabling the Go Live button — checks source feed connectivity, overlay variable completeness, and RTMP destination test-push response. Admin Panel: show lifecycle management, user role assignment via Keycloak group sync, destination config (platform name and Vault key reference label only), broadcast profile selection.
target_output.system_components: NGINX Ingress (TLS 1.3 termination), Next.js 14 pods (2 replicas), Fastify API pods (2 replicas) with /ws/production WebSocket endpoint and /api/v1 REST routes, Redis 7 Cluster (3 nodes) for session store and WebSocket pub/sub, PostgreSQL Patroni cluster (primary plus synchronous replica), HashiCorp Vault (3-node Raft on-cluster) with Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar injecting stream keys into streaming engine pods, Streaming Engine pods (one per active show) running FFmpeg compositor and per-destination Node child process workers, Puppeteer Overlay Service pod with process supervisor and 2Gi memory limit, Prometheus and Grafana for metrics, Loki for log aggregation.
target_output.workflows: Scene Switch: director clicks a scene in the canvas or uses a keyboard shortcut; browser WebSocket sends scene_switch command to Fastify; SceneControlService validates director role, updates Redis scene state, publishes to pub/sub; all API pods relay the confirmation event to connected clients; streaming engine compositor applies the new layout within one 33ms compositing frame. Pre-Show Checklist: operator opens the broadcast control panel before going live; checklist service queries each source feed health endpoint, confirms overlay template variables are all bound, and sends a test RTMP probe to each configured destination; results display per-item pass/fail in the UI; the Go Live button remains disabled until all checklist items pass. RTMP Destination Failover: per-destination child process detects a write error on its RTMP socket; the worker logs the failure with destination_id and timestamp to production_audit_log; the worker enters exponential backoff reconnection (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s); the parent supervisor detects missed IPC heartbeats and respawns the worker if it exceeds 3 consecutive missed heartbeats; sibling destination workers and the compositing pipeline continue unaffected throughout.
target_output.data_model: production_shows (id UUID PK, organization_id UUID, name VARCHAR, status ENUM draft/rehearsal/live/completed, created_at, updated_at). stream_scenes (id UUID PK, show_id FK, name VARCHAR, layout_json JSONB, is_default BOOL, created_at). overlay_templates (id UUID PK, show_id FK, name VARCHAR, template_html TEXT, variable_schema_json JSONB, created_at). rtmp_destinations (id UUID PK, show_id FK, platform_name VARCHAR, vault_key_ref VARCHAR, priority INT, status ENUM active/failed/reconnecting, last_tested_at). broadcast_profiles (id UUID PK, show_id FK, resolution VARCHAR, bitrate_kbps INT, keyframe_interval INT, codec VARCHAR). stream_health_metrics (id UUID PK, destination_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, bitrate_kbps INT, dropped_frames INT, rtt_ms INT) partitioned by day, 30-day retention. production_audit_log (id UUID PK, show_id FK, operator_id UUID, action VARCHAR, payload JSONB, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: WebSocket /ws/production: typed command objects scene_switch, go_live, end_broadcast, source_update — role guard validates Keycloak claims before dispatch. REST: GET/POST /api/v1/shows, GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE /api/v1/shows/:id/scenes, GET/POST/PATCH /api/v1/shows/:id/overlays, GET/POST/DELETE /api/v1/shows/:id/destinations (returns platform_name and status only, never raw keys), POST /api/v1/shows/:id/destinations/:destId/rotate-key (triggers Vault lease renewal server-side), GET /api/v1/shows/:id/checklist (per-item pass/fail state), GET /api/v1/health/destinations (per-destination bitrate, dropped frames, reconnect count).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Stream Key Isolation: RTMP keys stored in Vault KV v2 per destination_id; Vault Agent DaemonSet sidecar injects keys as environment variables into streaming engine pod containers at startup; keys never appear in API responses, WebSocket frames, or browser storage. Authentication: Keycloak OIDC with realm per organization; Fastify middleware validates JWT on every request and WebSocket upgrade; director role requires TOTP MFA. Authorization: role guards on REST routes and WebSocket command types — only director can issue go_live, end_broadcast, rotate_key. Audit Logging: every scene_switch, go_live, and key rotation event written to production_audit_log with operator_id and occurred_at for post-broadcast forensic review. Transport: TLS 1.3 via NGINX Ingress with cert-manager ACME certificates; RTMP push uses RTMPS where the destination platform supports it.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Kubernetes 1.30 on 3 Hetzner AX102 dedicated nodes: nodes 1 and 2 host Next.js, Fastify, Vault, Redis, and Patroni pods; node 3 is tainted for streaming engine and Puppeteer workloads to prevent CPU contention. GitLab CI pipeline: ESLint and Prettier lint, Vitest unit tests with 75% coverage gate, Docker build and push to self-hosted Harbor registry, Helm chart upgrade with --atomic flag (auto-rollback on failed readiness probes within 5 minutes). Environments: staging on Hetzner CX31 single-node minikube; production on 3-node cluster. Postgres HA via Patroni with dedicated etcd cluster for leader election; synchronous replica ensures zero RPO for show config. Streaming engine pods use preStop lifecycle hook to drain RTMP connections gracefully during rolling updates. Vault Raft storage provides on-cluster HA without external dependencies.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Fastify /metrics (HTTP request rate, WebSocket connection count, scene-switch command latency histogram) and streaming engine custom metrics (per-destination bitrate_kbps gauge, dropped_frame_rate counter, reconnect_count counter). Grafana dashboard 'Active Broadcast' shows live destination health cards, compositor frame rate, and WebSocket client count. Grafana dashboard 'Show History' shows completed broadcast health timelines per destination. Loki aggregates structured JSON logs from all pods with show_id and destination_id labels. Alertmanager pages on-call via PagerDuty if any destination bitrate drops more than 20% sustained for 30 seconds, or if the streaming engine pod restarts during a scheduled live broadcast window.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hetzner AX102 dedicated servers at approximately 79 EUR per month each — 3 nodes totalling 237 EUR per month, significantly cheaper than equivalent cloud compute for always-on streaming workloads. Redis Cluster, Vault, and Patroni running on existing nodes eliminate managed service fees. Scale path: provision a 4th Hetzner node tainted for streaming engine capacity when concurrent show count exceeds 2; Redis Cluster rebalances shards automatically on node addition.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): scene composer with JSONB layout persistence, 3-destination RTMP broadcaster with per-destination child process isolation, pre-show checklist gate, Keycloak RBAC auth with director MFA, Vault stream key management with Vault Agent sidecar, Grafana/Prometheus/Loki observability, GitLab CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (6 weeks): overlay template editor with variable binding, Puppeteer headless overlay renderer integrated into compositor, stream_health_metrics time-series dashboard with per-destination history, show archive and audit log viewer. Phase 3 (6 weeks): live tournament data API integration for automated score and bracket overlay population, replay clip extraction from broadcast recording buffer, remote talent IFB audio routing module.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced in both Next.js and Fastify codebases. ESLint with @typescript-eslint and import-order rules enforced in GitLab CI lint stage. Vitest for unit tests covering scene state machine transitions, checklist validation logic, and destination health aggregation with 75% coverage gate. Playwright E2E tests cover scene-switch flow, pre-show checklist pass and fail states, and destination config form submission. Streaming engine changes require manual QA with live RTMP test destinations before merge to main. ADR documents required for streaming pipeline architecture decisions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Puppeteer overlay process memory leak during broadcasts exceeding 8 hours. Mitigation: Puppeteer pod has a 2Gi memory limit with liveness probe triggering restart; overlay render frame rate capped at 30fps. Risk: Keycloak single realm per organization limits multi-tenant isolation at the identity layer. Tradeoff accepted for MVP simplicity; per-event realm isolation is a post-MVP improvement. Risk: Hetzner bare-metal hardware failure without cloud failover path. Mitigation: Patroni synchronous replication provides zero-RPO data HA; a pre-provisioned spare Hetzner node can rejoin the cluster within 10 minutes via Ansible playbook. Tradeoff: on-cluster Vault adds operational overhead versus cloud KMS, but is required by the no-external-cloud-dependency constraint in the broadcast hot path.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Vault over deploy-time environment variable secrets to enable per-event key rotation without container redeployment. Chose Node.js child_process workers over separate Kubernetes pods for per-destination RTMP workers to keep IPC latency under 1ms for frame-sync signaling between compositor and workers. Confirmed Puppeteer as primary overlay renderer with Canvas-based fallback for static templates to reduce headless Chrome resource usage during non-animated scenes.

metadata.notes: Esports live production platform on self-hosted Hetzner Kubernetes; round-2 fixes critical RTMP stream key exposure via Vault sidecar injection and per-destination worker isolation.

project_goal.value: Marketing landing page for a cloud-based live streaming infrastructure product targeting enterprise broadcasters, featuring dynamic ROI calculators that query real-time pricing data and bandwidth benchmarks
project_goal.rationale: Drive enterprise inbound leads through quantified ROI demonstration

target_users.value: Enterprise broadcast engineers and IT procurement teams evaluating live streaming CDN infrastructure
target_users.rationale: B2B audience comparing TCO of on-premise vs cloud streaming infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Marketing page with no backend; dynamic calculator uses client-side logic and Edge Config for pricing data

capabilities.rationale: Static delivery with client-side interactivity and conversion analytics; no auth or backend compute

complexity_level.rationale: Static page with React island components for the calculator and feature comparison table

risk_level.rationale: No user data stored on the page; lead data forwarded to Salesforce only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Public marketing content; leads are sent directly to Salesforce with no local persistence

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing page with no access restrictions

access_model.value: Fully public, no authentication required

feature_scope.rationale: Full marketing conversion page for enterprise audience

mvp_scope.value: Hero, ROI calculator, bandwidth benchmark chart, lead capture form, cookie consent banner
mvp_scope.rationale: Core conversion elements sufficient for initial campaign

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel; CSP headers; no server-side form processing; lead data posted directly to Salesforce webhook from client
security_baseline.rationale: Static hosting security baseline; no user data handled server-side

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with React islands and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro island architecture delivers fast static shell with interactive React components only where needed

backend_stack.value: None; static page with Vercel Edge Config for dynamic pricing data
backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime; pricing JSON served from Vercel Edge Config for near-zero latency reads

hosting_target.rationale: Optimal for Astro static output with edge-side rendering for dynamic pricing reads

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals and page performance; Segment for conversion event tracking; Google Analytics 4 for attribution
observability_baseline.rationale: Marketing team requires multi-channel attribution data for campaign optimization

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment from main; content updates via Vercel Edge Config without redeploy
execution_preference.rationale: Marketing team needs to iterate quickly on pricing and copy without engineering deployments

project.target_users: Enterprise broadcast engineers and IT procurement teams
project.timeline: Live for major industry conference launch; ongoing content iteration

frontend.framework: Astro 4 with React 18 islands for interactive components
frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with brand design tokens
frontend.calculator: React island fetching pricing JSON from Vercel Edge Config and computing projected savings client-side

security.consent: GDPR cookie consent banner blocking Segment and GA4 until user accepts

data.pricing_config: Vercel Edge Config key: pricing-tiers JSON with per-GB CDN cost and committed bandwidth tiers
data.benchmark_data: Static JSON file in repo updated by marketing team
data.lead_form: POST to Salesforce Web-to-Lead webhook from client

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: build Astro, Lighthouse CI check, deploy to Vercel
devops.environments: Production on main; preview per PR

constraints.pricing_updates: Via Vercel Edge Config dashboard without redeploy

reasoner_reviews.product: The ROI calculator is the highest-value conversion element for an enterprise audience comparing CDN costs. Fetching pricing JSON from Vercel Edge Config rather than hardcoding it is the right call -- pricing changes frequently during sales cycles and marketing cannot wait for a full redeploy. The lead form posting directly to Salesforce Web-to-Lead is standard for this funnel stage.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro island architecture is ideal here: the static shell loads with near-zero JS budget, and only the calculator and form components hydrate as React islands. Edge Config reads from within the Astro SSR context at request time give sub-millisecond pricing data access at the edge. The benchmark chart should use a pre-rendered SVG or Recharts island rather than a heavy charting library to protect the Lighthouse score.
reasoner_reviews.security: The GDPR cookie consent banner must block Segment and GA4 script initialization until explicit accept. The Salesforce Web-to-Lead endpoint should be proxied through a Vercel Edge Function to hide the full Salesforce org URL and add basic bot honeypot validation. CSP must allowlist Salesforce, Segment, and GA4 domains.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving Lighthouse performance above 95 on mobile requires: Astro static output with no unused JS in the critical path, image optimization via Astro Image component, font subsetting for the brand typeface, and deferring all analytics scripts until after consent. Lighthouse CI in GitHub Actions will gate merges that regress the score below 95.

security.auth_design: No authentication; fully public marketing page
security.abuse_prevention: Salesforce lead form protected by honeypot field (hidden input that bots fill but humans ignore); Vercel Edge Function wrapper on Salesforce webhook validates Origin header
security.privacy_controls: GDPR consent banner using @consent-manager/core blocks Segment and GA4 initialization until explicit accept; consent state stored in localStorage; no analytics cookies set for non-consenting visitors
security.security_risks: Risk: Salesforce Web-to-Lead org ID exposed in client-side form action -- mitigated by proxying through Vercel Edge Function

data.entities: pricing_tiers (tier_name STRING e.g. starter/business/enterprise, per_gb_cost_usd DECIMAL, committed_bandwidth_tbps DECIMAL, support_tier STRING -- stored in Vercel Edge Config), benchmark_data_points (scenario STRING, incumbent_cost DECIMAL, product_cost DECIMAL, latency_ms INT -- static JSON in /src/data/benchmarks.json), roi_calculator_inputs (monthly_stream_hours DECIMAL, concurrent_viewers INT, current_cdn_spend DECIMAL -- ephemeral client state), lead_capture_forms (company STRING, email STRING, monthly_spend_range STRING, use_case STRING -- forwarded to Salesforce), feature_comparison_tables (feature_name STRING, product_supported BOOL, competitor_a_supported BOOL -- static JSON)
data.storage_design: Vercel Edge Config for pricing JSON (updated by marketing without redeploy); all other content in static JSON files in /src/data/ committed to Git
data.retention_and_deletion: No data retained on the page; lead form submissions forwarded directly to Salesforce CRM

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions builds Astro static output, runs Lighthouse CI against Vercel preview URL, deploys to Vercel Edge Network on passing score
devops.environments: Production: streaming-infra.example.com on main; Preview: Vercel auto-URLs per PR branch
devops.ci_cd_design: Steps: bun install, bun run build (Astro), deploy preview with vercel deploy, run Lighthouse CI against preview URL with --assert performance=95 mobile threshold, promote to production with vercel deploy --prod on main
devops.cost_controls: Vercel Pro ~$20/month; Segment free tier for lead volume; no database cost; Vercel Edge Config included in Vercel Pro

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 static page with React islands for ROI calculator and interactive CDN spend comparison; anchor-based single-page layout

frontend.frontend_risks: ROI calculator results shown to enterprise prospects; ensure calculations are accurate and include clear methodology footnotes to avoid misleading claims

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 of a landing page for enterprise live streaming infrastructure. ISS-001 (consent) and ISS-002 (hardcoded pricing) are resolved. The focus issue ISS-003 is the Salesforce org URL exposed in the lead form action. Fix: introduce a Vercel Edge Function at /api/submit-lead that proxies to Salesforce, validates a honeypot field, and hides the org URL. This keeps the architecture static while solving the exposure.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced direct Salesforce Web-to-Lead form action with a POST to /api/submit-lead Vercel Edge Function. The Edge Function validates the honeypot field, reads the Salesforce Web-to-Lead URL and org ID from Vercel environment variables, and proxies the validated lead payload to Salesforce. The Salesforce org URL is never present in the static page source.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-003 resolved: Salesforce org URL removed from client-side page source; honeypot validation reduces spam lead volume; architecture remains effectively static with one lightweight Edge Function handling form submission.

target_output.title: Enterprise Live Streaming Infrastructure Landing Page v3
target_output.executive_summary: Astro 4 marketing landing page on Vercel Edge Network with React island components for the ROI calculator and enterprise lead capture form. Pricing tier data is served from Vercel Edge Config enabling marketing team updates without redeployment. GDPR cookie consent banner blocks Segment and GA4 initialization until explicit visitor consent. Lead form submissions are proxied through a Vercel Edge Function at /api/submit-lead that validates a honeypot field and forwards qualified leads to Salesforce without exposing the org URL in page source.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 generates a static HTML shell at build time with React islands hydrated client-side for the ROI calculator and lead capture form. The calculator island fetches the pricing-tiers JSON from Vercel Edge Config at component mount, computes projected CDN savings client-side, and renders results without a database round trip. The lead form island posts to /api/submit-lead, a Vercel Edge Function that validates the honeypot field, reads the Salesforce Web-to-Lead URL from a Vercel environment variable (not visible to clients), and proxies the form payload to Salesforce. Segment and GA4 are initialized only after the consent banner accept event fires, with consent state persisted to localStorage. GitHub Actions runs Lighthouse CI against each Vercel preview deployment before promoting to production.
target_output.technology_stack: Static Site: Astro 4 with React 18 islands, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS. Dynamic Pricing: Vercel Edge Config (pricing-tiers key, typed JSON schema). Lead Proxy: Vercel Edge Function (TypeScript, Web API fetch). Consent: @consent-manager/core blocking Segment and GA4. Analytics: Segment for conversion events, GA4 for attribution, Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals. Charts: Recharts as a React island for bandwidth benchmark visualization. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI asserting mobile performance score above 95. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: headline, product positioning statement, primary CTA button linking to lead form anchor. ROI Calculator Island: inputs for current monthly CDN spend (USD) and monthly stream hours; fetches pricing-tiers from Vercel Edge Config on mount; computes projected annual savings and payback period; displays breakdown chart. Bandwidth Benchmark Chart: Recharts island rendering pre-loaded benchmark_data_points comparing latency and cost-per-TB across three CDN scenarios. Feature Comparison Table: static Astro component from /src/data/features.json comparing product vs. two incumbents across ten criteria. Lead Capture Form Island: company, email, monthly spend range, use case fields plus hidden honeypot; submits to /api/submit-lead; shows inline success confirmation. Cookie Consent Banner: blocks Segment and GA4 until accept; respects navigator.doNotTrack.
target_output.system_components: Astro 4 Build: generates optimized static HTML from MDX and component files. Vercel Edge Config: stores pricing-tiers JSON readable by the calculator island without a server roundtrip. Vercel Edge Function /api/submit-lead: validates honeypot, reads SALESFORCE_WEBTOLEAD_URL from env, proxies lead payload. Vercel Edge Network: serves static assets from 40+ PoPs with automatic cache invalidation on deploy. Segment + GA4: loaded only post-consent for conversion and attribution tracking. Salesforce CRM: receives validated lead payloads from the Edge Function proxy.
target_output.workflows: Lead Capture Workflow: visitor fills the form with company and contact details plus monthly spend range and use case; the React island submits a POST to /api/submit-lead with a hidden honeypot field; the Edge Function checks the honeypot (non-empty = bot, returns 400), reads SALESFORCE_WEBTOLEAD_URL from Vercel env, and POSTs the payload to Salesforce Web-to-Lead; on Salesforce 200 response the Edge Function returns success JSON; the form island shows an inline confirmation message and fires a Segment lead_submitted event. Pricing Update Workflow: marketing manager logs into the Vercel dashboard, navigates to Edge Config, updates the pricing-tiers JSON key with new per-GB costs and committed bandwidth tiers; change propagates to all edge replicas within seconds; next calculator island load fetches updated pricing without any site redeploy.
target_output.data_model: pricing_tiers (stored in Vercel Edge Config as JSON: tier_name STRING e.g. starter/business/enterprise, per_gb_cost_usd DECIMAL, committed_bandwidth_tbps DECIMAL, support_tier STRING, contract_term_months INT). benchmark_data_points (scenario STRING e.g. 'peak-sports-4K', incumbent_cost_per_tb DECIMAL, product_cost_per_tb DECIMAL, p50_latency_ms INT, p99_latency_ms INT -- /src/data/benchmarks.json). feature_comparison_tables (feature_name STRING, product_supported BOOL, competitor_a_supported BOOL, competitor_b_supported BOOL -- /src/data/features.json). lead_capture_forms (company STRING, email STRING, monthly_spend_range ENUM <10k/10k-50k/50k-250k/>250k, use_case STRING, utm_source STRING -- ephemeral; forwarded to Salesforce and not retained).
target_output.api_design: Single Edge Function endpoint: POST /api/submit-lead. Request body (JSON): {company: string, email: string, monthly_spend_range: string, use_case: string, _honeypot: string (must be empty)}. Validation: if _honeypot non-empty return 400 {error: 'invalid_submission'}. Forward to Salesforce: fetch(process.env.SALESFORCE_WEBTOLEAD_URL, {method: POST, body: URLSearchParams({oid: process.env.SALESFORCE_OID, email, company, ...})}). Success: return 200 {status: 'submitted'}. Error: return 502 {error: 'submission_failed'}. The Salesforce URL and org ID are read from Vercel environment variables and never present in the static build or page source.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Salesforce Proxy: SALESFORCE_WEBTOLEAD_URL and SALESFORCE_OID stored in Vercel environment variables; never embedded in static build; Edge Function proxies all submissions. Honeypot: hidden _honeypot field; bot submissions rejected with 400. GDPR: @consent-manager/core blocks Segment and GA4 script loading until explicit consent; consent stored in localStorage; navigator.doNotTrack respected as default-deny. CSP: default-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' https://*.vercel.app https://cdn.segment.com https://www.google-analytics.com; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. HTTPS: enforced by Vercel with HSTS max-age=31536000.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow on push to main or PR: install bun dependencies, run bun run build (Astro), deploy preview with vercel deploy (outputs preview URL), run Lighthouse CI with lhci autorun against preview URL asserting mobile performance score greater than or equal to 95 and blocking merge if assertion fails, on main branch run vercel deploy --prod. Vercel environment variables: SALESFORCE_WEBTOLEAD_URL and SALESFORCE_OID set in Vercel project settings (not in repository). Pricing updates: Vercel Edge Config dashboard only, no CI involvement. Rollback: Vercel one-click rollback to any prior deployment. Domain: streaming-infra.example.com with Vercel-managed TLS.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics: Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per page, geographic breakdown, device type split -- all cookie-free. Segment (post-consent): roi_calculator_viewed, roi_calculation_completed (with projected_savings_usd), lead_form_started, lead_submitted conversion events forwarded to GA4 and Salesforce campaign source. GA4: attribution channel reports for campaign ROI. GitHub Actions: Lighthouse CI score and performance budget results posted as PR check; Slack notification on production deploy failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro: ~$20/month covering custom domain, team access, and analytics. Edge Functions: included in Vercel Pro with generous invocation limits at expected lead form submission volume. Vercel Edge Config: included in Vercel Pro. Segment: free tier sufficient for landing page event volume. Total: approximately $20/month with no per-visitor scaling cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Launch (3 weeks): Astro 4 scaffold with Tailwind brand theme, hero section, ROI calculator island with Vercel Edge Config pricing integration, bandwidth benchmark Recharts island, lead form island posting to /api/submit-lead Edge Function with Salesforce proxy and honeypot, GDPR consent banner, Lighthouse CI gate in GitHub Actions. Phase 2 - Conversion Optimization (2 weeks): feature comparison table, customer case study quote section, Segment conversion event tracking post-consent, GA4 attribution setup. Phase 3 - Growth (ongoing): A/B testing via Vercel Experimentation for headline and CTA copy variants, EMEA localization scaffold, video testimonials section.
target_output.development_guidelines: All page sections authored as Astro components; no inline scripts in HTML. React islands used only for genuinely interactive components (calculator, form, chart). Environment variables never hardcoded -- Salesforce credentials exclusively via Vercel project settings. Lighthouse CI score must not regress below 95 on mobile; PR is blocked if it does. Prettier and ESLint enforced in CI. Consent banner behavior tested manually in EU locale simulation (navigator.language = 'de-DE') before each production deploy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Vercel Edge Config read latency causes a flash of loading state in the calculator island on slow connections. Mitigation: store last-known pricing in localStorage as a stale-while-revalidate cache so the calculator renders immediately with cached values while fetching fresh data. Risk: Salesforce Web-to-Lead rate limits could cause 429 errors for the /api/submit-lead proxy during high-traffic campaign periods. Mitigation: Edge Function returns 429 with Retry-After header and the form island shows a friendly retry message. Tradeoff: Vercel Edge Function for form proxy adds a lightweight server-side component to an otherwise fully static page -- acceptable given the security benefit of hiding the Salesforce org URL.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-003 resolved by introducing Vercel Edge Function proxy for lead form submission. Confirmed Vercel Edge Config over a separate pricing API endpoint for sub-millisecond read latency without a dedicated service. Selected @consent-manager/core over a CMP SaaS vendor to avoid third-party script loading overhead. Confirmed Recharts over Chart.js for the benchmark visualization as a React island for smaller bundle and TypeScript-first API.

metadata.notes: Round 3 landing page revision fixing Salesforce org URL exposure via Vercel Edge Function proxy; previous rounds resolved GDPR consent and hardcoded pricing issues.

project_goal.value: Scalable multi-tenant OTT platform backend for independent content publishers to launch branded streaming channels, manage subscriber tiers, and monetize content via subscriptions and pay-per-view
project_goal.rationale: Core business: enable publishers to self-serve branded channel launch with full monetization without custom engineering

target_users.value: Independent content publishers (tenant admins) and their end subscribers
target_users.rationale: Two-sided platform: publishers configure and operators subscribe on publisher-branded channels

project_class.rationale: Requires Next.js publisher admin panel, subscriber-facing channel pages, and Node/Fastify API with Stripe integration

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, payments, data, analytics, admin_panel, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Full monetization platform requires subscriber auth, Stripe payments, real-time entitlement, analytics dashboard, and admin panel for publishers

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant data isolation, Stripe webhook reliability, concurrent playback authorization, and revenue reconciliation at scale

risk_level.rationale: Financial transaction processing, PCI-DSS scope, multi-tenant data isolation failures could expose subscriber payment data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores subscriber payment method tokens, transaction records, revenue splits, and VAT receipts

external_exposure.rationale: Subscriber-facing channel pages are publicly accessible; publisher admin panel requires authentication

access_model.value: Auth0 multi-tenant organizations: each publisher is an Auth0 organization; subscribers authenticate per-channel with tenant-scoped JWTs
access_model.rationale: Auth0 organizations provide native multi-tenant isolation without custom identity infrastructure

feature_scope.value: Publisher channel setup, subscription tier configuration, Stripe product sync, subscriber registration and billing, PPV purchase flow, entitlement cache, revenue dashboard, monthly payout reconciliation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete publisher monetization lifecycle from channel launch through payout

mvp_scope.value: Single subscription tier per channel, Stripe Checkout for signup, entitlement-gated stream token issuance, publisher revenue dashboard, monthly reconciliation report
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates core monetization loop before multi-tier and PPV complexity

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3, Stripe tokenization (no raw card data touches our servers), Auth0 MFA for publisher admins, tenant-scoped database rows with RLS, Stripe webhook HMAC validation
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS SAQ-A scope requires Stripe-hosted forms; RLS prevents cross-tenant data leakage

frontend_stack.rationale: Server components for SEO on subscriber-facing channel pages; App Router for publisher admin panel

backend_stack.rationale: Fastify plugin system cleanly separates tenant routing, auth middleware, and Stripe webhook handling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security for multi-tenant isolation; Redis 7 for entitlement cache and stream token TTL
data_platform.rationale: RLS enforces tenant boundaries at the database layer; Redis entitlement cache keeps playback authorization latency under 20 ms

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container scaling without Kubernetes operational overhead; ECS service auto-scaling handles subscriber traffic spikes

privacy_retention_policy.value: GDPR: subscriber personal data deletion within 30 days of account closure; transaction records retained 7 years for tax compliance; EU data stays in eu-west-1
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 right to erasure; VAT audit trail requires 7-year financial record retention

future_scope.value: Multi-CDN stream delivery with geo-restrictions, ad-supported free tier (AVOD), white-label mobile SDK for publishers
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 revenue expansion after core monetization is validated

constraints.value: Stripe as sole payment processor for PCI-DSS SAQ-A eligibility; EU subscriber data must not leave eu-west-1
constraints.rationale: PCI SAQ-A requires Stripe-hosted payment fields; data residency required for GDPR compliance

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with Fastify plugin, custom metrics for subscription churn rate and Stripe webhook delivery lag, PagerDuty for payment processing failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Payment failures and Stripe webhook delivery delays require immediate alerting to prevent revenue loss

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; MVP subscription flow in 10 weeks; PPV and multi-tier in weeks 11-16
execution_preference.rationale: Core monetization loop must be validated before expanding to PPV complexity

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ-A via Stripe-hosted Checkout (no raw card data); GDPR for EU subscriber data; VAT compliance for digital goods sold in EU via Stripe Tax; GDPR Article 20 data portability for subscriber account export
compliance_context.rationale: Stripe SAQ-A scope eliminates PCI server-side controls; GDPR and VAT obligations remain our responsibility

project.goal: Multi-tenant OTT monetization platform for independent publishers
project.target_users: Content publishers (admins) and their channel subscribers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, PPV in week 16

frontend.approach: App Router; server components for subscriber channel pages for SEO; client components for admin panel interactions

backend.framework: Fastify with plugin-based tenant routing

security.auth: Auth0 multi-tenant organizations
security.stripe: Stripe Checkout hosted pages, HMAC webhook validation

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS per tenant_id
data.cache: Redis 7 for entitlement cache and stream tokens

reasoner_reviews.product: The platform correctly separates publisher configuration from subscriber experience. Subscriber tier configuration syncing to Stripe products is the most error-prone workflow and needs idempotent retry logic. Revenue reconciliation needs to handle Stripe payout timing differences from subscription billing cycles.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify plugin architecture cleanly maps to multi-tenant concerns: a tenant-resolution plugin sets request context before auth middleware fires, downstream handlers read tenant_id from context without per-route boilerplate. PostgreSQL RLS with tenant_id set_config per connection is the correct isolation mechanism at this scale - separate schemas per tenant would complicate migrations.
reasoner_reviews.security: Redis entitlement cache must be keyed by tenant_id:subscriber_id:content_id to prevent cross-tenant cache pollution. Stream tokens must be short-lived JWTs (15 min) signed with a per-tenant secret so a token from one channel cannot authorize playback on another tenant. Stripe webhook HMAC verification must occur before any database operation to prevent replay attacks.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Stripe SAQ-A eligibility is maintained only if card data never touches our servers. Any future inline card form would trigger SAQ-D scope. EU data residency must be enforced at the database connection pool level, not just at routing, to prevent accidental cross-region writes.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router with two route groups: (subscriber) for public channel pages rendered as server components for SEO, and (admin) for publisher dashboard using client components with React Query for state. Tenant resolution via subdomain or path prefix middleware.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Subscriber: /[channel]/home (server-rendered catalog), /[channel]/watch/[content_id] (entitlement-gated player), /[channel]/subscribe (Stripe Checkout redirect), /[channel]/account (subscription management). Admin: /admin/dashboard (revenue overview), /admin/content (library management), /admin/tiers (subscription plan config), /admin/subscribers (subscriber list with churn metrics).
frontend.state_management: React Query for admin dashboard server state (revenue metrics, subscriber counts). Zustand for transient UI state in content library editor. Server actions for tier configuration updates to avoid extra API round trips.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: Subscriber pages never expose entitlement logic client-side; stream token fetch happens server-side in a Next.js route handler. Admin panel protected by Auth0 organization membership check in middleware.

backend.service_design: Fastify monolith with plugin-based domain separation: @tenant (resolution and context), @auth (Auth0 JWT verification), @stripe (webhook handling and product sync), @entitlement (cache-first authorization), @analytics (event aggregation). Single deployable unit sized for current scale with module boundaries for future extraction.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful endpoints versioned at /api/v1. Stripe webhook handler at /webhooks/stripe validates HMAC before processing. Idempotency keys required on subscription creation and PPV purchase endpoints. Entitlement endpoint GET /api/v1/entitlement/{content_id} returns stream token or 403 with denial reason.
backend.background_jobs: Monthly reconciliation: Lambda function triggered by EventBridge on 1st of month, pulls Stripe payout events, computes net amounts per tenant after platform commission, writes payout_statements records, triggers SES email delivery to publisher. Stripe product sync: SQS queue of tier change events processed by worker that calls Stripe Products and Prices API with idempotency keys.
backend.failure_handling: Stripe webhook idempotency: webhook events stored in stripe_events table; duplicate webhook_id returns 200 immediately. Subscription creation failures trigger compensating transaction to cancel Stripe subscription if database write fails. Entitlement cache miss falls through to PostgreSQL with 50 ms timeout before returning 503.

security.auth_design: Auth0 organizations: each publisher is an organization; subscribers are Auth0 users scoped to the publisher organization. Fastify auth plugin extracts org_id from JWT and sets tenant_id in request context. Publisher admin actions require org:admin role claim.
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL RLS policies: all subscriber, transaction, and content tables have tenant_id column with policy `USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid)`. Application sets `SET LOCAL app.tenant_id = ?` at query start.
security.secrets_management: Auth0 client secrets and Stripe API keys in AWS Secrets Manager. Stripe webhook signing secrets per environment. Per-tenant stream token signing keys stored in Secrets Manager and cached in-process for 5 minutes.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All payment events (subscription created, PPV purchased, payout initiated) written to payment_audit_log with stripe_event_id, tenant_id, amount, currency, and processing timestamp. Retained 7 years in S3 Glacier via lifecycle policy.

data.entities: tenant_channels (id, slug, publisher_id, stripe_account_id, created_at), subscription_plans (id, tenant_id, name, stripe_product_id, stripe_price_id, interval, amount_cents, trial_days, created_at), subscriber_accounts (id, tenant_id, auth0_user_id, email, stripe_customer_id, created_at), ppv_transactions (id, tenant_id, subscriber_id, content_id, stripe_payment_intent_id, amount_cents, currency, status ENUM pending/succeeded/refunded, created_at), entitlement_grants (id, tenant_id, subscriber_id, plan_id, valid_from, valid_until, grant_type ENUM subscription/ppv/trial), stream_tokens (id, tenant_id, subscriber_id, content_id, jwt_token, expires_at, created_at), revenue_splits (id, tenant_id, period_start, period_end, gross_revenue_cents, platform_commission_cents, net_payout_cents, stripe_payout_id).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ with RLS. Redis 7 ElastiCache for entitlement cache (key: ent:{tenant_id}:{subscriber_id}:{content_id}, TTL 5 min) and stream tokens (key: tok:{token_id}, TTL 15 min).
data.consistency_notes: Stripe webhook events are the source of truth for subscription status. Local subscriber_accounts.subscription_status is a cache invalidated on webhook receipt, not authoritative. Entitlement decisions fall back to PostgreSQL on Redis miss.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service with auto-scaling (CPU target 60%, min 2 tasks, max 30). ALB with HTTPS listener. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster. Two ECS services: API service and background worker service (reconciliation, Stripe sync SQS consumer).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: TypeScript tsc --noEmit, ESLint, Jest unit tests with pg-mem and ioredis mock, integration tests against docker-compose (Postgres + Redis + Stripe mock via stripe-mock), Docker build, push ECR, ECS rolling deploy. Blue/green on production via CodeDeploy with canary 10% traffic shift.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with Fastify plugin. Custom metrics: subscription_mrr_delta per tenant (gauge), stripe_webhook_processing_lag_ms (histogram), entitlement_cache_hit_rate (gauge), stream_token_issued_total (counter). PagerDuty P1 for Stripe webhook processing lag > 30 seconds.
devops.cost_controls: Reserved Fargate capacity for baseline 2 tasks. RDS Reserved Instance 1-year for primary. ElastiCache Reserved Node. Stripe webhook retry backoff reduces duplicate processing costs. S3 Intelligent-Tiering for audit log archival.

ISS-001.title: Entitlement cache keyed without tenant_id allows cross-tenant cache pollution
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan used key ent:{subscriber_id}:{content_id} without tenant scoping, which would return entitlement grants from a different tenant for a subscriber with the same auth0 ID on a different channel

history.note: Cache key design missing tenant scoping
Key updated to ent:{tenant_id}:{subscriber_id}:{content_id} in data model and backend specialist plan

ISS-002.title: Stream tokens not scoped per tenant, token from one channel could authorize another
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 stream tokens were signed with a global key, not per-tenant keys, allowing a valid token from channel A to potentially authorize content on channel B if the player did not validate tenant claim

history.note: Single global signing key for stream tokens
Per-tenant signing keys implemented with keys stored in Secrets Manager

ISS-003.title: Revenue reconciliation does not handle Stripe payout timing offset for mid-month subscriptions
ISS-003.detail: The monthly reconciliation Lambda sums all succeeded payment_intent events in the calendar month, but Stripe payouts can include transactions from the prior month due to T+2 settlement. This causes payout statements to show mismatched amounts versus Stripe payout totals

history.note: Reconciliation uses calendar month window instead of Stripe payout window

focus_issues.title: Revenue reconciliation does not handle Stripe payout timing offset for mid-month subscriptions
focus_issues.detail: The monthly reconciliation Lambda sums all succeeded payment_intent events in the calendar month, but Stripe payouts include transactions from the prior month due to T+2 settlement, causing mismatched payout statement amounts

latest_recommendations: Align reconciliation Lambda to iterate Stripe payout objects directly rather than calendar-month transaction windows, so each payout statement maps 1:1 to a Stripe payout_id
Add stripe_payout_id as a foreign key on revenue_splits to enable exact reconciliation against Stripe balance transactions

Redis entitlement cache keyed by ent:{tenant_id}:{subscriber_id}:{content_id}. Stream tokens signed with per-tenant keys from Secrets Manager. Stripe Checkout for subscriber payment. Monthly Lambda reconciliation.

best_plan.executive_summary: Same as previous_plan; best score 8.1 achieved in round 2.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 frontend with Fastify API on ECS Fargate. PostgreSQL 16 with RLS. Redis tenant-scoped entitlement cache. Per-tenant stream token signing keys. Stripe Checkout and Billing.
