previous_plan.title: Broadcast Signal Monitor v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A WPF desktop application on .NET 8 for broadcast engineers to monitor live satellite and terrestrial feed signal quality parameters in real time, replacing legacy hardware monitoring terminals with a software-defined dashboard backed by local SQLite telemetry storage.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: WPF application with an in-process SignalR hub bridging a background SNMP polling service to the UI. Signal quality metrics (MER, C/N, BER) are polled every 500ms via SharpSnmpLib and DVB-API calls, stored in SQLite, and displayed as live bar meters and historical charts.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Windows Authentication via Active Directory for role-based access. SNMP community string stored in App.config. Alarm configuration changes logged to Windows Event Log.
previous_plan.phased_implementation: Phase 1: carrier monitor grid, real-time meters, alarm thresholds, alarm ack. Phase 2: shift handover report, maintenance log, SLA compliance report, SCCM deployment.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-2 revision for a WPF broadcast signal monitoring app. Two issues to address: SNMP community string must be encrypted with Windows DPAPI rather than stored in plaintext App.config, and the shift handover report feature needs to be fully specified in workflows, data model, and implementation sections. Plan quality is intentionally weak — several sections meet minimum word counts but lack deep detail, consistent with the manifest plan_quality designation.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced plaintext App.config credential storage with Windows DPAPI encryption. The SNMP community string is encrypted on first configuration using ProtectedData.Protect(DataProtectionScope.LocalMachine) and stored as a Base64 ciphertext value in the application user settings file. Decryption occurs in-process at polling service startup via ProtectedData.Unprotect. The community string never appears in plaintext on disk.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SNMP credentials at rest are encrypted by the Windows DPAPI machine-scope key, accessible only to processes running on the same Windows machine under the local SYSTEM account, meeting facility security policy requirements.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified the shift handover report feature: a UI toolbar button triggers a query aggregating alarm_records and alarm_acknowledgements for a configurable 8-hour or 12-hour window, computing per-carrier outage duration and mean time to acknowledge. The report is formatted using QuestPDF and exported as a PDF file saved to a configurable network share path. The data model now includes an engineer_shifts table to track shift boundaries.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Engineers can generate a PDF shift handover report summarizing signal incidents and acknowledgement times for the preceding shift, replacing manual handover notes from the hardware terminal era.

target_output.title: Broadcast Signal Monitor v2 — DPAPI Credentials and Handover Report
target_output.executive_summary: A WPF desktop application on .NET 8 for broadcast engineers monitoring live satellite and terrestrial feed signal quality at playout facilities, replacing aging hardware terminals. The application polls up to 50 carriers every 500ms via SNMP and DVB-API, displays real-time MER, C/N, and BER metrics with configurable alarm thresholds, and stores a rolling 90-day telemetry history in local SQLite. Round-2 revision encrypts SNMP credentials with Windows DPAPI and specifies the shift handover PDF report workflow.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process WPF application on .NET 8 using a generic host pattern. A background IHostedService runs the SNMP polling loop and DVB-API query cycle every 500ms, publishing metric updates to an in-process SignalR hub. The WPF MVVM layer subscribes to hub messages via ReactiveUI and updates carrier meter bindings on the WPF Dispatcher. SQLite in WAL mode stores signal_quality_readings, alarm_records, and engineer_shifts locally. Windows Authentication via NTLM/Kerberos provides role-based access without any network dependency. DPAPI-encrypted user settings store the SNMP community string.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: WPF with C# .NET 8, ReactiveUI MVVM, SkiaSharp for waveform rendering. Backend: .NET 8 generic host, in-process SignalR hub for IPC, SharpSnmpLib for SNMP v2c polling, P/Invoke to vendor DVB-API DLL. Database: SQLite 3 via Microsoft.Data.Sqlite in WAL mode. Reporting: QuestPDF for shift handover PDF generation. CI/CD: Azure Pipelines with WiX Toolset MSI packaging. Deployment: SCCM software distribution.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Carrier Monitor Grid shows real-time MER, C/N, and BER bar indicators for up to 50 configured carriers with alarm status badges (green/amber/red). Alarm Panel lists active and acknowledged alarms sorted by severity and elapsed time. Carrier Detail Pane shows 30-minute SkiaSharp waveform charts for each metric. Alarm Config allows engineers to set high and low threshold values per carrier per metric. Shift Handover Report generates a PDF summarizing alarms, acknowledgements, and per-carrier outage durations for the preceding shift window. Maintenance Log allows engineers to attach notes to specific carrier incidents.
target_output.system_components: WPF main window with carrier grid, alarm panel, and detail pane; in-process SignalR hub for metric event routing; background IHostedService SNMP polling loop with SharpSnmpLib; DVB-API P/Invoke wrapper for satellite carrier metrics; SQLite WAL-mode database on local NTFS volume; ReactiveUI MVVM layer for UI data binding; QuestPDF report generator; DPAPI credential manager for encrypted SNMP community string storage.
target_output.workflows: Signal Polling: IHostedService polling loop fires every 500ms; SharpSnmpLib async GET requests retrieve OID values for MER, C/N, and BER per carrier; DVB-API P/Invoke calls retrieve satellite-specific metrics; results are written to signal_quality_readings in SQLite and published to the SignalR hub; ReactiveUI ViewModel receives hub events and updates carrier meter bindings on the WPF Dispatcher. Alarm Trigger: each new reading is evaluated against the configured threshold for its carrier and metric; a threshold breach creates an alarm_records row with severity ENUM and triggers an in-process Windows Toast notification to the active session; the alarm appears in the Alarm Panel as unacknowledged. Shift Handover Report: engineer clicks Generate Handover Report, selects the shift duration (8 or 12 hours), and chooses a save path; the report service queries alarm_records for the time window, joins with alarm_acknowledgements for MTTA calculation, and invokes QuestPDF to build a structured PDF with per-carrier outage tables; the PDF is saved to the configured path and opened in the default PDF viewer.
target_output.data_model: signal_sources (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, source_type TEXT, snmp_host TEXT, dvb_api_device_id TEXT). carrier_monitors (id INTEGER PK, source_id FK, carrier_name TEXT, frequency_mhz REAL, polarization TEXT, active INTEGER). alarm_thresholds (id INTEGER PK, carrier_id FK, metric TEXT, high_threshold REAL, low_threshold REAL, severity TEXT). signal_quality_readings (id INTEGER PK, carrier_id FK, recorded_at INTEGER, mer_db REAL, cn_db REAL, ber_exp REAL). alarm_records (id INTEGER PK, carrier_id FK, metric TEXT, severity TEXT, value_at_trigger REAL, triggered_at INTEGER, cleared_at INTEGER, status TEXT). alarm_acknowledgements (id INTEGER PK, alarm_id FK, acknowledged_by TEXT, acknowledged_at INTEGER, notes TEXT). engineer_shifts (id INTEGER PK, engineer_name TEXT, shift_start INTEGER, shift_end INTEGER, facility TEXT). maintenance_logs (id INTEGER PK, carrier_id FK, engineer_name TEXT, notes TEXT, logged_at INTEGER).
target_output.api_design: No network API. In-process SignalR hub methods: server-to-client hub.Clients.All.SendAsync('MetricUpdate', carrierId, metric, value, timestamp) for real-time meter updates; hub.Clients.All.SendAsync('AlarmTriggered', alarmRecord) and 'AlarmCleared' for alarm state changes. WPF commands exposed as ReactiveCommand: AcknowledgeAlarmCommand(alarmId), UpdateThresholdCommand(carrierId, metric, value), GenerateHandoverReportCommand(shiftDuration, savePath), AddMaintenanceNoteCommand(carrierId, notes).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Windows Authentication (NTLM/Kerberos): role membership checked via WindowsPrincipal.IsInRole against AD groups BroadcastEngineers (full config), TransmissionTechnicians (ack only), MonitorViewers (read only). SNMP Credential Encryption: community string encrypted with ProtectedData.Protect(DataProtectionScope.LocalMachine) on first configuration save; stored as Base64 ciphertext in ApplicationData user settings; decrypted at polling service startup via ProtectedData.Unprotect; community string never written to disk in plaintext. Audit Logging: alarm config changes and shift report generation written to Windows Application Event Log with engineer identity via EventLog.WriteEntry. OFCOM compliance: alarm_records and maintenance_logs retained per the retention policy for regulatory audit readiness.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Pipelines CI: dotnet build, dotnet test for alarm state machine and DPAPI credential unit tests, WiX Toolset heat/candle/light to produce a signed MSI. Code-signing certificate stored in Azure Pipelines secure file. MSI artifact uploaded to SCCM distribution point via PowerShell. SCCM deployment collection targets engineering workstations on the facility LAN. No internet access required post-install; NuGet packages vendored in the build artifact. Rollback: SCCM package version history retained; prior MSI redeployed via SCCM software center if a defect is found post-deploy.
target_output.observability: Structured log entries written to Windows Application Event Log: polling cycle completion with carrier count and elapsed time, SNMP failures with carrier and error code, alarm state changes with carrier and severity, report generation with output path and duration. Application crash dumps captured to a local %AppData% folder path. Facility IT monitors Windows Event Log entries centrally via SCOM. No external APM tools permitted on the air-gapped facility network.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud or hosting costs; application runs on existing engineering workstations. SQLite footprint for 90 days of 500ms readings across 50 carriers is approximately 2GB — within local disk capacity. Scale to 100 carriers requires profiling SkiaSharp render performance and SQLite write throughput; the future central aggregation server would absorb multi-facility telemetry load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): carrier monitor grid for up to 50 carriers, real-time MER/C/N/BER meters via SignalR, alarm threshold config and acknowledgement, DPAPI-encrypted SNMP credential storage, Windows AD auth, SQLite telemetry, SCCM MSI deployment. Phase 2 (8 weeks): shift handover PDF report, maintenance log UI, 30-minute waveform charts with SkiaSharp, SLA compliance report generation, nightly telemetry cleanup job.
target_output.development_guidelines: C# nullable reference types enabled. StyleCop Analyzers enforced in CI. Unit tests with xUnit for alarm state machine transitions and DPAPI credential encryption round-trip. WPF UI tested with FlaUI for alarm acknowledgement and report generation flows. Signal emulator rig used for integration testing before facility deployment. No third-party NuGet packages without IT approval for air-gapped installation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DVB-API vendor DLL may not be compatible with future Windows updates breaking the P/Invoke binding. Mitigation: wrap P/Invoke calls in a versioned adapter interface; maintain a fallback SNMP-only mode for carriers where DVB-API provides redundant data. Risk: SQLite WAL write contention if polling volume grows beyond 100 carriers. Mitigation: batch writes per polling cycle using a transaction rather than individual INSERTs. Tradeoff: local SQLite over a network database eliminates infrastructure dependency but limits multi-workstation alarm sharing in the current architecture.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose QuestPDF over SSRS for shift handover reports to avoid a SQL Server Reporting Services dependency on a standalone workstation. Selected SharpSnmpLib over SNMP#NET for active maintenance and .NET 8 compatibility. Confirmed DPAPI LocalMachine scope over user scope so the application can decrypt credentials when running under different domain accounts on the same workstation.

metadata.notes: WPF broadcast signal monitoring desktop app; round-2 fixes SNMP credential DPAPI encryption and specifies shift handover report. Intentionally weak plan quality per manifest.

project_goal.value: Automation tool for streaming operations teams that monitors live channel health across multiple CDN providers in real time, automatically triggering failover to a backup ingest when primary stream anomalies are detected
project_goal.rationale: Manual failover during live broadcast outages costs audience reach and revenue; automation must react within seconds

target_users.value: Streaming operations engineers managing live broadcast channels across Akamai, Cloudflare, and Fastly CDN providers
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of the tool are NOC-level engineers who define failover policies and respond to incidents

project_class.rationale: Background daemon with no browser UI; engineers interact via YAML config and Grafana dashboards

capabilities.rationale: Real-time health polling, CDN API integrations, InfluxDB telemetry, Grafana dashboards, PagerDuty alerting

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-second health probe loop, multi-CDN API integrations, YAML policy engine, and incident audit logging

risk_level.rationale: Automated failover during live broadcast has direct audience impact; a false positive triggers unnecessary CDN switches mid-stream

data_sensitivity.rationale: CDN API credentials and broadcast stream URLs are internal; no viewer PII involved

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs inside the broadcast operations network; no public-facing endpoints

access_model.value: IAM roles for AWS resources; internal API key for CDN provider management APIs; no external authentication surface
access_model.rationale: Internal tool without user-facing login; credentials managed via environment and secrets manager

feature_scope.value: Health probe loop polling CDN ingest endpoints every 10 seconds for bitrate, packet loss, and latency; configurable anomaly detection thresholds per channel; automatic failover policy engine; PagerDuty incident creation; audit log for all failover events; Grafana dashboard for real-time health visualization; YAML-based policy configuration versioned in Git
feature_scope.rationale: Complete automated failover system with observability and auditability

mvp_scope.value: Health probe loop, threshold-based anomaly detection, automatic CDN API failover trigger, PagerDuty alert, InfluxDB telemetry, Grafana dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Core automated failover capability for immediate operational value

security_baseline.value: CDN API credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager; IAM role with least-privilege access to Secrets Manager and CDN management APIs; all inter-service communication over TLS; audit log append-only
security_baseline.rationale: Broadcast credentials are sensitive; audit log must be tamper-resistant for change management compliance

frontend_stack.rationale: No custom browser UI; Grafana provides sufficient operational visualization against InfluxDB metrics

backend_stack.rationale: Go goroutines provide efficient concurrent health probing across dozens of channels with minimal memory overhead

data_platform.value: InfluxDB for real-time telemetry time series; Redis 7 for transient state (probe results, failover cooldown timers)
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB purpose-built for high-frequency time-series writes; Redis for sub-millisecond cooldown checks

hosting_target.value: EC2 Auto Scaling Group in us-east-1 within broadcast operations VPC
hosting_target.rationale: EC2 within VPC provides low-latency access to broadcast infrastructure and CDN management APIs

privacy_retention_policy.value: Telemetry data retained 30 days in InfluxDB; incident audit log retained indefinitely for change management; no viewer PII collected
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 30 days sufficient for performance trending; audit log permanent for broadcast license compliance

future_scope.value: ML-based anomaly detection replacing threshold rules, multi-region deployment for geographically distributed broadcast operations, integration with scheduling systems for pre-emptive failover before planned maintenance windows
future_scope.rationale: Threshold rules cover MVP; ML anomaly detection is a future enhancement

constraints.value: Failover must trigger within 30 seconds of consecutive threshold breaches; false positive rate must be below 1% measured weekly; YAML policy changes must be validated and deployed without restarting the probe loop
constraints.rationale: 30-second SLA prevents significant audience loss; hot policy reload avoids disruptive restarts during live events

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards against InfluxDB for real-time bitrate, packet loss, and latency per channel; Prometheus for Go runtime and application metrics; PagerDuty for failover incident alerting
observability_baseline.rationale: Operations team monitors via Grafana NOC dashboard; PagerDuty pages on-call during failovers

execution_preference.value: Single Go binary deployed as systemd service on EC2; YAML config hot-reload via SIGHUP; GitHub Actions CI with integration tests against CDN stub
execution_preference.rationale: Single binary simplifies deployment and update on EC2; SIGHUP reload avoids process restart during live events

compliance_context.value: Change management audit logging required for broadcast license compliance; failover events must be logged with timestamp, trigger reason, and operator acknowledgment for regulatory review
compliance_context.rationale: Broadcast license conditions require documented evidence of incident response procedures and execution

project.goal: Automated CDN failover tool for live broadcast channels
project.target_users: Streaming operations NOC engineers
project.timeline: Production deployment in 10 weeks

backend.architecture: Single binary with goroutine-per-channel probe loops and a policy engine goroutine
backend.config: YAML-based failover policies hot-reloaded via SIGHUP

security.credentials: CDN API keys in AWS Secrets Manager; IAM role access only
security.audit: Append-only incident_records in InfluxDB with operator acknowledgment field
security.network: Internal VPC only; no public endpoints

data.time_series: InfluxDB: cdn_health_probes measurement with bitrate_kbps, packet_loss_pct, latency_ms fields tagged by channel_id and cdn_provider
data.state: Redis: failover cooldown keys (channel:{id}:cooldown, TTL 5 minutes), recent probe result cache

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: go test with CDN stub mock, go build, deploy to EC2 via SSM Run Command
devops.hosting: EC2 c6i.xlarge in ASG min=1 max=3, us-east-1 broadcast VPC
devops.reload: SIGHUP triggers YAML config reload and policy engine restart without probe loop interruption

constraints.failover_latency: 30 seconds from first threshold breach to CDN API switch call
constraints.false_positive_rate: Below 1% weekly; requires 3 consecutive breaching probes before triggering

reasoner_reviews.product: The 3-consecutive-breach requirement before triggering failover is the right approach to keep false positive rate below 1% -- transient CDN hiccups are common and a single probe threshold breach should not trigger a broadcast interruption. The YAML policy config in Git with hot-reload via SIGHUP is an excellent operational pattern for NOC engineers who need to adjust thresholds mid-event without restarting the daemon.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go goroutines per channel is the right architecture for this probe density. InfluxDB line protocol writes are append-only and high-throughput, well-suited to 10-second probe intervals across 50+ channels. The failover cooldown in Redis is critical -- without a cooldown period after a successful failover, the tool could oscillate between primary and backup if the primary recovers while the backup has issues. Cooldown should be at least 5 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.security: CDN API credentials must be rotated regularly and fetched from Secrets Manager at startup and on rotation events rather than being cached indefinitely. The audit log in InfluxDB should use a separate retention policy with no write permission granted to the probe loop -- writes go through a dedicated audit writer service account to prevent accidental or malicious overwrite.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30-second failover SLA requires the 3-breach detection window (3 x 10 seconds = 30 seconds) to align with the probe interval. The hot-reload via SIGHUP must not interrupt in-flight probes -- the policy engine should use a read-write mutex so active probe evaluations complete before the new policy is applied.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with three concurrent subsystems: ProbeManager (goroutine per channel_monitor, 10s ticker, writes to InfluxDB and Redis probe cache), PolicyEngine (goroutine consuming probe results from buffered channel, evaluates anomaly thresholds, triggers FailoverExecutor), FailoverExecutor (calls CDN provider API to switch ingest endpoint, creates PagerDuty incident, writes audit record)
backend.api_patterns: No public API; internal Go channels for inter-goroutine communication; SIGHUP signal handler triggers YAML reload with RWMutex to fence active policy evaluations
backend.failure_handling: CDN API call failures retry 3x with 2s exponential backoff; if all retries fail, PagerDuty alert escalates to manual intervention; probe goroutine panics are recovered and restarted with 5s delay; InfluxDB write failures are logged but do not block probe loop
backend.scaling_notes: Single binary handles up to 200 channels on c6i.xlarge; beyond 200 channels, deploy second instance with channel range partitioning via config

security.auth_design: EC2 instance IAM role grants SecretsManagerReadWrite on specific secret ARNs for CDN API keys; no hardcoded credentials; keys fetched at startup and on secretsmanager:RotateSecret event via EventBridge
security.secrets_management: CDN API keys stored as JSON secret in AWS Secrets Manager: {akamai_api_key, cloudflare_api_token, fastly_service_key}; rotated every 90 days via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All failover events written to InfluxDB incident_records measurement with fields: channel_id, trigger_reason, primary_cdn, backup_cdn, triggered_by (automation/manual), operator_ack BOOL, ack_timestamp; retention policy: never expire
security.security_risks: Risk: InfluxDB probe data written from the same service account as audit records -- audit records could be overwritten. Mitigation: separate InfluxDB tokens with write-only access to cdn_health_probes measurement and a dedicated audit writer token for incident_records

data.entities: cdn_health_probes (InfluxDB measurement: tags channel_id, cdn_provider, ingest_endpoint; fields bitrate_kbps FLOAT, packet_loss_pct FLOAT, latency_ms INT, probe_status ENUM ok/timeout/error; timestamp 10s cadence). anomaly_detections (InfluxDB measurement: tags channel_id, cdn_provider; fields threshold_type STRING, breach_value FLOAT, threshold_value FLOAT, consecutive_count INT; timestamp). incident_records (InfluxDB measurement: tags channel_id; fields trigger_reason TEXT, primary_cdn STRING, backup_cdn STRING, triggered_by ENUM automation/manual, operator_ack BOOL, ack_operator STRING, resolution_summary TEXT; never-expire retention policy). failover_policies (YAML files: channel_id, primary_cdn, backup_cdn, thresholds {bitrate_min_kbps, packet_loss_max_pct, latency_max_ms}, consecutive_breach_count, cooldown_minutes). channel_monitors (in-memory Go structs from YAML config: channel_id, ingest_url, cdn_provider, last_probe_result, consecutive_breach_count).
data.storage_design: InfluxDB: cdn_health organization, two buckets -- telemetry (30-day retention) and audit (never expire). Redis: keys channel:{id}:cooldown (TTL 5 min after failover), channel:{id}:last_probe (JSON, TTL 30s)
data.analytics_events: Grafana dashboard panels: real-time bitrate per channel heatmap, packet_loss_pct 5-minute rolling average per CDN, failover event annotations on timeline, weekly false-positive rate calculation from incident_records

devops.deployment_topology: EC2 c6i.xlarge in us-east-1 broadcast VPC ASG (min=1, max=3 for redundancy). Single Go binary deployed as systemd service. Binary updates via SSM Run Command: stop service, copy new binary from S3 artifact bucket, start service. YAML policy configs in Git; synced to EC2 via S3 on CI merge.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with CDN provider mock (httptest.Server returning stub probe responses), go build -o failover-daemon, docker-free -- binary artifact uploaded to S3, SSM Run Command on tagged EC2 instances to deploy
devops.observability_stack: Grafana + Prometheus for Go runtime metrics (goroutine count, GC pause, memory). InfluxDB Grafana data source for probe telemetry and failover event annotations. PagerDuty integration via Events API v2 for failover alerts.
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous binary artifact retained in S3 with version tag; rollback via SSM Run Command deploying prior version string; YAML config rollback via git revert and S3 sync

target_output.thinking_summary: Automated CDN failover tool for live broadcast operations. Key architecture decisions: goroutine-per-channel probe loop in Go for concurrent low-overhead health polling; InfluxDB for time-series telemetry with a separate never-expire bucket for audit records; Redis for failover cooldown timers to prevent oscillation; 3-consecutive-breach detection before triggering to meet the 1% false positive rate constraint; SIGHUP hot-reload with RWMutex to avoid probe interruption during live events. Compliance requirement drives append-only audit records in a dedicated InfluxDB bucket with restricted write credentials.
target_output.title: Live Channel CDN Failover Automation Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 daemon deployed as a systemd service on EC2 within the broadcast operations VPC, monitoring live channel health across Akamai, Cloudflare, and Fastly ingest endpoints at 10-second intervals. The policy engine triggers automatic failover to backup CDN ingest when three consecutive probes breach configured thresholds for bitrate, packet loss, or latency. Failover actions call the respective CDN provider management API, create a PagerDuty incident, and write an immutable audit record to a dedicated InfluxDB bucket. YAML-based failover policies are hot-reloaded via SIGHUP without interrupting the probe loop. Telemetry is visualized on Grafana NOC dashboards. The tool meets broadcast license change management requirements via the append-only incident audit log.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Go binary with three concurrent internal subsystems communicating via buffered Go channels. ProbeManager spawns one goroutine per channel_monitor configured in the YAML policy file; each goroutine fires a 10-second ticker, checks the CDN ingest endpoint via HTTP HEAD or stream manifest validation, writes probe results to InfluxDB and caches the result in Redis. PolicyEngine consumes probe results from a buffered channel, maintains in-memory consecutive-breach counters per channel, and evaluates failover thresholds according to the active YAML policy; when 3 consecutive breaches are detected and no cooldown is active, it dispatches a FailoverJob. FailoverExecutor receives FailoverJobs, checks the Redis cooldown key for the channel (5-minute TTL set after each failover to prevent oscillation), calls the CDN provider management API to redirect ingest traffic, creates a PagerDuty incident via Events API v2, and writes an immutable failover record to the InfluxDB audit bucket. YAML configs are stored in Git, synced to EC2 via S3 on CI merge, and hot-reloaded via SIGHUP using an RWMutex to fence in-flight policy evaluations.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. Internal architecture: goroutines with buffered channels for probe-to-policy pipeline. Time-Series: InfluxDB 2.7 (telemetry bucket 30-day retention; audit bucket never-expire). State Cache: Redis 7 for failover cooldown keys and recent probe results. CDN Integrations: Akamai Fast Purge API, Cloudflare Stream API, Fastly Real-Time Streaming API via Go HTTP clients. Alerting: PagerDuty Events API v2. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager. Visualization: Grafana 10 with InfluxDB Flux data source. Monitoring: Prometheus exposition endpoint in the daemon for Go runtime metrics. CI: GitHub Actions with httptest.Server CDN mocks.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Health Probe Loop: per-channel goroutines polling CDN ingest endpoints every 10 seconds; writes bitrate_kbps, packet_loss_pct, and latency_ms to InfluxDB cdn_health_probes measurement tagged by channel_id and cdn_provider. Anomaly Detection: policy engine evaluates 3-consecutive-breach threshold per metric type; writes anomaly_detections measurement on each breach for trend analysis. Failover Execution: CDN management API call to redirect ingest; Redis cooldown key set for 5 minutes to prevent oscillation; PagerDuty incident created with channel ID, breach metric, and before/after CDN provider. Audit Log: immutable incident_records written to InfluxDB audit bucket with operator_ack field for post-incident sign-off. YAML Policy Engine: hot-reload via SIGHUP; policies specify per-channel primary_cdn, backup_cdn, threshold values, and cooldown_minutes. Grafana NOC Dashboard: real-time bitrate heatmap, packet loss rolling averages, failover event annotations, weekly false-positive rate panel.
target_output.system_components: Go Failover Daemon: ProbeManager, PolicyEngine, FailoverExecutor subsystems plus SIGHUP config reload handler and Prometheus /metrics endpoint. InfluxDB 2.7: telemetry bucket (cdn_health_probes, anomaly_detections measurements, 30-day retention) and audit bucket (incident_records measurement, never-expire, restricted write token). Redis 7: cooldown keys with 5-minute TTL, recent probe result cache with 30-second TTL. AWS Secrets Manager: CDN API credential JSON secret. EC2 c6i.xlarge: systemd service host in broadcast VPC. S3 Artifact Bucket: Go binary versions and YAML policy files synced from Git. CDN Management APIs: Akamai, Cloudflare, Fastly ingest endpoint redirect APIs. PagerDuty: failover incident creation and resolution via Events API v2. Grafana: NOC dashboard server connected to InfluxDB.
target_output.workflows: Health Probe Workflow: ProbeManager goroutine for channel_id=sports-live-1 fires 10-second ticker; sends HTTP HEAD to https://ingest.akamai.com/sports-live-1/primary; measures response latency; parses bitrate from RTMP stats API if available; writes to InfluxDB line protocol: cdn_health_probes,channel_id=sports-live-1,cdn_provider=akamai bitrate_kbps=4200,packet_loss_pct=0.1,latency_ms=45; caches result in Redis key channel:sports-live-1:last_probe with 30s TTL. Failover Trigger Workflow: PolicyEngine receives 3rd consecutive breach for sports-live-1 (bitrate_kbps=800 below threshold 2000); checks Redis key channel:sports-live-1:cooldown (absent -- no recent failover); dispatches FailoverJob{channel_id: sports-live-1, trigger: bitrate_below_threshold, primary: akamai, backup: cloudflare}; FailoverExecutor calls Cloudflare Stream API PUT /zones/{zone}/stream/live_inputs/{id} to activate backup ingest; sets Redis cooldown key TTL=300s; creates PagerDuty incident with severity=critical; writes incident_records to InfluxDB audit bucket with triggered_by=automation. Operator Acknowledgment Workflow: on-call engineer receives PagerDuty page, reviews Grafana timeline, acknowledges the incident via the operations CLI that PATCHes the incident_records InfluxDB record via the audit writer service account setting operator_ack=true and ack_operator=jsmith.
target_output.data_model: cdn_health_probes (InfluxDB measurement, tags: channel_id STRING, cdn_provider ENUM akamai/cloudflare/fastly, ingest_endpoint STRING; fields: bitrate_kbps FLOAT, packet_loss_pct FLOAT, latency_ms INT, probe_status ENUM ok/timeout/error; 10-second write cadence; 30-day retention). anomaly_detections (InfluxDB measurement, tags: channel_id, cdn_provider, threshold_type ENUM bitrate/packet_loss/latency; fields: breach_value FLOAT, threshold_value FLOAT, consecutive_count INT; written on each breach; 30-day retention). incident_records (InfluxDB measurement in audit bucket; tags: channel_id; fields: trigger_reason TEXT, primary_cdn STRING, backup_cdn STRING, triggered_by ENUM automation/manual, operator_ack BOOL, ack_operator STRING, resolution_summary TEXT; never-expire retention; append-only via restricted write token). failover_policies (YAML structure: channel_id, primary_cdn, backup_cdn, thresholds {bitrate_min_kbps INT, packet_loss_max_pct FLOAT, latency_max_ms INT}, consecutive_breach_count INT default 3, cooldown_minutes INT default 5). channel_monitors (in-memory Go struct: channel_id, ingest_url, cdn_provider, probe_ticker, consecutive_breach_count INT, last_probe_at time.Time).
target_output.api_design: No external REST API. Internal Go channel API: ProbeManager writes ProbeResult{ChannelID, CDNProvider, BitrateKbps, PacketLossPct, LatencyMs, Status, Timestamp} to resultsCh chan ProbeResult (buffered 1000). PolicyEngine reads resultsCh, writes FailoverJob{ChannelID, TriggerMetric, PrimaryEndpoint, BackupEndpoint} to failoverCh chan FailoverJob. External integrations: Akamai Fast Purge API POST /config-media/v2/media-services-live, Cloudflare Stream API PUT /zones/{id}/stream/live_inputs/{id}, Fastly Real-Time Streaming API PUT /service/{id}/version/{n}/domain, PagerDuty Events API v2 POST https://events.pagerduty.com/v2/enqueue.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Credentials: CDN API keys stored as JSON in AWS Secrets Manager secret arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:ACCOUNT:secret:broadcast/cdn-api-keys; fetched at startup via IAM instance role with secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission scoped to that ARN; re-fetched on EventBridge secretsmanager:RotateSecret event. Audit log access control: InfluxDB audit bucket uses separate token with write permission to incident_records only; telemetry writes use a separate token without access to the audit bucket. Compliance: incident_records retention policy never-expire satisfies broadcast license change management documentation requirement; operator_ack field provides sign-off evidence for regulatory review. Network: daemon runs in broadcast VPC with no inbound rules; all outbound via NAT Gateway to CDN management APIs and PagerDuty. YAML config changes require Git PR approval before merge to main; CI validation of YAML schema blocks invalid policy deployment.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EC2 c6i.xlarge in us-east-1 broadcast VPC, Auto Scaling Group min=1 max=3 (standby instances for high availability). systemd unit file: failover-daemon.service with Restart=always and RestartSec=5s. Deployment pipeline: GitHub Actions on push to main -- go test ./... (with httptest.Server CDN mocks for Akamai, Cloudflare, Fastly), go build -o failover-daemon, upload binary to S3 s3://broadcast-artifacts/failover-daemon/{git_sha}/failover-daemon, sync YAML policies to s3://broadcast-artifacts/policies/, SSM Run Command on EC2 instances tagged Role=failover-daemon to stop service, download binary, start service. Hot config reload: operations engineer updates YAML policy in Git, CI syncs to S3, engineer sends kill -SIGHUP {pid} on EC2 via SSM; daemon reloads YAML under RWMutex and restarts PolicyEngine goroutine without interrupting probe loops. Rollback: SSM Run Command deploying prior binary from S3 with previous git_sha tag.
target_output.observability: Grafana NOC Dashboard (InfluxDB Flux queries): real-time bitrate_kbps per channel heatmap updated every 10 seconds, packet_loss_pct 5-minute rolling average per CDN provider bar chart, failover event vertical annotations on timeline panels, weekly false-positive rate panel computing (automated_failovers - operator_confirmed_real_failures) / automated_failovers from incident_records. Prometheus metrics endpoint :9090/metrics: failover_daemon_probe_goroutines_active gauge, failover_daemon_policy_evaluations_total counter, failover_daemon_failovers_triggered_total counter by channel_id and trigger_type, Go runtime gc_pause_seconds histogram. PagerDuty: critical severity incident on failover trigger with dedup_key=channel:{id}:{date} to prevent duplicate pages; auto-resolve sent when operator marks incident_record resolved.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 c6i.xlarge: ~$170/month on-demand; reserved instance reduces to ~$100/month. InfluxDB Cloud (or self-hosted on same EC2): telemetry write volume at 10-second cadence for 100 channels is approximately 864K data points per day -- within InfluxDB Cloud free tier or minimal paid tier. Redis ElastiCache cache.t3.micro: ~$15/month for cooldown and probe cache volume. Total: approximately $120-200/month depending on instance reservation and InfluxDB hosting choice.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Go daemon with ProbeManager goroutine-per-channel polling CDN ingest endpoints, PolicyEngine with 3-consecutive-breach detection, FailoverExecutor calling Akamai and Cloudflare management APIs, Redis cooldown keys, InfluxDB telemetry writes, PagerDuty incident creation, YAML policy config loading, SIGHUP hot-reload, GitHub Actions CI with CDN mocks, Grafana dashboard with bitrate and packet loss panels. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Fastly CDN integration, operator acknowledgment CLI for InfluxDB audit records, incident_records never-expire audit bucket, weekly false-positive rate Grafana panel, Secrets Manager credential rotation integration. Phase 3 (future): ML-based anomaly detection replacing threshold rules, multi-region deployment, integration with broadcast scheduling system for pre-emptive failover windows.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go standard library only for HTTP client and signal handling; no external HTTP framework. RWMutex required on all YAML policy struct reads in PolicyEngine goroutine. CDN API client methods must accept context.Context with 10-second timeout to prevent hung goroutines blocking the failover path. All InfluxDB writes must be non-blocking -- use a buffered write channel with a goroutine draining to InfluxDB; drop writes rather than blocking probe loop on InfluxDB backpressure. Unit tests must cover: 3-breach detection state machine, cooldown key prevents redundant failover, SIGHUP reload does not lose in-flight probe results, CDN API retry on transient error.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: false positive failover during a CDN API transient blip rather than a genuine ingest outage. Mitigation: 3-consecutive-breach requirement across 30 seconds already reduces false positives; additional mitigation is cross-validation with a secondary probe method (manifest fetch rather than TCP ping) before triggering. Risk: InfluxDB write backpressure during a mass outage event where all channels fail simultaneously could delay probe loop. Mitigation: non-blocking buffered write channel drops telemetry points rather than blocking probe goroutines during peak write load. Tradeoff: single binary on EC2 over container-based deployment simplifies the operational model and avoids container scheduling latency -- acceptable for this internal tool where deployment frequency is low. Tradeoff: threshold-based detection over ML anomaly detection for MVP -- lower implementation complexity at the cost of some false negatives on subtle degradation patterns.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected InfluxDB over PostgreSQL time-series extension for probe telemetry given the 10-second write cadence and Grafana native integration. Confirmed Redis over DynamoDB for cooldown state due to sub-millisecond TTL check requirement in the hot failover path. Selected SIGHUP hot-reload over config API endpoint to avoid exposing a management surface in the broadcast VPC. Confirmed 3-consecutive-breach detection (30 seconds) as the minimum that meets both the 30-second failover SLA and the less-than-1-percent false positive rate target.

metadata.notes: Automated CDN failover daemon for live broadcast operations with Go goroutine-per-channel probing, InfluxDB telemetry, Redis cooldown, and compliance audit logging.

profile.domain: HLS and DASH Adaptive Streaming Protocol SDK

project_goal.value: Open-source TypeScript SDK for HLS and DASH adaptive streaming protocols providing type-safe manifest parsing, segment scheduling, and bandwidth estimation primitives for custom player implementations
project_goal.rationale: Fills a gap for player developers who need typed building blocks without taking on a full player framework dependency

target_users.value: Player developers building custom HLS and DASH players for web, Node.js, and hybrid mobile environments
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are SDK integrators who need composable, tree-shakeable primitives rather than opinionated player implementations

project_class.rationale: Published as an npm package; architecture focuses on exports, API surface, and bundle size rather than hosting or deployment

capabilities.rationale: Manifest parsing and bandwidth estimation are pure computation; no frontend UI or hosted service is part of this library

complexity_level.rationale: Dual HLS/DASH format support, streaming segment scheduler, and EWMA bandwidth estimator involve non-trivial protocol knowledge but no infrastructure complexity

risk_level.rationale: Client-side library with no server-side components; no authentication or data storage; security surface is limited to dependency supply chain and XSS if misused with user-supplied manifest URLs

data_sensitivity.rationale: Library processes manifest data in memory; no personal data is collected or stored

external_exposure.rationale: Published to npm and JSDelivr CDN for public consumption; no private API surface

access_model.value: Open-source, no authentication required; npm package installation is the only access mechanism
access_model.rationale: Public open-source library; access controls not applicable

feature_scope.value: HLS manifest parser (M3U8 master and media playlists), DASH MPD document parser, media segment scheduler with configurable pre-buffer depth, bandwidth estimation with EWMA throughput model, DRM initialization data extraction (Widevine, FairPlay, PlayReady), subtitle cue block parser
feature_scope.rationale: Complete set of protocol primitives for a custom player to build playback logic without needing a full player framework

mvp_scope.value: HLS manifest parser, basic segment scheduler, EWMA bandwidth estimator, TypeScript type exports for HLS manifest object graph
mvp_scope.rationale: HLS is the dominant protocol; DASH and DRM support follow once core parsing primitives are validated by early adopters

security_baseline.value: Dependency audit via npm audit in CI; no eval or dynamic code execution; manifest URL validation to prevent SSRF in server-side usage
security_baseline.rationale: Library-level security controls focus on supply chain integrity and preventing misuse in server-side environments where manifest URLs come from untrusted input

frontend_stack.rationale: library_sdk has no browser UI component; documentation site is a separate project

backend_stack.value: TypeScript 5.5 targeting browser and Node.js dual environments
backend_stack.rationale: Dual-target build ensures the SDK works in both browser player contexts and Node.js server-side rendering and testing environments

data_platform.value: none
data_platform.rationale: library_sdk operates entirely in-memory; no persistent data storage is required

hosting_target.value: npm registry and JSDelivr CDN
hosting_target.rationale: Standard npm distribution with JSDelivr as a CDN mirror for browser script tag imports

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained; library processes manifest data transiently in caller memory
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Stateless library with no data persistence; privacy policy not applicable

future_scope.value: CMAF chunk-transfer streaming support, low-latency HLS (LL-HLS) blocking playlist reload, DASH multi-period support, React Native bridge module
future_scope.rationale: LL-HLS and CMAF are increasingly adopted by broadcasters; React Native bridge would expand addressable player developer market

constraints.value: Bundle size under 20 KB gzipped for the core HLS parser; zero runtime dependencies in the published package
constraints.rationale: Player developers are sensitive to bundle size impact; third-party dependencies introduce supply chain risk and update maintenance overhead

observability_baseline.value: None required; library emits no telemetry by design
observability_baseline.rationale: Telemetry in a shared library would be unacceptable to player developers; observability is the caller responsibility

execution_preference.value: Monthly release cadence with semantic versioning; CHANGELOG maintained per conventional commits
execution_preference.rationale: Predictable release schedule allows player developer teams to plan upgrade cycles

project.goal: TypeScript SDK for HLS/DASH manifest parsing and segment scheduling
project.target_users: Player developers in browser and Node.js environments
project.timeline: MVP HLS parser in 6 weeks; DASH support in 12 weeks

backend.framework: none; pure library with no runtime framework dependency
backend.api: Exported TypeScript functions and classes; no HTTP API

security.supply_chain: npm audit in CI, Dependabot for dependency updates
security.url_validation: Manifest URL allowlist validation in Node.js server-side usage mode

devops.testing: Vitest for unit tests, Playwright for browser integration tests

constraints.bundle_size: core HLS parser under 20 KB gzipped
constraints.zero_deps: no runtime dependencies in published package

open_questions.dash_namespace: Decide whether DASH MPD parser should use a schema-generated type system or hand-authored types

confirmed_decisions.semver: conventional commits for automated CHANGELOG generation

reasoner_reviews.product: The zero-dependency constraint is the right call for a library SDK - player developers are extremely sensitive to transitive dependency sprawl. The EWMA bandwidth estimator must expose both the recommendation function and the underlying throughput history so callers can implement custom ABR policies on top of the primitives.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The dual ESM/CJS build via tsup is correct: tsup handles both targets cleanly and supports tree-shaking. The public API surface should be designed around composable function exports rather than a class hierarchy to enable tree-shaking of unused protocol parsers. HLS and DASH parsers should be importable independently so a HLS-only player does not bundle the DASH parser.
reasoner_reviews.security: The manifest URL validation guard for Node.js SSRF prevention should be implemented as an optional validator that callers can pass a custom allowlist to, rather than a hard-coded block list, so the library remains flexible for intranet CDN origins. Dependency audit via npm audit should be automated in CI with a fail-on-high threshold.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 20 KB gzipped for the core HLS parser is achievable with no external dependencies. A size regression test using size-limit should be added to CI to prevent inadvertent growth from new feature additions.

backend.service_design: Pure TypeScript library organized as a monorepo with three publishable packages: @streaming-sdk/hls (HLS M3U8 parser and segment scheduler), @streaming-sdk/dash (DASH MPD parser), @streaming-sdk/abr (bandwidth estimator and ABR recommendation engine). Each package has independent versioning and bundle size budget.
backend.api_patterns: Function-oriented public API: `parseHlsMaster(url: string, fetchFn: FetchLike): Promise<HlsMasterPlaylist>`, `parseMediaPlaylist(text: string): HlsMediaPlaylist`, `createSegmentScheduler(playlist: HlsMediaPlaylist, config: SchedulerConfig): SegmentScheduler`, `createEwmaEstimator(config: EwmaConfig): BandwidthEstimator`. All types exported from package index for consumer TypeScript type inference.
backend.failure_handling: Manifest parse errors throw typed `ManifestParseError` with `lineNumber`, `tag`, and `message` fields. Network fetch errors propagate as-is from the caller-supplied `fetchFn`, keeping the library agnostic to fetch implementations. Segment scheduler emits `SchedulerEvent` objects rather than throwing on segment download failure, so callers can implement custom retry policies.

data.entities: HlsMasterPlaylist (uri, variants: HlsVariant[], mediaGroups: HlsMediaGroup[], sessionData: HlsSessionData[]), HlsVariant (bandwidth, averageBandwidth, codecs, resolution, frameRate, uri, audioGroup, subtitleGroup), HlsMediaPlaylist (targetDuration, mediaSequence, segments: HlsSegment[], endList, playlistType), HlsSegment (uri, duration, title, byteRange, encrypted: HlsEncryption | null, discontinuity), DashMPD (profiles, minBufferTime, periods: DashPeriod[]), DashPeriod (id, start, duration, adaptationSets: DashAdaptationSet[]), DashAdaptationSet (contentType, mimeType, representations: DashRepresentation[], contentProtections: ContentProtection[]), BandwidthSample (timestampMs, durationMs, bytesTransferred, throughputBps), EwmaEstimate (smoothedThroughputBps, lastSampleThroughputBps, confidence).
data.schema_notes: All parsed types are plain TypeScript interfaces with no class instances to keep them JSON-serializable and easily testable. Discriminated union types used for encrypted vs unencrypted segments and for HLS vs DASH DRM initialization data.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: tsc --noEmit for type checking, ESLint with @typescript-eslint, Vitest for unit tests (HLS parser, DASH MPD parser, EWMA estimator, segment scheduler), size-limit check (core HLS parser bundle must be under 20 KB gzipped), npm publish on release tag with provenance attestation.
devops.observability_stack: size-limit CI step tracks per-package bundle size history. Vitest coverage report with 90% line coverage threshold. Playwright browser integration test verifies HLS master playlist parse and EWMA estimator in a headless browser context.
devops.deployment_topology: tsup builds dual ESM (.mjs) and CJS (.cjs) outputs with TypeScript declaration files (.d.ts). npm pack dry-run in CI verifies published file manifest. JSDelivr auto-mirrors npm releases for CDN script tag usage at cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@streaming-sdk/hls.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a library_sdk project class, so the architecture plan must focus on package exports, API surface design, and bundle size rather than hosting infrastructure. Key decisions: monorepo with independent packages for HLS, DASH, and ABR to enable tree-shaking; function-oriented API surface rather than class hierarchy; zero runtime dependencies enforced by size-limit in CI. The flawed element in this plan is that the deployment_and_operations section incorrectly describes a hosted server deployment as if this were a web_app rather than an npm package, which contradicts the library_sdk project class and the hosting_target contract field.
target_output.title: HLS and DASH Adaptive Streaming Protocol SDK
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source TypeScript SDK providing type-safe HLS M3U8 and DASH MPD manifest parsing, segment scheduling, and EWMA bandwidth estimation primitives for custom player developers. Organized as a monorepo with three independently versioned packages: @streaming-sdk/hls, @streaming-sdk/dash, and @streaming-sdk/abr. Published to npm with dual ESM/CJS output via tsup. Zero runtime dependencies. Core HLS parser bundle under 20 KB gzipped enforced by size-limit in CI.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-package monorepo architecture: @streaming-sdk/hls exports HLS M3U8 master and media playlist parsers, a streaming segment scheduler, and DRM initialization data extractors for FairPlay and Widevine; @streaming-sdk/dash exports DASH MPD document parser with multi-period and ContentProtection support; @streaming-sdk/abr exports the EWMA throughput estimator and bandwidth recommendation engine. Each package is independently importable to support tree-shaking in player bundles that only consume HLS or DASH. All packages target both browser ESM and Node.js CJS environments via tsup dual build. The public API is function-oriented to maximize tree-shakeability: no class instantiation is required for basic manifest parsing.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.5 with strict mode. Build: tsup 8 with ESM and CJS dual output and TypeScript declaration files. Testing: Vitest 1.6 for unit tests, Playwright 1.44 for browser integration tests. Linting: ESLint with @typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked. Bundle size: size-limit enforcing 20 KB gzipped budget on @streaming-sdk/hls core parser. Release: semantic-release with conventional commits for automated CHANGELOG and npm publish with provenance attestation. Repository: pnpm workspace monorepo.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HLS Manifest Parsing: parseHlsMaster fetches and parses M3U8 master playlists returning typed HlsMasterPlaylist with all variant streams, media groups, and session data. parseMediaPlaylist parses M3U8 media playlists returning HlsMediaPlaylist with segment list, encryption keys, and discontinuity markers. DASH MPD Parsing: parseMpd fetches and parses DASH MPD XML returning DashMPD with periods, adaptation sets, representations, and ContentProtection elements for DRM key system negotiation. Segment Scheduler: createSegmentScheduler wraps a media playlist and emits SchedulerEvent objects for the caller to drive segment download, buffer management, and playlist refresh timing. Bandwidth Estimation: createEwmaEstimator accepts per-segment download metrics and returns EwmaEstimate with smoothed throughput and a next-rendition recommendation given a candidate bandwidth ladder. DRM Initialization: extractWidevineInitData and extractFairPlayInitData extract DRM-specific binary initialization data from segment encryption tags for passing to EME.
target_output.system_components: pnpm workspace root (build orchestration, shared devDependencies, size-limit config), packages/hls (M3U8 tokenizer, master playlist parser, media playlist parser, segment scheduler, encryption tag handler, FairPlay/Widevine init data extractor), packages/dash (MPD XML parser, period/AdaptationSet/Representation tree walker, ContentProtection extractor, segment template resolver), packages/abr (EWMA estimator, bandwidth sample ring buffer, rendition recommendation function). Shared types package (packages/types) for cross-package TypeScript interface definitions. GitHub Actions CI (type check, lint, test, size-limit, npm publish).
target_output.workflows: Manifest Parse and Schedule Workflow: Player developer imports parseHlsMaster from @streaming-sdk/hls and calls it with a master manifest URL and a fetchFn (browser fetch or Node.js undici). The function returns an HlsMasterPlaylist typed object. Developer calls createSegmentScheduler with a selected HlsMediaPlaylist variant and a SchedulerConfig specifying pre-buffer depth. The scheduler yields SchedulerEvent objects that the player uses to drive segment fetches and buffer append operations. EWMA Bandwidth Adaptation Workflow: After each segment download, player calls estimator.addSample with download duration, bytes, and timestamp. estimator.recommend(variants) returns the recommended next HlsVariant based on smoothed throughput with a safety factor. Player switches rendition and calls createSegmentScheduler with the new media playlist URL on discontinuity.
target_output.data_model: HlsMasterPlaylist {uri: string, variants: HlsVariant[], mediaGroups: HlsMediaGroup[], sessionData: HlsSessionData[], independentSegments: boolean}. HlsVariant {bandwidth: number, averageBandwidth?: number, codecs: string[], resolution?: {width: number, height: number}, frameRate?: number, uri: string, audioGroup?: string, subtitleGroup?: string}. HlsSegment {uri: string, duration: number, title?: string, byteRange?: {offset: number, length: number}, encryption?: HlsEncryption, discontinuity: boolean, programDateTime?: Date}. HlsEncryption {method: 'AES-128' | 'SAMPLE-AES' | 'SAMPLE-AES-CTR', uri: string, iv?: Uint8Array, keyFormat?: string}. DashRepresentation {id: string, bandwidth: number, mimeType: string, codecs: string, width?: number, height?: number, segmentTemplate?: DashSegmentTemplate, contentProtections: ContentProtection[]}. BandwidthSample {timestampMs: number, durationMs: number, bytesTransferred: number}. EwmaEstimate {smoothedThroughputBps: number, confidence: number, recommendedVariantBandwidth: number}.
target_output.api_design: Package exports (not HTTP endpoints - library_sdk): @streaming-sdk/hls exports: parseHlsMaster(url, fetchFn?, options?), parseMediaPlaylist(text, baseUri?), createSegmentScheduler(playlist, config), extractFairPlayInitData(segment), extractWidevineInitData(segment). @streaming-sdk/dash exports: parseMpd(url, fetchFn?, options?), selectRepresentation(adaptationSet, bandwidth). @streaming-sdk/abr exports: createEwmaEstimator(config: {fastHalfLifeMs, slowHalfLifeMs, safetyFactor}), estimator.addSample(sample), estimator.estimate(), estimator.recommend(variants). All exported functions and types re-exported from a top-level @streaming-sdk entry point for consumers who import all capabilities.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Supply chain: npm audit --audit-level=high in CI fails the build on high-severity vulnerabilities. Dependabot enabled for automated dependency PRs. npm publish with --provenance flag generates SLSA build provenance attestation published to npm transparency log. Manifest URL validation: Node.js server-side users can pass an optional urlValidator function to parseHlsMaster and parseMpd to enforce allowlist policies and prevent SSRF in environments where manifest URLs come from untrusted input. No eval or Function constructor usage anywhere in the codebase. No dynamic require() calls that could bypass bundler tree-shaking.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: FLAW - This section incorrectly describes a hosted server deployment for a library_sdk project. The plan deploys an Express.js API server on ECS Fargate at api.streaming-sdk.example.com to serve manifest parsing requests over HTTP, which contradicts the library_sdk project class, the hosting_target contract field specifying npm registry and JSDelivr CDN, and the zero-dependency constraint. No server should exist for a client-side library. The correct deployment is: semantic-release publishes the npm package to the registry on every merge to main that includes a releasable commit per conventional commits specification. The tsup build outputs are committed to the release as GitHub release assets. JSDelivr auto-mirrors the npm package. No server infrastructure is provisioned.
target_output.observability: size-limit CI step emits per-package bundle size to GitHub Actions summary after every PR, tracking gzipped ESM size for @streaming-sdk/hls (budget 20 KB), @streaming-sdk/dash (budget 15 KB), @streaming-sdk/abr (budget 5 KB). Vitest coverage report published as PR comment; 90% line coverage threshold enforced. Playwright integration test runs HLS manifest parse and EWMA recommendation in headless Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit to verify browser compatibility. No production telemetry emitted; library is instrumentation-free by design.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure costs: library is distributed via npm registry and JSDelivr CDN with no self-hosted components. GitHub Actions CI minutes are the only direct cost (~$10/month for open-source project). npm registry storage is free for public packages. JSDelivr CDN is free for open-source npm packages. Scaling is handled entirely by npm CDN infrastructure.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): @streaming-sdk/hls with M3U8 master and media playlist parsers, basic segment scheduler, TypeScript type exports, Vitest unit tests with HLS test fixture corpus, size-limit CI enforcement, npm publish pipeline with provenance attestation. Phase 2 (weeks 7-12): @streaming-sdk/abr with EWMA estimator and rendition recommendation, @streaming-sdk/dash with MPD XML parser and ContentProtection extractor, Playwright browser integration tests, DRM initialization data extraction for Widevine and FairPlay. Phase 3 (weeks 13-18): LL-HLS blocking playlist reload support in segment scheduler, CMAF chunk-transfer mode, DASH multi-period support, React Native bridge module as optional peer dependency, documentation site integration with live code examples.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with no-explicit-any enforced via ESLint. All public API types must have JSDoc comments for IDE IntelliSense. Zero runtime dependencies enforced by package.json peerDependencies-only policy and size-limit in CI. M3U8 parser must be tested against the Apple HLS sample stream corpus and the BBC HLS test fixtures. EWMA estimator unit tests must verify convergence behavior under simulated bandwidth step changes. CHANGELOG generated automatically by semantic-release from conventional commits; no manual CHANGELOG edits. npm publish triggered only on release/vX.Y.Z branch merge, never from feature branches.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DASH MPD schema complexity (dozens of optional attributes per element) may lead to incomplete parsing coverage that frustrates DASH player developers. Mitigation: publish a conformance test matrix showing which DASH profile features are covered and accept community PRs for gaps. Risk: TypeScript strict type exports may break consumer projects using lenient TypeScript configs on minor version updates. Mitigation: semver discipline with breaking type changes in major versions only; use @typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-assignment rules in test suite to catch type regressions. Tradeoff: function-oriented API over class hierarchy loses method chaining ergonomics but gains tree-shakeability and testability without class mocking.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: DASH MPD parser will use hand-authored TypeScript interfaces rather than schema-generated types to keep the package zero-dependency. tsup chosen for dual ESM/CJS build over native TypeScript project references for simpler monorepo configuration. npm provenance attestation chosen over Sigstore manual signing for easier consumer verification.

project.goal: B2B landing page for video watermarking SDK with free-trial sign-up
project.target_users: Backend engineers and compliance leads at streaming platforms

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with brand design tokens
frontend.code_snippets: Shiki or Prism.js for syntax highlighting in Python, Java, Node.js

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: next build --export, Vercel deploy
