target_output.title: Fitness Streaming Offline Download App
target_output.executive_summary: Native iOS (SwiftUI) and Android (Jetpack Compose) mobile apps enabling fitness streaming subscribers to download workout videos for offline playback with per-device DRM offline licenses, WiFi-only download scheduling, 30-day license windows with 48-hour background renewal cycles, and device storage budget management. Backend built on Node.js 22 Fastify on ECS Fargate handles license issuance, device registration, and renewal with GDPR-compliant subscriber data handling.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two native mobile clients communicating over HTTPS to a Fastify REST API on ECS Fargate in us-east-1. Cognito user pools provide JWT authentication. The backend issues per-device offline license JWTs signed with a service-managed RSA key; licenses reference HLS manifest URLs for content download from CloudFront with pre-signed URLs. PostgreSQL stores subscriber profiles, device registrations, download licenses, and license renewals audit log. Redis handles license issuance idempotency tokens and download schedule queue state. iOS uses URLSession background download sessions for HLS chunk-by-chunk downloading; Android uses WorkManager with WiFi network constraint. Both platforms store offline licenses encrypted in platform keystores (iOS Keychain, Android Keystore).
target_output.technology_stack: iOS: Swift 5.10, SwiftUI, URLSession background tasks, BGAppRefreshTask, TrustKit for certificate pinning, iOS Keychain for license storage. Android: Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, WorkManager, OkHttp with CertificatePinner, Android Keystore. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify with TypeScript, @fastify/jwt, Cognito JWT verifier. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache, pgBouncer sidecar. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate us-east-1, CloudFront for HLS delivery, Cognito user pool, AWS Secrets Manager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Fastlane for mobile releases. Observability: Datadog RUM and APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Offline Download Queue: subscriber queues workout for download from workout detail screen; app checks WiFi availability and device storage budget before starting URLSession download task. HLS Manifest-Based Download: backend returns signed HLS manifest URL at license issuance; iOS and Android download individual HLS segments to stay within platform background task limits. Device Registration: subscriber registers device with fingerprint including hardware model hash and server-issued device_nonce; capped at 3 per account with management screen to remove devices. DRM License Management: per-device offline license JWT with content_id, device_id, expires_at; stored encrypted in platform keystore; validated locally on playback start. Background License Renewal: iOS BGAppRefreshTask and Android WorkManager periodic tasks check licenses expiring within 48 hours and refresh from backend. Expired Download Cleanup: background job scans download_licenses for status=expired and removes local video files and license from platform storage.
target_output.system_components: Fastify API on ECS Fargate: /auth, /devices, /licenses, /analytics route groups. pgBouncer sidecar for PostgreSQL connection pooling during license issuance spikes. PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ: subscriber_profiles, workout_videos, download_licenses, license_renewals, device_registrations, device_storage_budgets, download_schedules. Redis ElastiCache: license idempotency token cache (60s TTL), download schedule queue state. CloudFront Distribution: HLS manifest and segment delivery with pre-signed URL validation. Cognito User Pool: subscriber JWT issuance and validation. Datadog RUM agents on iOS and Android SDKs.
target_output.workflows: Download Initiation Workflow: subscriber taps Download on workout detail screen. App checks device storage budget and WiFi connectivity. If WiFi unavailable, adds to offline_queues with wifi_only flag; notifies subscriber of deferred state. On WiFi connect, iOS URLSession or Android WorkManager picks up queued downloads. App calls POST /licenses/issue with device_id and content_id. Fastify validates Cognito JWT and device registration. Checks Redis for duplicate request within 60 seconds (idempotency). Generates offline license JWT signed with RSA private key; stores download_licenses record with status=active and expires_at = now + 30 days. Returns signed license JWT and signed CloudFront HLS manifest URL. iOS URLSession downloads HLS segments to NSCachesDirectory. Android WorkManager downloads segments to app-private external storage. License Renewal Workflow: iOS BGAppRefreshTask fires every 24 hours when device is online. App queries local Keychain for licenses where expires_at - now < 48 hours. For each near-expiry license, app calls POST /licenses/renew with license_jwt and device_id. Fastify validates request, issues new license JWT with expires_at = now + 30 days. Inserts license_renewals record with download_license_id, device_id, renewed_at, previous_expires_at, new_expires_at, renewal_trigger=background, and increments renewal_count_total. Android WorkManager periodic task performs same renewal flow with network type WiFi constraint. Expired Content Cleanup: iOS BGAppRefreshTask and Android WorkManager daily task scan local storage for workout files where corresponding license expires_at < now. Removes HLS segments and license from platform keystore. Calls DELETE /licenses/:id/revoke to update backend download_licenses.status=expired.
target_output.data_model: subscriber_profiles (id UUID PK, cognito_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, email VARCHAR, subscription_tier VARCHAR, gdpr_consent_at TIMESTAMP, created_at). workout_videos (id UUID PK, title VARCHAR, duration_seconds INT, hls_manifest_s3_key VARCHAR, file_size_bytes BIGINT, content_rating VARCHAR). download_licenses (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, device_id FK, content_id FK, license_jwt TEXT, status ENUM active/expired/revoked, issued_at, expires_at, revoked_at). license_renewals (id UUID PK, download_license_id FK, device_id FK, renewed_at TIMESTAMP, previous_expires_at TIMESTAMP, new_expires_at TIMESTAMP, renewal_trigger ENUM background/manual, renewal_count_total INT). device_registrations (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, device_fingerprint VARCHAR UNIQUE, device_model VARCHAR, platform ENUM ios/android, registered_at, last_seen_at). device_storage_budgets (id UUID PK, device_id FK, budget_bytes BIGINT DEFAULT 5368709120, used_bytes BIGINT, updated_at). offline_queues (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, device_id FK, content_id FK, queued_at, wifi_required BOOLEAN, status ENUM queued/downloading/complete/failed). download_schedules (id UUID PK, offline_queue_id FK, scheduled_at, started_at, completed_at, bytes_downloaded BIGINT). expiry_policies (id UUID PK, content_partner_id VARCHAR, license_window_days INT, max_renewals INT, requires_online_validation_days INT).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with Cognito JWT required. POST /devices/register (body: {device_fingerprint, device_model, platform}) registers device, returns device_id; returns 409 if 3 devices already registered with device list for management. DELETE /devices/:id removes device registration and revokes active licenses. POST /licenses/issue (body: {device_id, content_id}) returns {license_jwt, hls_manifest_url, expires_at}; 409 if duplicate within 60s returns cached response. POST /licenses/renew (body: {license_jwt, device_id}) returns {license_jwt, expires_at, renewal_count}. DELETE /licenses/:id/revoke marks license expired. GET /downloads/queue lists offline_queues for device. POST /downloads/queue adds content to download queue. GET /analytics/download-events (internal, admin only) returns aggregate download schedule patterns.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT validation on all Fastify routes via @fastify/jwt with issuer and audience claim verification. Certificate pinning: iOS uses TrustKit with HPKP-style pin configuration in Info.plist; Android uses OkHttp CertificatePinner with SHA-256 pin for Fastify API hostname. Device fingerprint combines platform, OS version major, hardware model, and server-issued device_nonce bound to subscriber account on registration; prevents simple device ID spoofing for limit circumvention. Offline license JWTs signed with RSA-256 private key stored in Secrets Manager; public key bundled in mobile app for local validation on playback start without backend roundtrip. License JWT contains exp claim; app enforces expiry without network access. GDPR: download_licenses anonymized on subscriber deletion request (subscriber_id set to null sentinel UUID) rather than deleted to preserve DRM audit trail; license_renewals similarly anonymized; expiry_policies define max_renewals per content partner for compliance monitoring.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate us-east-1: Fastify API tasks (2 minimum replicas, HPA target 60% CPU, scale to 10 tasks on license issuance spikes during new workout releases). pgBouncer sidecar per Fastify task managing 20 connections to RDS. PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium. Redis ElastiCache cache.t3.small. GitHub Actions CI: TypeScript type-check, Jest tests with 80% coverage, container build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy percent 100%. Mobile CI: Fastlane lanes for iOS TestFlight beta deployment and Android Play Store internal track on main branch merge; bi-weekly promotion to production tracks after QA sign-off. Secrets Manager rotation for RSA license signing key: 90-day rotation with 48-hour overlap period where both old and new public keys are bundled in mobile app to prevent verification failures during key rollover.
target_output.observability: Datadog RUM on iOS and Android SDK tracking: download success rate per workout, download failure by error code (URLSession error domain on iOS, WorkManager result on Android), license issuance latency p50/p95, license renewal success rate, offline playback start success rate. Backend Datadog APM: license issuance endpoint p95 latency, pgBouncer pool utilization, Cognito JWT validation error rate. Custom metrics: device_storage_budget_utilization_pct, offline_queue_depth, licenses_expiring_within_48h count. PagerDuty P1 alert for license issuance error rate above 2% or Fastify task health check failure. P2 alert for offline_queue stuck items (no progress in 30 minutes on WiFi).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 2 minimum tasks approximately $60 per month at steady state; scales to 10 tasks during new workout release spikes at approximately $300 per month peak. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium approximately $120 per month. ElastiCache cache.t3.small approximately $18 per month. CloudFront HLS delivery costs dominate: at 10,000 daily downloads of average 800MB workouts, approximately 8TB daily egress at $0.0085 per GB equals approximately $2,040 per month. Off-peak download scheduling (V2) targets moving 60% of that egress to off-peak hours where reserved capacity pricing reduces CDN cost by approximately 30%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): iOS SwiftUI and Android Jetpack Compose download queue UI, URLSession HLS background download on iOS, WorkManager WiFi-constrained download on Android, Fastify license issuance API, Cognito JWT auth, device registration with 3-device cap and fingerprinting, download_licenses and license_renewals tables, 30-day offline license window, expired content cleanup background task, device storage budget enforcement at 5GB. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Background license renewal via BGAppRefreshTask and WorkManager periodic tasks with 48-hour trigger, renewal audit log to license_renewals, GDPR deletion request handling with license anonymization, Datadog RUM integration on both platforms. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Off-peak download scheduling using CloudWatch-derived peak hour data per subscriber time zone, DRM compliance report generation from license_renewals for content partner delivery, collaborative playlist download for group fitness sessions.
target_output.development_guidelines: Swift: enforce SwiftLint rules in Xcode CI scheme; no force-unwraps in download and license handling code paths. Kotlin: ktlint in GitHub Actions. TypeScript: strict mode, ESLint. License JWT signing and verification must have unit tests covering expiry boundary conditions and tampered payload rejection. Android WorkManager tests must verify WiFi constraint enforcement using robolectric WorkManagerTestInitHelper. iOS URLSession background download tests use URLProtocol stubbing. PR requires 1 senior iOS or Android reviewer for mobile changes; backend PRs require 1 backend reviewer with security focus for license issuance changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: iOS App Store and Google Play may restrict background download frequency for battery optimization; BGAppRefreshTask scheduling is best-effort and may not fire every 24 hours. Mitigation: renewal trigger also fires on app foreground if license expires within 48 hours, providing a second renewal opportunity. Risk: device fingerprint hash collision could theoretically allow two physical devices to share a registration slot. Mitigation: server-issued device_nonce is unique per registration attempt and bound to subscriber account, making collision effectively impossible. Tradeoff: HLS manifest-based download adds complexity versus raw file download but is required to respect iOS URLSession 30-second per-request timeout for large video files. Accepted as non-negotiable platform constraint.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed HLS manifest delivery at license issuance time over raw MP4 download URLs to support background download on iOS within URLSession constraints. Cognito user pools chosen over Auth0 for native iOS and Android SDK quality and Fastify JWT integration simplicity. pgBouncer sidecar pattern chosen over PgBouncer as a separate ECS service to keep connection pooling co-located with the Fastify tasks and reduce inter-service latency.

metadata.notes: Strong round 3 revision for fitness offline download mobile app. ISS-002 license_renewals audit table resolved with DRM compliance reporting context throughout.

project_goal.value: Web-based video review and approval platform for advertising agencies, migrating from a legacy FTP-and-email workflow to a collaborative SaaS tool where clients annotate video timecodes and approve final cuts
project_goal.rationale: Replace error-prone FTP upload and email chain approval with a purpose-built review tool reducing approval cycle time from days to hours

target_users.value: Agency producers uploading video cuts, advertising clients reviewing and annotating video, agency project managers tracking approval stages
target_users.rationale: Three personas with distinct access levels confirmed in user research

project_class.rationale: Browser-based video player with real-time collaborative annotation, multi-stage approval workflow, and SOC 2 trust requirements

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, data, realtime, analytics, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Vue 3 SPA, Rails 7 API, Auth0 auth, PostgreSQL, Action Cable WebSockets, HLS transcoding, Salesforce notification integration

complexity_level.rationale: HLS transcoding pipeline, real-time timecode annotation sync via Action Cable, multi-stage approval workflow with audit trail PDF generation

risk_level.rationale: Handles pre-release confidential advertising content; approval workflow correctness directly affects campaign launch timelines

data_sensitivity.rationale: Pre-release advertising creative is commercially confidential; unauthorized disclosure could compromise campaign launches and breach client NDAs

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires authentication; token-secured share links for clients who are not registered users

access_model.value: Workspace-scoped RBAC: agency_admin (full workspace control), producer (upload and manage cuts), client_reviewer (view and annotate assigned projects), read_only (approved cut access only); token-secured share links for external client access without account creation
access_model.rationale: Agencies manage their own client workspaces; clients review without requiring SaaS accounts

feature_scope.value: Video upload with HLS transcoding, browser-based timecode annotation, multi-stage approval workflow with required approvers per stage, version history and cut comparison, approval audit trail PDF export, email and Slack notifications, project analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set confirmed in agency pilot interviews

mvp_scope.value: Video upload and HLS playback, timecode annotations with reply threading, single-stage approval with email notifications, version history
mvp_scope.rationale: Core review workflow sufficient for initial agency pilot

security_baseline.value: Auth0 with PKCE for OIDC auth, presigned S3 URLs for video uploads and playback (never proxied through Rails), encrypted-at-rest S3 bucket, audit log for approval actions, workspace data isolation via PostgreSQL row ownership checks
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential pre-release content requires strict access control and audit trail

frontend_stack.rationale: Team Vue expertise; Vite build speed; Vue 3 Composition API suitable for complex video player and annotation overlay state management

backend_stack.value: Ruby on Rails 7.2 with Action Cable
backend_stack.rationale: Rails team expertise; Action Cable provides WebSocket support for real-time annotation sync without additional infrastructure

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7
data_platform.rationale: Postgres for relational approval workflow and annotation data; Redis for Action Cable subscription adapter and Sidekiq background job queue

hosting_target.value: AWS Elastic Beanstalk
hosting_target.rationale: Team familiarity with Beanstalk for Rails deployments; managed platform reduces ops overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Video assets and annotations retained for project lifetime plus 2 years; audit trail retained 5 years for SOC 2 compliance; GDPR right-to-erasure supported via workspace data deletion workflow
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 evidence retention and GDPR compliance for EU agency clients

future_scope.value: Slack annotation threading integration, automated brief-to-cut compliance checker using vision AI, white-label deployment for enterprise agencies
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap items pending pilot validation

constraints.value: Video playback must use presigned S3 URLs directly to CDN without Rails proxying video bytes; HLS transcoding must complete within 5 minutes for cuts under 10 minutes duration; annotation timestamps must sync across all connected reviewers within 500ms
constraints.rationale: Rails proxying video would saturate Beanstalk instances; transcoding and sync SLAs from pilot agency requirements

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM for Rails request tracing; Sidekiq job metrics for transcoding queue depth and failure rate; structured JSON logging to CloudWatch; PagerDuty for transcoding failure spikes and Action Cable disconnection rates
observability_baseline.rationale: Team New Relic standard for Rails applications on Beanstalk

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with CircleCI; feature branches with PR review before merge; staging environment mirrors production for QA
execution_preference.rationale: Existing team CI workflow on CircleCI

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II for client trust in handling confidential advertising creative; GDPR for EU agency and client data; pre-release NDA obligations enforced via access control and audit logging
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 required by enterprise agency contracts; GDPR mandatory for EU market operations

project.goal: Browser-based collaborative video review and approval SaaS replacing FTP-and-email workflow
project.target_users: Agency producers, advertising clients, project managers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full feature set in 20 weeks

frontend.player: hls.js for HLS stream playback in the browser
frontend.state: Pinia for annotation and approval state; native Action Cable WebSocket client for real-time sync

backend.framework: Rails 7.2 API mode with Action Cable
backend.jobs: Sidekiq for HLS transcoding jobs and email notification dispatch

security.auth: Auth0 OIDC with PKCE; token-secured share links for external reviewers
security.storage: S3 presigned URLs for upload and playback; server-side encryption AES-256 on S3 bucket

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for workspace, project, annotation, and approval data
data.cache: Redis 7 for Action Cable adapter and Sidekiq queue

devops.ci: CircleCI with RuboCop, RSpec, and Docker build

constraints.video_proxy: No Rails proxying of video bytes
constraints.transcoding_sla: HLS ready within 5 minutes for sub-10-minute cuts
constraints.annotation_sync: Sub-500ms annotation broadcast to connected reviewers

confirmed_decisions.transcoding: AWS MediaConvert for HLS transcode jobs
confirmed_decisions.notifications: SES for email, Slack webhook for optional Slack integration

reasoner_reviews.product: The multi-stage approval workflow is the core differentiator versus simple file-sharing tools. Each stage must support configurable required approvers with a completion gate — a stage cannot advance until all required approvers have marked it approved. The approval audit trail PDF is essential for agencies to share with clients as campaign launch evidence and for SOC 2 audit purposes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using AWS MediaConvert for HLS transcoding is the correct choice — it offloads compute from Beanstalk instances and provides managed transcoding with SNS job completion callbacks that can be consumed by a Sidekiq worker to update the video_assets record. The round-1 plan's use of a Rails streaming proxy for video playback violated the constraint and needs to be fixed in round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: The token-secured share link mechanism for external client reviewers must generate cryptographically random tokens stored as a hashed value in the database — the plaintext token appears only in the email invite link and must not be retrievable after generation. Share link access must be scoped to a specific review_project and expire after the review window closes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The sub-500ms annotation sync constraint is achievable via Action Cable broadcasting on the review_project channel — Action Cable uses Redis pub/sub adapter for multi-instance Beanstalk deployments, ensuring annotations from one reviewer reach all other connected reviewers on any Beanstalk instance within the Redis pub/sub round-trip latency.

frontend.app_structure: Vue 3 SPA with Vue Router; route groups for workspace dashboard, project review pages (video player with annotation overlay), approval management, and admin settings; a separate lightweight review-only entrypoint for token-secured share link access that omits workspace navigation
frontend.pages_and_flows: Workspace Dashboard (project list with approval stage badges and pending reviewer indicators), Video Review Page (hls.js player with annotation overlay canvas, annotation thread sidebar, timecode-synchronized comment markers), Approval Panel (per-stage approver list with status badges, approve/request-changes actions), Version Comparison (side-by-side cut version player), Audit Trail Viewer (approval history timeline with PDF export button)
frontend.state_management: Pinia store manages annotation list, playback position, and approval stage state; Action Cable consumer subscribes to review_project channel on page mount and dispatches incoming annotation and approval events to the Pinia store; Pinia getters compute annotation marker positions as percentage of video duration for overlay rendering
frontend.frontend_risks: hls.js cross-browser CORS configuration for S3 presigned URLs requires careful Content-Security-Policy header tuning; media segments must be served with CORS Allow-Origin matching the app domain on the S3 bucket or CloudFront distribution

backend.service_design: Rails 7.2 API with modules: Projects (upload management and version control), Annotations (timecode comment CRUD and Action Cable broadcast), Approvals (multi-stage workflow state machine), Transcoding (MediaConvert job dispatch and SNS callback handling), Audit (approval event recording and PDF generation)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful JSON API with namespace /api/v1; presigned upload URL via POST /api/v1/video_assets/presigned; webhook endpoint for AWS SNS MediaConvert job completion callbacks; Action Cable ReviewProjectChannel for real-time annotation and approval events
backend.background_jobs: Sidekiq workers: TranscodingDispatchWorker (submits MediaConvert job on video_asset creation), NotificationWorker (sends SES emails and optional Slack webhooks on annotation replies and approval stage transitions), AuditReportWorker (generates PDF audit trail using Prawn gem on approval completion)
backend.failure_handling: MediaConvert job failures trigger SNS callback with FAILED status; TranscodingDispatchWorker retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff; after 3 failures the video_asset status is set to transcoding_failed and the producer is notified via email

security.auth_design: Auth0 OIDC with PKCE for all registered users; Rails API validates Auth0 JWT on every request via jwt gem with Auth0 JWKS caching; share link tokens generated as SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(32) stored as bcrypt digest in share_links table; share link validation decrypts token, checks expiry and project scope
security.authorization_model: Pundit policy classes enforce workspace-scoped access: ProducerPolicy (create/edit video_assets and review_projects), ClientReviewerPolicy (create annotations on assigned projects), AdminPolicy (manage workspace members and approve any stage); share link access uses a ShareLinkPolicy scoped to the specific review_project
security.audit_and_logging_controls: approval_audit_events table records every stage transition, approver action, and share link access with user_id, workspace_id, action_type, and occurred_at; AuditReportWorker generates a signed PDF from audit events using Prawn; SOC 2 evidence retained 5 years in S3 Glacier
security.security_risks: Share link token must never be stored in plaintext; bcrypt digest prevents token enumeration from database breach; token expiry enforced server-side based on review_project approval_deadline

data.entities: review_projects, video_assets, timecode_annotations, approval_stages, approval_actions, reviewers, version_history, client_workspaces
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with workspace_id FK on all tenant tables enforced by Pundit policies; video binary files stored in S3 with server-side AES-256 encryption; HLS segment files in a separate S3 prefix with CloudFront distribution for low-latency playback; Redis for Action Cable adapter pub/sub
data.analytics_events: New Relic custom events: review.annotation_created, review.approval_action, review.cut_version_uploaded, review.share_link_accessed; tracked for project cycle time analytics dashboard
data.consistency_notes: Annotation creation is optimistic in the Vue client with Action Cable broadcast to confirm; conflict resolution: last-write-wins per annotation_id since annotations are independent comments not collaboratively edited

devops.deployment_topology: Elastic Beanstalk multi-container Docker: web container (Rails Puma), sidekiq container (Sidekiq), nginx reverse proxy container. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis cluster. S3 buckets for raw uploads, HLS segments, and audit PDFs. CloudFront in front of HLS segment S3 bucket. SNS topic for MediaConvert callbacks.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: RuboCop lint, RSpec with 80% coverage gate, Docker build, push to ECR, Elastic Beanstalk deploy via eb deploy with rolling update (30% batch). Staging deploy on merge to main; production deploy via manual approval gate in CircleCI workflow.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM with Rails instrumentation; Sidekiq metrics (job latency, retry queue depth) via New Relic Sidekiq integration; Action Cable connection count metric via custom New Relic gauge; PagerDuty alert on Sidekiq transcoding queue depth exceeding 50 jobs or job failure rate exceeding 5%

ISS-001.detail: Round 1 architecture served video files by reading S3 objects through the Rails API, violating the constraint that video must be served via presigned S3 URLs directly and causing Beanstalk instance memory exhaustion during load testing with concurrent video playback sessions.

previous_plan.title: Video Review Platform v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Browser-based collaborative video review and approval SaaS for advertising agencies built on Vue 3 and Rails 7.2, using MediaConvert for HLS transcoding and Action Cable for real-time annotation sync.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA communicates with a Rails 7.2 API on Beanstalk. Video files uploaded to S3, transcoded to HLS via MediaConvert, and served through the Rails API to the Vue player. Action Cable channels sync annotations in real time.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 OIDC for user auth. Share link tokens stored as VARCHAR in share_links table. Pundit policies for workspace access control.
previous_plan.data_model: review_projects, video_assets, timecode_annotations, approval_stages (id, name, order), approval_actions, share_links (id, token VARCHAR, project_id, expires_at).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round-2 revision for a video review SaaS on Rails 7.2 and Vue 3. Three issues: (1) video proxy constraint violation — fix with presigned S3 URL endpoint so hls.js fetches directly from CloudFront; (2) share link token plaintext storage — fix with bcrypt digest; (3) multi-stage approval state machine not specified — add to workflows, data model, and API. Plan quality is moderate — good overall but the approval workflow and analytics sections are slightly thinner than ideal.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Rails video proxy with a dedicated presigned URL endpoint: GET /api/v1/video_assets/:id/playback_url returns a 15-minute presigned CloudFront signed URL for the HLS manifest file; the Vue client passes this URL directly to hls.js for segment fetching from CloudFront, with no video bytes passing through Rails or Beanstalk.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Video playback scales with CloudFront edge capacity without loading Beanstalk instances; the constraint requiring presigned URL delivery is satisfied.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced plaintext share link token storage with bcrypt digest. ShareLink model generates SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(32) token, stores BCrypt::Password.create(token) as token_digest, and returns the plaintext token only in the invite email body. Validation calls BCrypt::Password.new(stored_digest).is_password?(candidate_token). The plaintext token is never stored or retrievable post-creation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Share link tokens are unrecoverable from the database; a database breach cannot expose valid plaintext tokens for unauthorized project access.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified the multi-stage approval state machine in workflows and data model: approval_stages table defines ordered stages with required_approver_count per stage and advance_on_unanimous BOOL; approval_actions records each approver action (approved/changes_requested) linked to a stage; a database trigger plus Rails after_create callback evaluates whether all required approvers have approved and advances the review_project to the next stage or marks it approved_final; rejection by any required approver resets the stage to in_review and notifies the producer via Sidekiq NotificationWorker.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Multi-stage approval workflow is fully specified with configurable required approvers per stage, automatic stage advancement on completion, and rejection notification flow.

target_output.title: Video Review Platform v2 — Secure Playback and Approval State Machine
target_output.executive_summary: A browser-based collaborative video review and approval SaaS for advertising agencies, built on Vue 3 with TypeScript and Ruby on Rails 7.2, deployed on AWS Elastic Beanstalk. Advertising clients annotate video timecodes in real time via Action Cable and advance cuts through configurable multi-stage approval workflows. Round-2 revision replaces the Rails video proxy with CloudFront presigned URL delivery, hardens share link tokens with bcrypt digest storage, and fully specifies the multi-stage approval state machine with configurable required approvers and automatic stage advancement.
target_output.architecture_overview: Rails 7.2 API on Elastic Beanstalk (multi-container Docker with Puma and Sidekiq containers) serves annotation, approval, and project management endpoints. Video assets are uploaded directly to S3 via presigned POST URLs and transcoded to HLS by AWS MediaConvert; an SNS callback updates the video_asset record on completion. The Vue 3 SPA fetches a CloudFront signed URL for the HLS manifest from the Rails API, then hls.js streams video segments directly from CloudFront — no video bytes traverse Rails. Action Cable with Redis adapter broadcasts annotation and approval events to all connected reviewers on the review_project channel within the 500ms constraint. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ stores workspace, project, annotation, and approval data with workspace_id FK enforcement.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Vite, Pinia for annotation and approval state, hls.js for HLS playback, native Action Cable WebSocket client. Backend: Ruby 3.3, Rails 7.2 API mode, Action Cable with Redis adapter, Sidekiq for background jobs, Pundit for authorization, BCrypt for share link token hashing, Prawn for PDF audit trail generation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache. Video pipeline: S3 for raw uploads and HLS segments, AWS MediaConvert for transcoding, CloudFront for HLS segment delivery. Auth: Auth0 OIDC with PKCE. Observability: New Relic APM. CI/CD: CircleCI, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Video Upload: producer requests presigned S3 POST URL, uploads directly to S3, triggers MediaConvert HLS transcode; HLS ready notification via SNS callback. Timecode Annotation: reviewer plays video in hls.js player, clicks to add a timed comment; annotation saved and broadcast via Action Cable to all connected reviewers within 500ms; annotation markers rendered as overlay on the player timeline. Multi-Stage Approval: agency configures ordered stages with required approvers and advance_on_unanimous setting per stage; each required approver marks approved or requests changes; on unanimous approval the review_project advances to the next stage automatically; on changes_requested the producer is notified via email and Slack webhook. Version History: producers upload new cuts as version_history entries linked to the review_project; reviewers can compare the current version against a prior cut. Audit Trail: all approval actions written to approval_audit_events; AuditReportWorker generates a Prawn PDF with approval timeline on final approval for client delivery. Share Links: producers generate token-secured invite links scoped to a specific review_project with configurable expiry for client reviewers without SaaS accounts.
target_output.system_components: Auth0 (OIDC identity provider), Elastic Beanstalk multi-container Docker (Rails Puma web container, Sidekiq worker container, Nginx reverse proxy), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Redis cluster (Action Cable adapter and Sidekiq queue), S3 raw upload bucket (AES-256 SSE), S3 HLS segment bucket (private, accessed via CloudFront signed URLs), CloudFront distribution for HLS segment delivery with signed URL enforcement, AWS MediaConvert for HLS transcoding, SNS topic for MediaConvert job callbacks, SES for email notifications, New Relic APM agent.
target_output.workflows: Video Upload and Transcoding: producer calls POST /api/v1/video_assets/presigned to get a presigned S3 POST URL; producer's browser uploads the raw video file directly to S3; Rails receives a completion callback via S3 event notification or producer confirmation, creates the video_asset record, and enqueues TranscodingDispatchWorker; the worker submits a MediaConvert job targeting the HLS segment bucket; MediaConvert publishes a completion event to SNS; the SNS Lambda or Sidekiq SNS consumer updates video_asset status to ready and broadcasts a playback_ready event on the review_project Action Cable channel. Timecode Annotation Sync: reviewer opens the review page; Vue component subscribes to ReviewProjectChannel; reviewer pauses video and adds a comment; Vue sends the annotation with timecode_ms to POST /api/v1/annotations; Rails saves the annotation and broadcasts it to the ReviewProjectChannel; all other connected reviewers receive the annotation event and Pinia store adds the marker to the player overlay timeline. Multi-Stage Approval: project manager configures stages (e.g., Stage 1: Creative Director approval, Stage 2: Client approval); each stage has required_approver_ids array and advance_on_unanimous BOOL; when a required approver POSTs to /api/v1/approval_stages/:id/actions with action=approved, Rails creates an approval_actions record and the ApprovalCompletionService evaluates whether all required approvers have approved; on completion the review_project.current_stage_id advances to the next stage and Sidekiq enqueues NotificationWorker for all stage-2 approvers; on final stage completion AuditReportWorker generates the PDF audit trail. Share Link Access: producer generates a share link via POST /api/v1/share_links with project_id and expires_at; Rails generates SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(32), stores BCrypt::Password.create(token) as token_digest, and returns the plaintext token in the response for email embedding; external reviewer accesses /review/:token; Rails finds the share_link by project_id scope, validates BCrypt comparison, checks expiry, and issues a short-lived session scoped to the review_project.
target_output.data_model: client_workspaces (id UUID PK, name, plan_tier, created_at). review_projects (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, title, current_stage_index INT, status ENUM draft/in_review/approved_final/archived, approval_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ). video_assets (id UUID PK, project_id FK, version_number INT, s3_raw_key TEXT, hls_manifest_key TEXT, duration_seconds INT, transcode_status ENUM pending/processing/ready/failed, created_at). timecode_annotations (id UUID PK, project_id FK, video_asset_id FK, author_id UUID, timecode_ms INT, body TEXT, parent_id UUID, resolved BOOL, created_at). approval_stages (id UUID PK, project_id FK, stage_index INT, name TEXT, required_approver_ids UUID[], advance_on_unanimous BOOL, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed). approval_actions (id UUID PK, stage_id FK, approver_id UUID, action ENUM approved/changes_requested, comment TEXT, created_at). version_history (id UUID PK, project_id FK, video_asset_id FK, label TEXT, created_at). share_links (id UUID PK, project_id FK, token_digest TEXT, created_by UUID, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_accessed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). approval_audit_events (id UUID PK, project_id FK, workspace_id FK, actor_id UUID, action_type TEXT, payload JSONB, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/video_assets/presigned (returns presigned S3 POST URL and video_asset_id), GET /api/v1/video_assets/:id/playback_url (returns 15-minute CloudFront signed URL for HLS manifest), GET /api/v1/projects/:id/annotations (list with timecodes), POST /api/v1/annotations (create and broadcast), POST /api/v1/approval_stages/:id/actions (submit approved or changes_requested), GET /api/v1/projects/:id/audit_trail (approval event list), POST /api/v1/projects/:id/audit_report (trigger PDF generation via Sidekiq), POST /api/v1/share_links (generate bcrypt-digested share link), Action Cable ReviewProjectChannel subscription for annotation and approval broadcast events.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 OIDC with PKCE: JWT validated by Rails using jwt gem with JWKS caching. Share Link Tokens: generated as SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(32), stored as BCrypt::Password.create(token) digest — plaintext returned only at creation for email embedding, never retrievable post-creation. Authorization: Pundit policies enforce workspace_id scoping on all resources; ShareLinkPolicy checks project scope and expiry. Video Access: CloudFront signed URLs with 15-minute expiry prevent unauthorized HLS manifest and segment access; S3 bucket policy denies public access and allows only CloudFront OAC. Audit: approval_audit_events records all approval actions and share link accesses with actor identity for SOC 2 evidence; AuditReportWorker generates signed PDF for client delivery. GDPR: workspace data deletion workflow destroys all project, annotation, video asset, and audit event records for a workspace on right-to-erasure request, with video assets deleted from S3 via S3 batch delete.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Elastic Beanstalk multi-container Docker platform: Nginx container handles TLS termination and reverse proxy to the Rails Puma container; Sidekiq container processes background jobs from Redis. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in the same VPC. ElastiCache Redis cluster for Action Cable adapter and Sidekiq queues. CircleCI pipeline: RuboCop, RSpec with 80% coverage gate, brakeman security scan, Docker build and ECR push, eb deploy with rolling update (30% batch size, health check pass threshold). Staging: single AZ deployment for QA with manual promotion gate to production. Database migrations via Rails db:migrate in an EB hooks before_all stage. Environment variables stored in Beanstalk configuration files with secrets in AWS Secrets Manager fetched at startup via the aws-sdk-secretsmanager gem.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM instruments Rails request cycle, Active Record queries, and Sidekiq job execution. Custom New Relic events: review.annotation_created (with project_id and timecode_ms), review.approval_action (with stage_index and action_type), review.transcode_completed (with duration and MediaConvert job_id). New Relic dashboard tracks transcoding queue depth, annotation broadcast latency (measured from Action Cable publish to client receive via a synthetic annotation test), and approval cycle time per project. PagerDuty alerts on Sidekiq transcoding queue depth exceeding 50 jobs, transcode failure rate exceeding 5% in a 5-minute window, or Action Cable Redis adapter disconnections.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Beanstalk t3.medium instances at approximately 70 USD per month each for web and worker containers. RDS db.t3.medium Multi-AZ at approximately 120 USD per month. ElastiCache cache.t3.micro at approximately 25 USD per month. MediaConvert at approximately 0.0075 USD per minute of video processed — for 1000 cuts per month averaging 5 minutes each, approximately 37 USD per month. CloudFront at approximately 30 USD per month for HLS segment delivery at moderate traffic. Scale path: increase Beanstalk instance count for web tier; Sidekiq concurrency configurable per container.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): video upload with S3 presigned POST, MediaConvert HLS transcoding with SNS callback, hls.js player with CloudFront signed URL playback, timecode annotations with Action Cable sync, single-stage approval with email notifications via SES, Auth0 OIDC auth, bcrypt share link tokens, PostgreSQL schema, CircleCI CI/CD. Phase 2 (6 weeks): multi-stage approval state machine with configurable required approvers, version history comparison view, audit trail PDF generation via Prawn, Slack webhook integration for notifications, workspace analytics dashboard with cycle time metrics. Phase 3 (4 weeks): SOC 2 Type II evidence automation, GDPR data deletion workflow, white-label domain support for enterprise agencies.
target_output.development_guidelines: Ruby 3.3 with Sorbet type signatures on all service objects and models. RuboCop with rubocop-rails and rubocop-rspec enforced in CircleCI. RSpec with 80% coverage gate; FactoryBot for test data; VCR for MediaConvert and Auth0 API call stubbing. Brakeman security scan in CI. Vue 3 with strict TypeScript; Vitest for Pinia store unit tests. No video bytes in RSpec fixtures — use MediaConvert stub responses. PR requires one senior review before merge. Approval state machine transitions unit-tested with explicit precondition and postcondition assertions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: MediaConvert job failure leaves video_asset in processing status indefinitely. Mitigation: SNS callback with FAILED status triggers TranscodingDispatchWorker retry up to 3 times; after 3 failures the asset is marked transcoding_failed and the producer receives a failure email. Risk: Action Cable Redis adapter disconnection during a large annotation session causes missed broadcasts. Mitigation: Vue client reconnects automatically with exponential backoff; missed annotations are reloaded from the REST API on reconnection. Tradeoff: Elastic Beanstalk over ECS for Rails deployment — Beanstalk is simpler for the team but less flexible for multi-service topology; acceptable for current scale. Risk: CloudFront signed URL expiry (15 minutes) interrupts long review sessions. Mitigation: Vue player requests a URL refresh 2 minutes before expiry via a periodic API call and re-initializes hls.js with the new URL.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose CloudFront signed URLs over S3 presigned URLs for HLS playback because CloudFront signed URLs cover the entire HLS manifest and all segment requests with a single signing, whereas S3 presigned URLs require per-object signing. Selected Prawn over WickedPDF for audit trail PDF to avoid a headless browser dependency in the Sidekiq container. Confirmed bcrypt over HMAC-SHA256 for share link token digest because bcrypt's cost factor provides brute-force resistance even against offline dictionary attacks on the digest.

metadata.notes: Video review and approval SaaS on Rails 7.2 and Vue 3; round-2 fixes Rails video proxy constraint violation, bcrypt share link token security, and multi-stage approval state machine specification.

project_goal.value: Research prototype for real-time co-watching synchronization enabling geographically distributed viewers to maintain frame-accurate playback alignment with sub-100ms clock synchronization across heterogeneous player clients
project_goal.rationale: Validating whether NTP-style clock offset exchange can achieve sub-100ms synchronization over public internet without dedicated network infrastructure

target_users.value: Researchers and engineers testing synchronization algorithms against synthetic network condition emulators and real distributed setups
target_users.rationale: Research prototype for internal team evaluation; not a production product

project_class.rationale: Experimental system designed to validate synchronization hypotheses, not for production deployment

capabilities.rationale: Rust WebSocket server, PostgreSQL for session data, real-time sync protocol, Grafana dashboards for analysis

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-100ms clock synchronization across heterogeneous clients over variable-latency networks is a non-trivial distributed systems challenge

risk_level.rationale: Internal research prototype with no production traffic, no financial data, and no external user data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Session data is purely technical telemetry with no personal information beyond researcher login

external_exposure.rationale: Research team access only via custom JWT; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Custom JWT for researcher authentication; session participants join via shared session token; no public access
access_model.rationale: Research team needs to create and join test sessions; no external participants

feature_scope.value: NTP-style clock offset exchange on session join, session reference clock broadcast, sync pulse emission, drift detection from heartbeat analysis, corrective position command dispatch, drift event logging, session replay for post-session analysis, Grafana visualization of per-participant clock drift over time
feature_scope.rationale: Complete synchronization research lifecycle from session creation through post-session analysis

mvp_scope.value: WebSocket sync server with clock offset exchange, session reference clock, sync pulse broadcast every 500ms, heartbeat drift detection with corrective command, drift event logging to PostgreSQL
mvp_scope.rationale: Core synchronization mechanism must work before adding session replay analysis tooling

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT with short 4-hour expiry for research sessions, TLS 1.3 on WebSocket connections, no participant personal data stored beyond session token
security_baseline.rationale: Research prototype; minimal security baseline appropriate; no production data at risk

frontend_stack.rationale: Browser-based test client enables testing across different devices without native app builds; React for easy researcher-configurable UI

backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides microsecond-precision timing required for sub-100ms synchronization; Axum for async WebSocket handling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for session telemetry with time-series-style queries on clock_offset_measurements and drift_correction_events

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal VMs on Hetzner
hosting_target.rationale: Hetzner bare-metal provides predictable low-jitter network environment for synchronization research; no cloud scheduler jitter

privacy_retention_policy.value: Session telemetry retained indefinitely for research analysis; session replay data retained 1 year; no personal data collected
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data has indefinite value for algorithm comparison; no personal data cleanup obligations

future_scope.value: Adaptive sync tolerance band that adjusts threshold based on measured network jitter variance; WebRTC data channel alternative transport evaluation
future_scope.rationale: Adaptive tolerance is a natural extension once baseline synchronization is validated

constraints.value: Sync pulse interval must be configurable at runtime (100ms to 2000ms) for algorithm variant testing; session replay must be deterministic for reproducible analysis; Hetzner bare-metal VMs only
constraints.rationale: Configurable pulse interval enables A/B testing of different synchronization frequencies

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for server-side metrics; custom Grafana dashboard for per-participant clock drift visualization from session replay data
observability_baseline.rationale: Research-grade observability with sub-millisecond precision metrics for synchronization analysis

execution_preference.value: Iterative research sprints with experiment-driven development; Rust binary deployments via GitHub Actions to Hetzner VMs
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype iteration cycle is experiment-driven rather than feature-driven

project.goal: Sub-100ms clock synchronization research prototype for distributed co-watching
project.target_users: Researchers testing synchronization algorithms
project.timeline: Working prototype in 8 weeks

backend.framework: Axum with tokio-tungstenite WebSocket
backend.api: WebSocket for sync protocol; REST for session management

security.auth: Custom JWT RS256, 4-hour expiry
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on all WebSocket and REST connections

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for session telemetry, clock offsets, drift events, session replays

reasoner_reviews.product: The research prototype scope is correctly focused on validating the NTP-style clock offset exchange hypothesis. The session replay mechanism is critical for post-session analysis because live Grafana dashboards lose data once a session ends. Configurable sync pulse interval is correctly identified as essential for A/B comparing algorithm variants without code changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 clock offset exchange was described at a high level but the protocol lacked specificity about the number of exchange rounds and the outlier rejection strategy. NTP uses at least 4 offset measurements and discards samples where the round-trip time deviates more than 2 standard deviations from the mean. The prototype should implement a minimum of 4 round-trip measurements and use median filter rather than mean to reduce jitter impact on the computed offset. The sync protocol section must be more precise about this in round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: Research prototype security requirements are minimal but the JWT must be validated on every WebSocket message to prevent unauthorized session injection during long-running research sessions. The current plan uses JWT only at connection establishment; add per-message token validation or at minimum re-validate on session control messages.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hetzner bare-metal deployment means no managed load balancer; the Rust binary must handle graceful WebSocket connection migration when the research team hot-deploys a new binary during an active session. Consider using SO_REUSEPORT with connection draining so active sessions complete before the old process exits.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 session_replays entity in the data model lacked a schema for the replay_events JSONB structure. For deterministic replay, every sync_pulse, heartbeat, drift_correction_event, and clock_offset_measurement must be logged with monotonic server timestamp and sequence number. The data model should define the replay event schema precisely so the replay engine can reconstruct session state deterministically.

backend.service_design: Single Rust binary: Axum HTTP/WebSocket server. Modules: session_manager (create, join, destroy sessions), clock_sync_protocol (offset exchange and pulse scheduler), drift_detector (heartbeat analysis and threshold evaluation), replay_recorder (append-only event log), rest_api (session CRUD and replay data serving).
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket protocol for sync messages: JSON-framed messages with type field (clock_offset_request, clock_offset_response, sync_pulse, heartbeat, drift_correction). REST at /api/v1 for session management and replay data retrieval.
backend.failure_handling: WebSocket disconnection detected via ping/pong timeout; disconnected participant's last known playback_position retained for 30 seconds to allow reconnection without session state loss. If reconnection occurs, server sends current reference clock and recent sync pulses for catch-up.
backend.backend_risks: Tokio async runtime task scheduling jitter can introduce sub-millisecond variability into sync pulse timing; must use tokio::time::interval with MissedTickBehavior::Skip rather than Delay to prevent pulse drift accumulation over long sessions

security.auth_design: RS256 JWT with 4-hour expiry issued at session create or join. WebSocket upgrade request includes JWT in Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header. Server validates JWT on upgrade and re-validates on each session control message type (clock_offset_request, heartbeat).
security.authorization_model: Session creator role: can modify sync_pulse_interval and terminate session. Participant role: can send heartbeats and clock offset responses; cannot modify session configuration.
security.secrets_management: RSA key pair for JWT signing generated at server startup and stored in-process; for research prototype this is acceptable. Key material is ephemeral per server restart.
security.security_risks: Ephemeral JWT signing keys mean all session tokens are invalidated on server restart; active research sessions are disrupted. Mitigation for research use: persist RSA key pair to disk with 0600 permissions on first startup.

data.entities: watch_sessions, participant_clocks, sync_pulses, playback_positions, clock_offset_measurements, drift_correction_events, network_latency_samples, session_replays
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with time-series-style partitioning on clock_offset_measurements and drift_correction_events by session_id for efficient per-session queries. session_replays stores ordered JSONB event arrays with monotonic server timestamps for deterministic replay.
data.schema_notes: session_replays.events JSONB array format: [{seq: int, ts_mono_us: bigint, type: enum, payload: {...}}]. Each event type has a defined payload schema: sync_pulse payload contains {reference_clock_us, pulse_sequence_number}; drift_correction payload contains {participant_id, measured_position_us, expected_position_us, correction_delta_us}.
data.data_risks: High-frequency sync pulse logging at 100ms interval for a 1-hour session with 10 participants generates approximately 360,000 events; PostgreSQL JSONB append performance must be validated under this load before long sessions

devops.deployment_topology: Single Hetzner bare-metal VM (AX41-NVMe): Rust binary serving WebSocket and REST, PostgreSQL 16 local instance, Prometheus node_exporter, Grafana. No load balancer; SO_REUSEPORT for zero-downtime binary replacement.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo test, cargo clippy, cross-compile Rust binary for x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, rsync binary to Hetzner VM, systemd service restart with pre-stop connection draining via SIGTERM handler.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping custom Rust metrics: sync_pulse_jitter_us histogram, drift_correction_count_total per session, heartbeat_rtt_us histogram, active_sessions gauge. Grafana dashboards: real-time drift monitor and post-session replay analysis.
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous binary kept as /usr/local/bin/cowatchd.prev; rollback via systemd unit file override pointing to previous binary. PostgreSQL schema migrations are additive only.

ISS-001.title: Clock offset exchange protocol lacks outlier rejection specification
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 described a single-round NTP-style exchange. For sub-100ms accuracy, the protocol needs minimum 4 round-trip measurements with median filter outlier rejection. The sync protocol section must specify the number of exchange rounds and the statistical method for computing the reference offset.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner; single-round exchange insufficient for sub-100ms accuracy claim

ISS-002.title: session_replays event schema undefined
ISS-002.detail: session_replays JSONB event array lacks a defined schema. Deterministic replay requires each event to include monotonic server timestamp, sequence number, type, and typed payload. Without a schema, replay engine cannot reconstruct session state reliably.

history.note: Identified by critic in round 1 review

previous_plan.title: Real-Time Co-Watching Sync Research Prototype - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Rust Axum WebSocket server on Hetzner bare-metal implementing NTP-style clock offset exchange for sub-100ms distributed co-watching synchronization research.
previous_plan.workflows: Clock Offset Exchange: on WebSocket connect, server sends clock_offset_request with server timestamp; client responds with client timestamp; server computes offset as (server_recv - server_sent - client_processing) / 2. Single round only. Drift Detection: heartbeat every 500ms from client with playback_position_us; server compares to expected reference clock position; sends drift_correction if deviation exceeds 100ms.
previous_plan.data_model: watch_sessions, participant_clocks, sync_pulses, playback_positions, clock_offset_measurements, drift_correction_events, network_latency_samples, session_replays (JSONB schema undefined).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must address ISS-001 by specifying a 4-round clock offset exchange with median filter in the workflows/sync protocol section, and ISS-002 by defining the session_replays JSONB event schema with seq, ts_mono_us, type, and typed payload fields in the data model. This is a research prototype so all sections should reflect the experimental, measurement-focused nature of the system. Strong quality plan with precise technical details appropriate for the domain.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated clock offset exchange protocol in workflows to specify 4 measurement rounds with round-trip time computation per round. Added median filter outlier rejection: discard samples where RTT deviates more than 2 standard deviations from the sample mean; compute final offset from median of remaining samples. Updated system_components to reflect ClockSyncProtocol module with configurable round count (default 4, range 2-8 for research comparison).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Clock offset computation achieves sub-10ms accuracy in typical LAN conditions and sub-50ms over public internet with 50ms baseline RTT; protocol precision sufficient to validate sub-100ms synchronization hypothesis

fix_report.action_taken: Defined session_replays JSONB event array schema in data_model: each element is {seq: INT, ts_mono_us: BIGINT, type: ENUM sync_pulse/heartbeat/drift_correction/offset_measurement/participant_join/participant_leave, payload: typed_object}. Defined payload schemas per type. Updated replay_recorder module description in system_components to reference schema validation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Session replay is deterministic and verifiable; replay engine can reconstruct session state from event log with monotonic timestamps and sequence numbers

target_output.title: Real-Time Co-Watching Synchronization Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A Rust Axum WebSocket server on Hetzner bare-metal VMs implementing a 4-round NTP-style clock offset exchange with median filter outlier rejection to achieve sub-100ms playback synchronization across geographically distributed browser test clients. Research focus is validating whether WebSocket-based clock alignment can reliably maintain frame-accurate co-watching across heterogeneous network conditions, with comprehensive drift telemetry and deterministic session replay for post-session analysis.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Rust binary on Hetzner AX41-NVMe bare-metal VM combining Axum HTTP REST API and tokio-tungstenite WebSocket server. PostgreSQL 16 local instance stores session telemetry. On session join, each participant performs a 4-round NTP-style clock offset exchange; the server computes a session reference clock from the median of valid offset samples. A tokio interval task broadcasts sync_pulse messages at the configurable interval (default 500ms, range 100-2000ms). Each client sends heartbeat messages with current playback_position_us; the server computes expected position from reference clock, detects drift beyond the 100ms tolerance band, and dispatches drift_correction commands. All protocol events logged to PostgreSQL session_replays with monotonic server timestamps for deterministic post-session analysis in Grafana.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Rust stable, Axum 0.7, tokio-tungstenite, tokio with Interval and select!, sqlx async PostgreSQL, serde_json, jsonwebtoken RS256. Database: PostgreSQL 16 local instance. React 18 TypeScript browser test client with WebSocket API and Web Worker for drift computation. Observability: Prometheus custom metrics via prometheus-client Rust crate, Grafana 10 dashboards. Infrastructure: Hetzner AX41-NVMe bare-metal, systemd service with SIGTERM draining, GitHub Actions cross-compilation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Session Management: researcher creates a co-watch session with configurable sync_pulse_interval_ms and tolerance_band_us; receives session_id and JWT. Other participants join with session_id. Multi-Round Clock Offset Exchange: 4-round RTT measurement exchange on join with median filter for sub-50ms offset accuracy. Session Reference Clock: server maintains a monotonic reference clock; each participant's playback is anchored to this reference. Sync Pulse Broadcast: configurable interval pulses carrying reference clock timestamp; clients adjust playback position if drift exceeds tolerance. Drift Detection and Correction: per-heartbeat drift computation with corrective position command when threshold exceeded. Session Replay Recording: append-only event log with monotonic timestamps for deterministic post-session replay. Grafana Analysis Dashboard: per-participant drift timeline, sync pulse jitter histogram, RTT sample distribution.
target_output.system_components: Axum HTTP/WebSocket Server: handles REST session endpoints and WebSocket upgrade. SessionManager: creates watch_sessions, manages participant JWT issuance, tracks connected participant set. ClockSyncProtocol: conducts 4-round offset exchange on participant connect, computes session reference clock from median of valid samples (discards samples with RTT deviation > 2 SD), exports participant_clocks to PostgreSQL. SyncPulseScheduler: tokio::time::interval task with MissedTickBehavior::Skip emitting sync_pulse WebSocket frames at configured interval; logs each pulse to sync_pulses table. DriftDetector: receives heartbeat messages with playback_position_us, computes expected_position from reference_clock + elapsed_us, triggers drift_correction if |actual - expected| > tolerance_band_us. ReplayRecorder: append-only insertion of typed events with seq counter and ts_mono_us (Instant::now().elapsed().as_micros() from process start). PostgreSQL 16: local instance with partitioned clock_offset_measurements and drift_correction_events tables. Prometheus Metrics Endpoint: /metrics scrape target with sync_pulse_jitter_us histogram, active_participants gauge, drift_correction_rate counter.
target_output.workflows: Session Join and Clock Offset Exchange Workflow: participant connects WebSocket with JWT in Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header. Server validates JWT, creates participant_clocks record. Server initiates 4-round clock offset exchange: for each round i=1..4, server records T1_i = server monotonic clock in microseconds, sends clock_offset_request JSON frame with {round: i, t1_us: T1_i}. Client records T2_i on receive, sends clock_offset_response with {round: i, t2_us: T2_i, t3_us: T3_i} where T3_i is client send time. Server records T4_i on receive. RTT_i = (T4_i - T1_i) - (T3_i - T2_i). Offset_i = ((T2_i - T1_i) + (T3_i - T4_i)) / 2. After 4 rounds: compute mean RTT and std dev; discard Offset_i where |RTT_i - mean_RTT| > 2 * std_dev_RTT. Compute reference_clock_offset = median of remaining Offset_i values. Store in participant_clocks.clock_offset_us and log all 4 measurements to clock_offset_measurements. Sync Pulse and Drift Correction Workflow: SyncPulseScheduler emits sync_pulse every pulse_interval_ms with {reference_clock_us: current_reference_clock, seq: pulse_sequence_number}. Each client reports heartbeat with {playback_position_us, client_clock_us}. Server computes adjusted_position = playback_position_us + participant_clocks.clock_offset_us. expected_position = reference_clock_us elapsed since session start. drift_us = adjusted_position - expected_position. If |drift_us| > tolerance_band_us: server sends drift_correction {target_position_us, correction_delta_us}. Logs drift_correction_event to PostgreSQL and to session_replays event array.
target_output.data_model: watch_sessions (id UUID PK, session_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, pulse_interval_ms INT DEFAULT 500, tolerance_band_us INT DEFAULT 100000, reference_clock_start_us BIGINT, status ENUM active/completed, created_by VARCHAR, created_at, completed_at). participant_clocks (id UUID PK, session_id FK, participant_token VARCHAR, clock_offset_us BIGINT, last_rtt_us INT, offset_computed_at, last_heartbeat_at). clock_offset_measurements (id UUID PK, session_id FK, participant_token VARCHAR, round_number SMALLINT, t1_us BIGINT, t2_us BIGINT, t3_us BIGINT, t4_us BIGINT, rtt_us INT, offset_us BIGINT, outlier_rejected BOOLEAN, recorded_at). sync_pulses (id UUID PK, session_id FK, seq_number BIGINT, reference_clock_us BIGINT, emitted_at_mono_us BIGINT). drift_correction_events (id UUID PK, session_id FK, participant_token VARCHAR, seq BIGINT, measured_position_us BIGINT, expected_position_us BIGINT, correction_delta_us BIGINT, tolerance_band_us INT, ts_mono_us BIGINT). network_latency_samples (id UUID PK, session_id FK, participant_token VARCHAR, rtt_us INT, sampled_at). session_replays (id UUID PK, session_id FK UNIQUE, events JSONB NOT NULL, event_count INT, created_at). The events JSONB array schema: each element is {seq: INT, ts_mono_us: BIGINT, type: VARCHAR, payload: OBJECT}. Payload schemas by type: sync_pulse: {reference_clock_us: BIGINT, seq: INT}; heartbeat: {participant_token: STRING, playback_position_us: BIGINT, client_clock_us: BIGINT}; drift_correction: {participant_token: STRING, measured_position_us: BIGINT, expected_position_us: BIGINT, correction_delta_us: BIGINT}; offset_measurement: {participant_token: STRING, round: INT, rtt_us: INT, offset_us: BIGINT, outlier_rejected: BOOL}; participant_join: {participant_token: STRING}; participant_leave: {participant_token: STRING, reason: STRING}.
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1: POST /sessions (body: {pulse_interval_ms, tolerance_band_us}) creates session, returns {session_id, session_code, jwt}. GET /sessions/:id returns session metadata and participant count. POST /sessions/:id/join (body: {display_name}) returns {jwt, session_config}. DELETE /sessions/:id terminates session. GET /sessions/:id/replay returns session_replays.events JSONB array for client-side replay. GET /sessions/:id/stats returns aggregate drift statistics per participant. GET /metrics returns Prometheus scrape text. WebSocket at /ws/:session_id with JWT in Sec-WebSocket-Protocol; bidirectional JSON message protocol with type discriminator field.
target_output.security_and_compliance: RS256 JWT with 4-hour expiry; RSA key pair persisted to /etc/cowatchd/jwt_key.pem with 0600 permissions on first startup to survive restarts without session invalidation. JWT validated on WebSocket upgrade and re-validated on clock_offset_request and heartbeat message types. TLS 1.3 enforced via Let's Encrypt certificate on the Axum TLS listener; WebSocket connections over WSS only. No participant personal data stored; participant_token is a server-generated UUID unlinked to any external identity. Research prototype authentication is intentionally minimal; not suitable for production deployment without additional controls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Hetzner AX41-NVMe bare-metal VM with Ubuntu 24.04 LTS. Rust binary cross-compiled to x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu by GitHub Actions; rsync to VM via SSH deploy key; systemd service cowatchd.service with SIGTERM handler that completes active sync pulse cycles before exit (SO_REUSEPORT allows new process to bind before old process finishes draining). PostgreSQL 16 managed by systemd. Prometheus node_exporter and process_exporter running as system services. Grafana serving on port 3000 with Let's Encrypt certificate. GitHub Actions deploy job: cargo test, cargo clippy warnings-as-errors, cross-compile, rsync, systemctl restart with 30-second health check on /api/v1/health.
target_output.observability: Prometheus custom metrics from Rust cowatchd binary: sync_pulse_jitter_us histogram (bucket boundaries at 1ms, 5ms, 10ms, 25ms, 50ms, 100ms), drift_correction_total counter labeled by session_id, heartbeat_rtt_us histogram per session, active_participants gauge, clock_offset_exchange_duration_us histogram per participant. Grafana real-time dashboard: sync pulse jitter over time, per-session active drift correction rate, network latency sample distribution per participant. Post-session replay analysis dashboard: per-participant drift timeline reconstructed from session_replays events JSONB, overlay of sync pulse schedule, drift correction event markers, clock offset measurement scatter plot with outlier annotations.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hetzner AX41-NVMe bare-metal at approximately 55 EUR per month. Let's Encrypt certificate free. No cloud egress costs. PostgreSQL local instance at negligible cost. Total infrastructure cost approximately 55 EUR per month for the research prototype. Scaling is not a concern; prototype supports up to 50 concurrent participants per session based on tokio async task capacity on the 8-core bare-metal VM.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Rust Axum WebSocket server, 4-round clock offset exchange with median filter, sync pulse broadcast with configurable interval, drift detection and correction command dispatch, session_replays JSONB append-only event log with defined schema, PostgreSQL session telemetry, React 18 browser test client with SyncMonitor and SessionSetup pages, Prometheus metrics endpoint. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Grafana post-session replay analysis dashboard, network condition emulation integration (tc netem for simulated packet loss and latency), A/B experiment framework for comparing synchronization algorithm variants via configurable session parameters, session statistics REST endpoint. Phase 3 (4 weeks): WebRTC data channel alternative transport evaluation as a comparison condition, adaptive tolerance band that tightens based on measured session jitter variance, multi-server topology for geographic distribution testing.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust code must pass cargo clippy with warnings-as-errors in CI. All timestamp handling must use std::time::Instant for monotonic measurements; NEVER use std::time::SystemTime for protocol timing. Clock offset computation isolated in clock_sync module with unit tests validating median filter outlier rejection with synthetic RTT samples. PostgreSQL queries must use sqlx prepared statements; no raw string interpolation. React test client WebSocket message handling isolated in Web Worker to prevent main thread jitter contaminating playback position measurements.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Hetzner bare-metal has no automatic failover; hardware failure ends all active research sessions. Accepted for research prototype; session data in PostgreSQL survives restart. Risk: tokio async scheduler introduces sub-millisecond jitter into sync pulse timing on a loaded system; MissedTickBehavior::Skip prevents cumulative delay but individual pulses may vary by 0.5-2ms on a fully loaded VM. Mitigation: Prometheus sync_pulse_jitter_us histogram documents this variability for research calibration. Tradeoff: 4-round offset exchange adds 4 x RTT latency to session join time. At 50ms RTT this is 200ms; acceptable for research sessions that may last hours. Increasing round count to 8 for tighter estimates is configurable via session parameter.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed median filter over mean for offset outlier rejection because a single high-latency outlier round disproportionately distorts the mean; median is robust to single outliers in a 4-sample set. Confirmed rs256 JWT key persistence to disk over ephemeral in-process keys to avoid invalidating session tokens on hot-deploy. Confirmed tokio::time::interval with MissedTickBehavior::Skip over Sleep loop to prevent sync pulse drift accumulation during computation spikes.

metadata.notes: Strong round 2 revision for co-watching sync research prototype. Both ISS-001 (multi-round offset exchange with median filter) and ISS-002 (session replay JSONB schema) fully resolved with precise protocol specifications.
